The present-key remove tombstones the bucket, but karac_map_free_with_drop_vec only walks OCCUPIED slots, so the tombstoned {ptr,len,cap} String/Vec key buffer is orphaned at map drop. Distinct from the INCOMING-key no-adopt class (B-2026-06-20-9, c7b72bd4) which handled the key ARGUMENT; this is the key/value already STORED in the bucket. Needs a runtime drop-flag ABI (the runtime cannot tell a heap key from a scalar — only codegen knows) + an archive rebuild, not a codegen-only change. Completes the map-key-ownership class B-2026-06-20-9 started. Linux-LSan-only (macOS ASAN misses reachable leaks).

Two codegen gaps surfaced by the bespoke word-frequency kata's `keys().sort()` ordered-report idiom over a `Map[String,_]`. (1) `Vec[String].sort()` was INTEGER-ONLY in codegen (errored 'Vec.sort() supports only integer element types; use sort_by(...)'); only the bare default-order `sort()` for ints had a comparator thunk. Fix (vec_method.rs): a new `emit_default_sort_thunk_string` builds the (ctx,*a,*b)->i64 comparator that loads each `{ptr,len,cap}` String header and calls the existing `karac_string_cmp` byte-lexicographic runtime fn (the same comparator `Vec.binary_search` and the String-key `sort_by` path already use); the `sort` arm picks it when `vec_elem_type_name(var) == "String"` (a Vec[T] element can't reach it — Vec is not Ord, so the typechecker only admits `.sort()` on String among heap `{ptr,len,cap}` shapes). Integer sort unchanged. (2) `Map.keys()/values()/entries()` SHALLOW-copied heap key/value halves into the result Vec, which is drop-tracked as OWNING its elements — so the result Vec's scope-exit drop and the map's own drop freed the same `{ptr,len,cap}` buffer (double-free). It crashed `Map[String,_].keys()` outright (SIGTRAP before any read; buffered stdout lost). Fix (maps.rs compile_map_keys_values_entries): deep-clone each heap half into the result buffer via `emit_clone_fn_for_type_expr` (the same owned-copy contract as `Map.get_or` — deep for String/Vec/struct, memcpy for scalar, pointer-copy for shared), through a new `kvg_emit_half` helper; the key TypeExpr comes from `map_key_type_exprs`, the value from `var_elem_type_exprs` (both populated for annotated maps). Falls back to the prior shallow load+store when a half's TypeExpr side-table entry is absent (inferred maps with no recorded K/V TypeExpr) — correct for scalars, regression-free otherwise. Interpreter was already correct (GC-by-Value-clone); A/B byte-identical. Tests: codegen E2E (vec string sort; keys-sorted report exercising BOTH fixes; values/entries owned-copy content) + memory_sanitizer (keys/values/entries deep-copy no double-free, macOS ASAN + Linux LSan). clippy --all --all-targets --features llvm + fmt clean. NO new runtime symbol (karac_string_cmp / karac_vec_sort_by / karac_clone_* all pre-exist), NO archive rebuild. RELATED OPEN (kata still blocked on it, tracked separately): the flagship `for w in words { *m.entry(w).or_insert(0) += 1 }` DOUBLE-FREES when the key is a MOVED FOR-LOOP ELEMENT — `push(w)` from a loop works, `entry(w)` does not, despite the entry chain running the identical consume-site suppress as push (B-2026-06-20-9). Distinct from the local-binding entry case (which passes). The kata uses `entry(w.to_string())` until that lands. Also a minor type-inference follow-up: an un-annotated `let keys = m.keys()` doesn't propagate the String element type to the `.sort()` gate (annotate `let keys: Vec[String]` meanwhile).

fix: efeb9dbf. Completes the Set side of the map/set key-ownership class: B-2026-06-20-9 (c7b72bd4) fixed the INCOMING key for Map's no-adopt paths but never applied it to Set (collections.rs had ZERO free_fresh_owned_str_arg calls), and B-2026-06-20-10 (a1b59c5e) fixed only the Set STORED element on a present-key remove. This handles the remaining gap — the INCOMING element ARGUMENT on Set's no-adopt paths. Set lowers to Map[T, ()], so the three element-taking arms in src/codegen/collections.rs call karac_map_remove_old / karac_map_contains / karac_map_insert_old; each leaked the incoming element buffer (Linux-LSan-only; macOS ASAN misses reachable leaks). (1) Set.remove and (2) Set.contains are lookup-only and never retain the incoming element — added free_fresh_owned_str_arg(&args[0].value, elem_val) after the runtime call (mirrors Map.remove / Map.contains_key from B-2026-06-20-9; no-ops on a moved binding via its own un-suppressed source free, on a borrowed cap==0 view, and on a non-Vec-String element). Set.remove's incoming free is a DISTINCT buffer from the STORED element freed by the existing drop_key flag (B-2026-06-20-10): the present-key remove test leaks exactly ONE buffer pre-fix (the incoming), confirming drop_key already frees the stored one. (3) Set.insert lowers to karac_map_insert_old, which ADOPTS the element only on the VACANT insert; on the EXISTS (duplicate) path it keeps the bucket's existing element and never adopts the incoming one, while the insert arm's consume-site dance (suppress_fstr_acc_if_moved_out + maybe_defensive_copy_param_arg + suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg) already either suppressed a moved source's scope-exit free or made a private defensive copy of an owned-param element — so the incoming {ptr,len,cap} buffer is orphaned. Fix: free it on the EXISTS branch ONLY (mirror Map.insert's exists-path free), via a conditional-branch diamond guarded by the `existed` bool calling free_str_vec_buffer_if_heap(elem_val); on the vacant branch the bucket adopted the buffer, so a free there would double-free. A vec-struct compile-time gate (llvm_ty_is_vec_struct(elem_ty)) restricts the diamond to heap (Set[String]/Set[Vec[T]]) elements; the cap>0 runtime guard inside free_str_vec_buffer_if_heap no-ops on a borrowed view / rodata literal. Audited ALL Set.* arms: len/is_empty/clear take no element; union/intersection/difference take another SET HANDLE (borrowed, not an owned element buffer) — only insert/contains/remove take an owned element. Reuses the existing free_fresh_owned_str_arg / free_str_vec_buffer_if_heap helpers (runtime.rs) — no duplicated cap>0 {ptr,len,cap} free logic. NO runtime/ABI change, NO archive rebuild, NO interpreter change (interpreter is GC-by-Value-clone; A/B output byte-identical). TESTS: 4 LSan regression tests in tests/memory_sanitizer.rs (>=36-byte elements per the LSan-reachability rule, since LSan misses short still-reachable String/Vec buffers) — asan_set_remove_present_fresh_temp_element_no_leak (Set[String].remove(present): STORED element freed by drop_key + INCOMING fresh temp freed by this fix, two distinct buffers), asan_set_contains_present_fresh_temp_element_no_leak (Set[String].contains(present) fresh temp), asan_set_insert_moved_binding_duplicate_element_no_leak (Set[String].insert dup moved binding -> exists-branch free), asan_set_remove_absent_fresh_temp_vec_element_no_leak (Set[Vec[i64]].remove(absent) fresh-temp Vec from a helper fn, >=48-byte data buffer; uses an EXPLICIT MISS because Set[Vec] does not dedupe equal-contents vecs — a pre-existing Set/Vec hash-eq gap, out of scope — so the lookup-only incoming free is isolated without depending on content equality). NEGATIVE CONTROL: stashing only collections.rs (fix reverted, tests kept) makes all 4 new tests FAIL under the Linux LSan gate with EXACTLY ONE leaked allocation each (39 / 40 / 64 / 39 bytes = the incoming element buffer; the present-remove test's single 39-byte leak confirms the stored element is NOT leaked); restoring the fix makes all 4 pass. GATES (worktree): fmt clean; clippy --all --all-targets --features llvm -D warnings clean; macOS codegen 1707; macOS memory_sanitizer 325 passed / 1 ignored (incl. the 4 new); Linux LSan gate `set` filter 15 passed / 0 failed (incl. the 4 new). The 3 PRE-EXISTING non-map/non-set LSan leaks documented in B-2026-06-20-9/-10 (asan_owned_struct_option_shared_field_captured_from_builder_no_uaf, asan_single_field_struct_option_payload_sizing_no_bad_access, asan_string_eq_mismatched_len_no_overread_in_indexed_payload_match) are unaffected (not in the `set` filter; macOS ASAN does not surface them). Completes the map/set key-ownership class B-2026-06-20-9 / B-2026-06-20-10 started.
title: Set INCOMING-element NO-ADOPT leak: Set.remove(x) / Set.contains(x) / Set.insert(x) of a HEAP element (Set[String], Set[Vec[T]]) leaked the incoming element buffer when the argument is a fresh-owned temp (s.remove("x".to_string())) or a moved binding on a no-adopt path. The B-2026-06-20-9 incoming-key class fixed for Map was never applied to Set (collections.rs had zero free_fresh_owned_str_arg calls); B-2026-06-20-10 fixed only the Set STORED element on a present-key remove. Codegen-only (Set lowers to Map[T,()], arms call karac_map_remove_old / karac_map_contains / karac_map_insert_old); no runtime/ABI change, no archive rebuild. Linux-LSan-only (macOS ASAN misses reachable leaks). Completes the map/set key-ownership class B-2026-06-20-9 / B-2026-06-20-10 started.

Heap `for`-loop element BORROW consumed by a retaining sink double-freed in codegen (A/B mismatch on the flagship counter idiom). `for w in vec` over a Vec is BORROW-iteration: the loop binds `w` to an ALIAS of `data[i]` (a plain header load, compile_for_vec_var) and the source Vec RETAINS ownership — it is usable after the loop (`for w in words {..}; words.len()` works, prints the full count). So a consume site that RETAINS `w` — `m.entry(w)`, `v.push(w)`, `m.insert(w, ..)` — bit-copied `w`'s `{ptr,len,cap}` into the sink, and BOTH the sink's drop and the source Vec's scope-exit drop freed the same buffer (double-free; SIGTRAP, buffered stdout lost). The interpreter clones values freely (GC-by-Value-clone), so it produced the right answer — this was a silent A/B divergence on the canonical histogram `for w in words { *m.entry(w).or_insert(0) += 1 }` (codegen crashed; interp printed the counts). Earlier mis-diagnosed as entry-specific (B-2026-06-20-11 RELATED note) because the `push(w)` control used STRING LITERAL elements (cap==0 rodata — no heap to double-free); with `.to_string()` heap elements, push/insert double-free identically. So it is general to all retaining-consume sites, not entry. FIX (codegen, no runtime change): a new `for_loop_borrow_vars` set marks heap (String/Vec) for-loop element bindings — `register_for_loop_bindings` adds a Binding (and the k/v of `for (k,v) in m`) when the element landed in `vec_elem_types` (the {ptr,len,cap} shapes) via the new `mark_for_loop_borrow_if_heap`. `maybe_defensive_copy_param_arg` treats membership the SAME as `owned_vecstr_params`: it deep-copies the borrowed element (`emit_vecstr_defensive_copy`) so the sink owns a private buffer while the source Vec keeps freeing its own elements — exactly the owned-param model, one level in (the source retains the scope-exit free in both). The consume sites (Vec.push/push_back, Map.insert key+value, the entry-chain key from B-2026-06-20-9) already call that helper, so they are fixed by the gate change alone; read-only loop bodies never call it, so they are untouched (no extra clone). A later `let` of the same name clears stale membership in the binding-registration block (shadow guard) so a fresh owned binding reusing a loop var name is not wrongly copied (the copy pairs with a source-suppress → would orphan the fresh String; LSan-only). Cleared per-function alongside `owned_vecstr_params`; NOT mono-saved (parity with owned_vecstr_params, which also relies on the per-fn clear). A/B verified byte-identical: the canonical word-frequency kata (histogram via `for w in words { *freq.entry(w)... }`, ascending report) now matches under `karac run` and `karac build`. Tests: 3 LSan regression tests in tests/memory_sanitizer.rs (entry counter with repeated keys + words-live-after; push+insert; shadow-rebind no-leak), macOS ASAN + Linux LSan green; clippy --all --all-targets --features llvm + fmt clean. RELATED MINOR OPEN (type-inference, not memory): an un-annotated `let keys = m.keys()` does not propagate the String element type to the `Vec.sort()` codegen gate (annotate `let keys: Vec[String]` meanwhile) — the kata annotates the binding. Separate from this ownership fix.

Three PRE-EXISTING leaks the Linux-LSan gate (scripts/lsan-local.sh) flags but the macOS post-landing ASAN run misses (Apple clang has no LeakSanitizer), surfaced by the phase-12 self-host render slices and confirmed failing on clean main. NOT introduced by the B-2026-06-20-9 map-key fix (all three are non-map). (1) SHARED-ENUM String/Vec payload MOVE-OUT double-free (suppress_shared_enum_payload_move_out, control_flow_match.rs). `match e { S(s) => s }` over `shared enum E { S(String) }` (the render_expr/get shape, returned) only zeroed the LOCAL binding's cap on move-out via the tail-move logic -- the RC box's payload words still pointed at the moved-out buffer, so the box's `__karac_rc_drop_<E>` (whose Vec/String arm frees cap>0) re-freed it after the caller already freed the returned String: a double-free (macOS ASAN catches it; the nested tests-1/2 form only LEAKED because the boxed-struct-drop gap below masked it -- the inner box's drop never ran). Fix: the shared sibling of the existing suppress_destructured_enum_payload_cleanup_at, which explicitly bails on layout.is_shared. For each consumed EnumDropKind::VecOrString field, zero its payload words IN THE BOX (heap index start_word+2, mirroring emit_shared_enum_rc_drop_fn) so the box rc-drop skips the buffer the binding now owns. Gated to owned (non-borrow) shared scrutinees; shared-handle (rc-managed) and NestedStruct payloads are untouched. (2) SHARED-ENUM BOXED-struct payload rc-drop gap (emit_shared_enum_field_drop boxed arm + emit_nested_struct_shared_rc_decs_ex, synth_drop.rs). A struct payload wider than its allotted payload-area words is heap-boxed at construction (coerce_to_payload_words, `out.len() > num_words`: e.g. `struct Block { tail: Option[Expr] }` used as `Expr.Blk(Block)` -- the Option field hits the enum-in-enum carve-out, undersizing the area while Block is 4 words), but the rc-drop's struct arm ALWAYS read the payload INLINE -- GEP'ing the box-POINTER word as the struct's first field (a garbage tag, so the recursion skipped) and never freeing the box. It leaked the enumbox container PLUS every heap child reachable through it (the Block's inner Option[shared Expr] box -- tests #48 88+80B and #49 32+40B). Fix: recompute the SAME `llvm_type_word_count(struct) > num_words` predicate the pack/unpack sites use, and on boxed: inttoptr word 0 -> heap struct, walk it, then free the box. The walk passes nested_buffer_free=Some(false) (a new param replacing the old recurse bool): the boxed struct's DIRECT Vec/String buffers + shared/Option[shared] children are reclaimed, but a NESTED heap struct FIELD (`IfNode.then_block: Block`) is recursed to rc-dec ONLY its RC children -- because that nested struct may be MOVED OUT by the match binding (`let tb = nd.then_block`), whose own __karac_drop_struct_<S> value-drop frees its BUFFERS but does NOT rc-dec its shared children. Re-freeing those buffers through the box walk was a double-free (regressed tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_shared_enum_payload_with_nested_heap_struct_field -> empty output: a SIGSEGV at the scope-exit drop lost the buffered stdout); skipping the children was a leak. Some(false) splits them: buffers to the move-out owner, RC children to the box -- no double-free, no stranded child. RESIDUAL BOUNDED LEAK (documented v1, never corruption): a NEVER-moved-out nested heap struct's own Vec/String buffer inside a boxed payload is not reclaimed (the box walk leaves nested buffers to a move-out owner that, in that case, does not exist); none of the suite hits this. (3) METHOD-CALL fresh String/Vec temp arg leak (compile_method_call, method_call.rs). A fresh String/Vec produced by a Call/MethodCall and passed DIRECTLY by value to a USER method (`lx.ident_matches("Fn".to_string())`) had no consuming binding, and an owned by-value Vec/String param is NOT freed by the callee (it lands in owned_vecstr_params for retaining-consume deep-copy, never a callee-side track_vec_var) -- so the temp orphaned, leaking one buffer per call. The free-fn compile_call arg loop already materializes such args into a caller-scope __owned_tmp with a scope-exit free (the #20 is_fresh_heap_call_arg arm + the bare-shared __owned_arg_tmp arm); the method arg loop was missing it. Fix: mirror both arms verbatim after track_inline_owned_aggregate_arg, with the same guards (expr_yields_fresh_owned_temp / inline-temp-vec-index, llvm_ty_is_vec_struct, !rhs_stages_fstr_acc). Built-in consuming methods (push/insert/entry.or_insert) are special-cased BEFORE this generic user-method dispatch, so they are unaffected (verified: or_insert(Vec.new()) emits no __owned_tmp). Tests: tests/codegen.rs +3 IR-shape (shared_enum_string_payload_moveout_zeros_box_cap; shared_enum_boxed_struct_payload_rc_drop_unboxes_and_frees; method_call_fresh_string_temp_arg_materialized); tests/memory_sanitizer.rs +2 E2E (asan_shared_enum_string_payload_moveout_no_double_free -- the minimal move-out double-free that no prior test isolated, all existing shared-enum tests extract only scalar/shared-handle payloads; asan_shared_enum_boxed_struct_payload_moveout_no_double_free -- the boxed-struct nested-move-out, LSan-clean). The 3 target tests (asan_owned_struct_option_shared_field_captured_from_builder_no_uaf, asan_single_field_struct_option_payload_sizing_no_bad_access, asan_string_eq_mismatched_len_no_overread_in_indexed_payload_match) now LSan-green. Suites: codegen 1703, memory_sanitizer 319, par_codegen 122 (macOS ASAN); full scripts/lsan-local.sh green except the pre-existing flake below; fmt + clippy --all --all-targets --features llvm clean. NO runtime change, NO archive rebuild, NO interpreter change. KNOWN OPEN (PRE-EXISTING, distinct, NOT this change): asan_match_arm_vec_binding_freed_on_arm_exit SEGVs INTERMITTENTLY under Linux LSan (__aarch64_cas1_acq_rel, addr 0xff..f1) -- a seed-dependent (codegen HashMap-order) heap corruption in `Map.remove` of a heap VALUE (Vec[i64]) moved out into a match binding, the VALUE side of the map-remove ownership class (the stored-KEY side was fixed in B-2026-06-20-10/a1b59c5e, which deliberately never frees the moved-out value). Confirmed reproducing on CLEAN primary main under LSan when run ALONE; passes on most seeds, including the full post-rebase gate run that landed this change (330 passed / 0 failed). The map codegen is untouched by this change; a deterministic fix is a separate follow-up.

fix: ddc625ad. TWO Vec-specific root causes, both fixed. (1) TYPECHECKER (src/typechecker/derives.rs): the built-in `Vec` is registered in env.structs with NO derived traits, so `type_supports_hash`/`type_supports_eq` fell through to the generic `Type::Named` lookup and reported `Vec` as un-Hash/un-Eq -- a HARD ERROR in the codegen (`karac build`) path (so `Set[Vec[T]]` did not even compile) but only a non-fatal WARNING in the interpreter (`karac run`) path (interpreter is GC-by-Value-clone and dedupes via structural `Value::Array` eq, so it printed the right answer). Fix: a dedicated `Type::Named { name: "Vec", args } if args.len()==1` arm in BOTH predicates, placed BEFORE the generic Named struct/enum lookup, that recurses into the element (`Vec[T]` is Hash/Eq by content iff `T` is) -- mirrors the existing `Type::Array`/`Type::Slice`/`Type::Vector` recursion and `type_supports_display`'s explicit `"Vec"` arm. `Set[Vec[f64]]` stays correctly REJECTED (f64 is not Eq -- IEEE-754 NaN!=NaN). (2) CODEGEN (src/codegen/synth.rs): even past that gate, a `Vec[T]` element/key routed through the `emit_hash_fn_for_type`/`emit_eq_fn_for_type` byte-loop FALLBACK, which hashes/compares the {ptr,len,cap} HEADER (pointer identity), so two equal-contents vecs land in different buckets and never collapse. (String elements always deduped via the `type_name=="String"` byte-walk arm, masking the gap.) Fix: a `Vec` dispatch arm in `emit_hash_fn_for_type_expr`/`emit_eq_fn_for_type_expr` (before the user-struct arm) that, for a `TypeKind::Path` head "Vec" with a `GenericArg::Type` arg, calls the new `emit_hash_fn_for_vec`/`emit_eq_fn_for_vec`; absent the arg it falls back to the header byte-loop. `emit_hash_fn_for_vec` emits `karac_hash_Vec_<elem>(*const Vec)->i64`: loads {data,len} from the {ptr,len,cap} header, seeds the FxHash state with `len` (length is part of the digest, matching Rust's `Hash for [T]`; rotate_left(0,5)=0 collapses mix(0,len) to len*SEED), then folds each per-element hash (`emit_hash_fn_for_type_expr(elem)`, recurses for Vec[String]/Vec[Vec[_]]) via the same rotate-5/xor/mul tail-mix the tuple/struct combiners use. `emit_eq_fn_for_vec` emits `karac_eq_Vec_<elem>(*const Vec,*const Vec)->i1`: compare lengths, then each element via the per-element eq fn, short-circuit false on first mismatch. CRITICAL naming: keyed on the element-aware `karac_hash_Vec_<elem>`/`karac_eq_Vec_<elem>` (via `display_mangle_te`) NOT the shallow `mangled_type_name` "Vec" -- otherwise distinct element types would share one now-content-dependent body; the dispatcher's early cache check (keyed on shallow "Vec") simply misses for Vec and the real dedup happens inside the new fns. Empty vecs: len 0 -> no element loop, hash 0, eq true (data ptr loaded but never dereferenced -> no NULL deref). `cap` is NOT read (a Vec's value identity is len+contents, not capacity). NO runtime/ABI change, NO archive rebuild, NO interpreter change (A/B byte-identical). Fixes `Map[Vec[T], _]` keys and Vec-field-bearing struct/tuple keys for free (all route through the same dispatchers). Vec-ONLY on BOTH sides (not VecDeque): the codegen content path is keyed on a Path head "Vec", so admitting VecDeque in the typechecker without a matching codegen path would re-create the A/B divergence one layer down -- keeping both sides Vec-only stays in lockstep. The fix ALSO opens a previously-UNREACHABLE ownership path: once two equal-contents vecs collapse, the second insert takes `karac_map_insert_old`'s EXISTS branch, whose incoming-element free (B-2026-06-20-12) now actually fires -- covered by a new LSan regression. TESTS: 3 codegen E2E (tests/codegen.rs: test_e2e_set_vec_dedup_by_content [dedup + distinct-stay-separate + contains/remove + same-prefix-diff-length non-equality], test_e2e_set_string_dedup_companion, test_e2e_map_vec_key_dedup_by_content); 2 typechecker (tests/typechecker.rs: test_set_and_map_accept_vec_element_when_inner_hash_eq, test_set_rejects_vec_element_when_inner_not_hash_eq); 1 LSan (tests/memory_sanitizer.rs: asan_set_vec_duplicate_element_dedup_no_leak_no_double_free, >=48-byte buffer per the LSan-reachability rule). Stale-comment fixup on asan_set_remove_absent_fresh_temp_vec_element_no_leak (its "Set[Vec] does not dedupe" parenthetical). GATES (worktree): fmt clean; clippy --all --all-targets --features llvm -D warnings clean; typechecker 1741; codegen 1710 (incl. 3 new); macOS memory_sanitizer 329 passed/1 ignored (incl. new); Linux LSan gate `set` filter 16 passed/0 failed (incl. new dedup test). Determinism verified across 6 codegen rebuilds (no HashMap-order nondeterminism). Closes the SEPARATE Set/Vec hash-eq gap noted while writing B-2026-06-20-12 (efeb9dbf).
title: Set[Vec[T]] (and Map[Vec[T], _]) did not deduplicate equal-CONTENTS vecs -- two equal vecs inserted as distinct elements (len()==2 instead of 1), an A/B divergence vs the interpreter (which dedupes via structural Value::Array eq). A `Set` has HashSet value semantics, so this is a correctness bug. Two Vec-specific causes: (1) the built-in `Vec` is registered with no derived traits so type_supports_hash/eq reported it un-Hash/un-Eq -- a hard ERROR in the codegen path (Set[Vec[T]] never compiled) but only a warning in the interpreter path; (2) past that gate, codegen's Vec element hash/eq used the byte-loop fallback over the {ptr,len,cap} HEADER (pointer identity) instead of walking the contents. String elements always deduped (byte-walk arm), masking the gap. Discovered while writing the B-2026-06-20-12 Set incoming-element leak tests (efeb9dbf); the leak fix is unrelated and already landed -- this is the separate functional dedup gap flagged there.

Auto-par (statement-level) RACED a map-mutating loop against a later read of the same map under the DEFAULT `karac build` — a silent wrong-answer/crash. The word-frequency kata's `for w in words { *freq.entry(w).or_insert(0) += 1 }` histogram loop and the subsequent `freq.keys()` were placed in the SAME parallel_group (`--concurrency-report`: "no data or effect dependencies"), so `freq.keys()` ran concurrently while the loop was still mutating `freq` — a read-after-write race on the map (mutex panics, garbled output). `karac run` (interpreter) and `KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0 karac build` were both correct, so it was a silent A/B mismatch on the flagship counter idiom. ROOT CAUSE (src/concurrency.rs): the dual-analysis dependency graph builds per-statement reads/writes sets. `collect_assign_target_defines` (the write-target place-root resolver) matched only `Identifier` / `SelfValue` / `FieldAccess` / `Index` — it had NO arm for a `Unary{Deref}` or a `MethodCall` target, so a `*<place> = …` / `*<place> += …` whose place is a method chain recorded NO write. The CompoundAssign `*freq.entry(w).or_insert(0) += 1` therefore contributed zero writes; the `for`-loop's writes set (collected from the body via collect_block_inner_writes -> collect_assign_target_defines) omitted `freq`; the loop statement looked write-free; and the parallelizer saw no write-vs-read conflict with `freq.keys()` -> grouped -> raced. FIX: `collect_assign_target_defines` now recurses a `Unary{op: Deref}` target into its operand and a `MethodCall` place target into its receiver, so a write through a deref/method-chain roots at the underlying container — `*m.entry(k).or_insert(d) += 1` records writes(m), `*v.get_mut(i) = x` records writes(v). For a plain `*ref += …` on a mut-ref local it records the binding (the pointee's owner), which is conservative — it may over-serialize but NEVER under-serializes, the only safe direction for the parallel-safety gate. Reads were already captured (collect_block_reads routes CompoundAssign/Assign targets through the general collect_expr_reads, which recurses MethodCall/Unary). After the fix the racy loop+keys() parallel_group no longer forms (`--concurrency-report` shows only the two genuinely-independent groups), and the DEFAULT auto-par build of the word-frequency kata is A/B byte-identical to `karac run`. Tests: tests/concurrency.rs unit (a map-mutating loop is not co-grouped with a later `m.keys()` read) + tests/par_codegen.rs E2E (auto-par histogram-then-keys, deterministic sorted output — exercises the whole word-frequency cascade: moved-loop-element copy B-2026-06-20-13, keys deep-copy + String sort B-2026-06-20-11, and this serialization). Full concurrency unit suite 100, clippy --all --all-targets --features llvm + fmt clean. NO runtime change, NO archive rebuild. FOURTH and final gap surfaced by the bespoke word-frequency kata (after B-2026-06-20-9 entry/get/insert no-adopt, -11 sort+keys deep-copy, -13 moved-loop-element copy); the kata now runs A/B-clean under run + default build + KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0.

Follow-up on the KNOWN-OPEN value-side `Map.remove` SEGV that B-2026-06-20-14 logged for asan_match_arm_vec_binding_freed_on_arm_exit (intermittent __aarch64_cas1_acq_rel crash at addr 0xff..f1 under Linux LSan, attributed to a seed-flavoured heap corruption in `Map.remove` of a heap VALUE moved into a match binding). CONCLUSION: does NOT reproduce on current main (886c2a2f); no latent codegen bug found; closed with permanent regression coverage rather than a code change. Evidence: (1) the emitted IR for the exact `match bucket.remove(k) { Some(indices) => indices.len() }` over `Map[i64,Vec[i64]]` is single-owner and provably correct -- `karac_map_remove_old(.., drop_key=0)` bitwise-moves the value out into the `Some` payload words and tombstones the slot WITHOUT freeing it; the arm reconstructs `{ptr,len,cap}` into `indices` and frees its data buffer EXACTLY ONCE under a `cap>0` guard; the scope-exit `karac_map_free_with_drop_vec(handle, key=0, val=1)` walks only OCCUPIED slots, all of which are tombstoned by then -> no double-free, no leak, no UAF. (2) The path has NO HashMap-order-dependent decision (drop_key and the val-is-vec free flag are pure type predicates; the per-arm cleanup frame is an ordered Vec with a single FreeVecBuffer; no shared types are involved, so the shared-heap-type-collision nondeterminism class does not apply) -- the single-function IR is therefore structurally seed-invariant, so the dumped IR is representative of every seed. (3) ~2680 REAL Linux arm64 ASAN+LSan runs across fresh per-process HashMap seeds (the original test plus three new churn stress variants), 0 failures, with a verified positive control (a deliberate 12345-byte C leak IS reported by the same container's LSan, and karac emits direct malloc/free so ASAN tracks the kara heap). ROOT-CAUSE HYPOTHESIS for the original transient symptom: a stale-archive ABI mismatch during the B-2026-06-20-10/a1b59c5e `drop_key` rollout -- codegen emitting the 4-arg `karac_map_remove_old` call against a not-yet-rebuilt 3-arg archive in the SHARED kara-lsan-target docker volume (the documented stale-karac-after-rebase footgun), so the 4th arg landed in a register the old body sometimes clobbered -> seed/layout-flavoured corruption that vanishes once all archives are rebuilt consistently. The earlier 'reproducing when run ALONE' observation is consistent with a single contaminated build, not a code defect. ADDED (tests/memory_sanitizer.rs, +3 E2E churn guards that exhaust the ASAN quarantine so any real value-side double-free/UAF surfaces as live-chunk corruption, not a benign quarantined catch): asan_map_remove_vec_value_moveout_under_churn (Vec[i64], 200 keys x 20 generations x 20 iters), asan_map_remove_vec_value_indexed_readback (reads every element of the moved-out Vec inside the arm before free), asan_map_remove_string_key_vec_value_both_heap_halves (heap String key drop_key=1 + heap Vec value move-out, both halves freed once). Suites: memory_sanitizer 337 passed/0 failed (macOS ASAN) + full scripts/lsan-local.sh value-path loop green; codegen unchanged-count green; fmt + clippy --all --all-targets --features llvm clean. NO src/ change, NO runtime change, NO archive ABI change.

SoA layout elements with String/Vec heap fields leaked across push/store/cleanup (front-end rejected them entirely). Lifted the resolver gate for String + Vec[POD] element fields (Map/Set/VecDeque/Sorted*/Vec[heap]/shared still rejected) and added per-element heap-field drops across the whole SoA write/drop lifecycle. Synthesized a per-layout __karac_soa_drop_<layout>(*mut SoaStruct) (emit_soa_drop_fn, cached in soa_drop_fns) that loops live elements [0,len) and frees each heap group's String/Vec buffers via emit_aggregate_heap_field_frees (recursing nested tuples/structs); None for a POD layout so that path stays byte-identical (Slipstream native oracle held). The drop fn is carried on CleanupAction::FreeSoaGroups / SlotOwnership::Soa and invoked inside the cap>0 guard BEFORE the group buffers are freed, at scope-exit cleanup, carried-grid reassignment (emit_free_soa_groups_inline), and single-element whole-element/field-store drop-old (emit_soa_drop_element_at). Move-in double-frees suppressed: a named owned struct moved into push / grid[i]= is cap-zeroed (zero_struct_move_caps), and an f-string into grid[i].name= has its accumulator's FreeVecBuffer neutralized. pop/remove unchanged (scatter-read moves the element out; decremented len excludes the vacated slot). Verified on the Linux LSan gate (21/21 SoA tests, ≥36-byte payloads ×20 frames). RESIDUAL: reading a heap SoA field back as a method receiver / index base (grid[i].name.len(), grid[i].data[k]) needs the field's address (the per-field read materialize-then-extracts a non-addressable SSA value — correct only as a direct value like println(grid[i].name)); and whole-element let e=grid[i] aliases the buffer (the same latent hazard AoS Vec[Struct] already carries). The SoA heap-field READ-back is the next slice.
tracker: docs/implementation_checklist/phase-7-codegen.md § SoA drop semantics / Heap-bearing-field SoA elements

title: First-class fn value bound to a LOCAL first miscompiled — sibling of B-2026-06-20-1 (which only closed the direct bare-fn-name argument case). `let f = doubler; apply(f, 21)` (and the `let f: Fn(i64)->i64 = doubler;` annotated form) failed LLVM module verification ('Call parameter type does not match function signature! ... load ptr ... call ... @apply(ptr, i64)'), because the free-fn-name-as-value path (exprs.rs Identifier arm) produced a raw `ptr` and the local was never registered in closure_fn_types — so the local held an 8-byte raw fn ptr while the `Fn(...)` parameter slot is the 16-byte {fn_ptr,env_ptr} fat pointer. WORSE: a direct call through such a local, `let f: Fn(i64)->i64 = doubler; f(21)`, compiled clean but SILENTLY RETURNED 0 (the unregistered name fell through compile_call's unknown-callee branch), a wrong-output miscompile rather than a verifier error. FIXED 2026-06-21 (fcc2c925): in compile_stmt's `let <name> = <bare fn name>` arm, reify the fn name into a {trampoline, null env} closure fat pointer and register the local in closure_fn_types, so both `apply(f, x)` (pass to a Fn-typed param) and `f(x)` (direct call through the local) work for annotated and inferred bindings; the fat pointer's env is null and the trampoline is a module global, so the binding owns no heap (early return, no scope cleanup). Refactor for one trampoline definition per fn: reify_named_fn_value is the shared core (argument-site + let-binding paths), emit_fn_value_trampoline derives its env-first signature from the TARGET fn's own LLVM signature (not a Fn annotation) so the single memoized __karac_fnval_<name> stays consistent, and a side-effect-free name_resolves_to_free_fn mirrors compile_expr's Identifier resolution precedence (const-subst/local/module-let/unit-enum-variant/top-level-const all shadow a free fn) via membership tables only — never try_load_module_binding/try_unit_enum_variant, which emit IR. Typechecker already accepts both forms (verified). Tests: tests/codegen.rs fn_value_let_bound_named_fn_passed_and_called / fn_value_let_and_arg_reify_share_one_trampoline (memoization) / test_ir_inline_always_through_fn_value_compiles (un-defers the codegen-hint-attrs indirect-call IR test that waited on B-2026-06-20-1); leak gate tests/memory_sanitizer.rs asan_let_bound_fn_value_heap_arg_no_leak (heap String through a let-bound fn value freed once — green on the Linux ASAN+LSan harness). RESIDUAL (next slice): returning a `Fn(...)` value (-> Fn(...)), storing one in a struct field / Vec[Fn()], and the `karac run` interpreter path for fn values are still unaddressed.

First-class fn values in return / struct-field / Vec[Fn] positions miscompiled — the remaining non-arg, non-let positions after B-2026-06-20-1/-06-21-1. A `fn` returning a `Fn(...)` value (`fn pick() -> Fn(i64)->i64 { doubler }`) failed verification ('Function return type does not match operand type of return inst! ret ptr @doubler / {ptr,ptr}') because the bare fn name in return-tail position emitted a raw ptr vs the {fn_ptr,env_ptr} return slot; a fn value stored in a struct field (`H { f: doubler }`) or Vec element (`v.push(doubler)`) stored a raw ptr into a fat-pointer slot and the call back through it silently returned 0. FIXED 2026-06-21 (98731b72): make the bare-fn-name-as-value lower to the {trampoline, null env} closure fat pointer at the ONE free-fn-as-value source — the Identifier arm of compile_expr (src/codegen/exprs.rs) — instead of a raw ptr. That single change fixes every value position at once (return tail, struct-field init, Vec element, let RHS); Server.serve resolves its handler name independently (resolve_free_fn_for_handler_arg) so its raw-fn-ptr FFI ABI is untouched (whole codegen suite 1736/0 confirms no regression). Call side: new let_binding_fn_value_type registers a fn-value `let` binding in closure_fn_types so `f(x)` is an indirect call — signature recovered from an explicit `Fn(...)` annotation, a bare free-fn-name RHS, or a call whose callee's declared return type is `Fn(...)` (so `let f = pick()` works un-annotated). env_first_fn_type factors the shared env-first signature build; the compile_stmt let arm routes through let_binding_fn_value_type (replacing the bare-name-only special case from B-2026-06-21-1). Interpreter (`karac run`) ALREADY handled fn values as args/let/return — verified, added regression cover (tests/interpreter.rs fn_value_passed_to_param/let_bound_then_called/returned_then_called) rather than a fix; closes the third 'residual' item from B-2026-06-21-1 as a non-bug. Tests: tests/codegen.rs fn_value_returned_then_called / fn_value_returned_passed_directly / fn_value_struct_field_annotated_extraction / fn_value_vec_element_annotated_extraction; leak gate tests/memory_sanitizer.rs asan_returned_fn_value_heap_arg_no_leak (heap String through a returned fn value freed once — green on Linux ASAN+LSan). RESIDUAL: un-annotated extraction of a fn value from a struct field / Vec element (`let g = h.f;` without `: Fn(..)`) still falls through to the unknown-callee path — needs per-field/per-element fn-type tracking; explicit `let g: Fn(..) = h.f` is the workaround. [CLOSED by B-2026-06-21-3.]

Un-annotated first-class fn-value extraction from a struct field / Vec element miscompiled — the last residual of B-2026-06-21-2. `let g = h.f;` / `let g = v[0];` (no `: Fn(..)` annotation) reading a `Fn(..)`-typed struct field or `Vec` element bound `g` to the correct fat pointer, but `g` was never registered in closure_fn_types because its signature wasn't recoverable from the RHS shape (field-read / index, unlike a bare fn name or a call-with-declared-Fn-return), so `g(x)` fell through compile_call's unknown-callee branch and SILENTLY RETURNED 0. Workaround was an explicit `let g: Fn(..) = h.f`. FIXED 2026-06-21 (7010bc86) with the architecturally-general mechanism (typechecker → lowering side-table → codegen), recovering the signature from the typechecker rather than the RHS shape: (1) typechecker type_to_type_expr (src/typechecker/patterns.rs) now maps Type::Function / Type::OnceFunction to a TypeKind::FnType (was the Error fallback) so a fn value's signature round-trips to a TypeExpr; (2) the lowering pass (src/lowering.rs) populates a new Program.fn_value_typed_exprs span table — for every Fn(..)/OnceFn(..)-typed expression (borrow-unwrapped), its FnType TypeExpr — a sibling of string_typed_exprs/tensor_typed_exprs; (3) codegen let_binding_fn_value_type (src/codegen/closures.rs) consults fn_value_typed_exprs as a general fallback after the annotation / bare-name / call-return cases, so ANY inferred fn-value binding (field read, index, method call, …) registers closure_fn_types and dispatches indirectly. closure_abi_fn_type now maps a unit return to void for ABI consistency with the env-first trampoline. Tests: tests/codegen.rs fn_value_struct_field_unannotated_extraction / fn_value_vec_element_unannotated_extraction. Full typechecker 1741/0, closures 14/0, interpreter 979/0, codegen 1738/0, memory_sanitizer 347/0 (macOS ASAN), clippy + fmt clean. No new heap/drop logic (fn values are heap-free; the heap-arg-forwarding path was LSan-validated in B-2026-06-21-1/-2), so no dedicated LSan run for this slice. First-class named-fn values are now complete across arg / let / return / struct-field / Vec positions, annotated or inferred, in both karac build and karac run.

Map.new()/SortedMap.new() did not back-propagate K/V from insert/get, so an un-annotated map field/binding failed `karac check` though it ran fine. `Map.new()` mints the receiver as `Map[?K, ?V]` (two fresh typevars), but the insert/get/contains_key/get_or/remove arms of infer_map_method (src/typechecker/stdlib_map.rs) only ran `check_assignable(?K, ConcreteK)` — never unifying the argument type into the slot typevar the way Vec.new()+push does. So `let mut m = Map.new(); m.insert("a", 1); let s = S { m: m }` (S.m: Map[String,i64]) reported a spurious `expected '?T0', found 'String'` at the insert AND a `Map<String,i64>` vs `Map<?T0,?T1>` mismatch at the struct literal, because K/V stayed unsolved and the inferred `Map[?K,?V]` then clashed with the annotated field. Annotating `let mut m: Map[String,i64] = Map.new()` was the only workaround — proving it was pure inference. Surfaced building protobuf `map<K,V>` support (runs correctly; only the user-facing map construction tripped check). FIXED 2026-06-22 (387c9346): a new check_map_slot_arg helper unifies the slot type with the inferred argument type (unify_types) BEFORE resolving the slot and running check_assignable — mirroring the Vec.new()+push element-type pinning. Applied to BOTH infer_map_method and infer_sorted_map_method (identical latent bug); get/insert/remove/get_or also resolve V through the substitutions in their return type so a sibling insert that pinned V surfaces it (e.g. `m.get(k)` returns `Option[i64]`, not `Option[?V]`). Pinning does not erase real checks: once the first insert pins V=i64, a later `insert("b", "oops")` is still a clean `expected 'i64', found 'String'` TypeMismatch. Tests: tests/typechecker.rs test_map_new_insert_infers_field_type / test_map_new_get_pins_key_and_resolves_value / test_map_new_insert_conflicting_value_type_rejected / test_sorted_map_new_insert_infers_field_type; codegen E2E tests/codegen.rs e2e_unannotated_map_new_insert_infers_field_type (full inference→codegen→runtime). Full non-llvm suite green, codegen map subset 99/0, clippy + fmt clean.

An ESCAPING capturing closure silently miscompiles (dangling stack environment) — soundness hole surfaced finishing first-class fn values (B-2026-06-20-1..B-2026-06-21-3). A closure that captures a local and then outlives its defining frame — returned (`fn make(k: i64) -> Fn(i64)->i64 { |x| x + k }`), or stored in a struct field / Vec / global that escapes — reads its captures from freed stack memory after the frame returns. Repro: `fn make(k){ |x| x + k }; let f = make(10); println(f(5))` typechecks with 0 errors and `karac build` prints -1 (garbage) instead of 15; it is a WRONG-OUTPUT miscompile, not a verifier error or a diagnostic. Root cause: compile_closure (src/codegen/closures.rs) builds the env as a stack alloca in the OUTER fn frame (`create_entry_alloca(outer_fn, "__closure_env", ..)`) and the fat pointer's env slot points at it; there is NO heap allocation, NO escape analysis, and NO closure-env drop machinery anywhere today (a non-escaping closure is fine because the frame is still live when it's called, which is why all existing closure tests — same-frame `let f = |x| x+base; f(5)` — pass). Non-capturing escaping closures are safe (null env). The interpreter (`karac run`) is unaffected (tree-walk closures own their captured environment). FIX = the heap-closure-environment epic (tracked in phase-7-codegen.md as a sequenced plan): heap-allocate an escaping closure's env, drop POD then heap (String/Vec) captures at the owning value's scope exit, handle a closure value copied to multiple owners (RC env or strict move + move-out suppression), and add escape analysis so non-escaping closures keep the cheap stack env. Interim-safety option (if a slice can't land): reject a not-yet-supported escaping capturing closure with a clear `not yet supported` diagnostic so no silent miscompile remains. Until fixed, the workaround is to not return / store a capturing closure (pass it down by `Fn(..)` parameter instead, which keeps the defining frame live — already supported). PROGRESS — Slice 0 of the epic landed 2026-06-22 (ab6ff88b): reject_escaping_capturing_closure (a pure-AST pre-check at the top of compile_function) now turns the silent miscompile into an honest `error[E_ESCAPING_CLOSURE_NOT_YET]` for the common TAIL-position escape — a capturing-closure literal, or an identifier bound to one, in the function's tail / return (recursing through block/if/match tails). Sound programs are untouched (non-capturing closures, same-frame use, pass-down-by-Fn-param all still compile — full codegen suite 1743/0). Slice 0 EXTENDED 2026-06-22 (c006ccda): the guard now also covers an explicit mid-body `return <capturing closure>` (collect_outer_return_values walks every `return` not inside a nested closure) and a capturing closure returned inside an aggregate literal (`return H { f: |x| x+k }` / `return (clo, 1)` / `return [clo]`, via aggregate-literal recursion in tail_escapes_capturing_closure). Both walkers are non-exhaustive on purpose — a missed container only UNDER-collects, never falsely rejects (full codegen 1746/0). STAYS OPEN: the feature itself (actually returning a capturing closure) is unimplemented, and ONE narrower silent-miscompile residual remains — a capturing closure stored into a LOCAL struct/Vec that is then returned via an identifier (`let h = H{f:|x|x+k}; return h`), which needs container-escape tracking (the escape-analysis slice). Severity stays high until that residual is guarded or the heap-env feature lands. SLICE 1 WIP on branch clo-rc1 (commit bcf8472c, NOT merged — unsound, do not merge): the RC heap-env CORE is built and proven — a capturing closure that is a function's direct tail gets a reference-counted heap env (emit_rc_alloc {i64 refcount, env}; body GEPs past the refcount), the owning `let f = make()` binding frees it via a new CleanupAction::FreeClosureEnv (RC-dec, free-at-0, null-guarded), driven by compute_fns_returning_heap_env + current_fn_heap_closure_spans; the demonstrated repro `let f = make(21); println(f(21))` compiles and prints 42 (test heap_env_returned_capturing_closure_runs). BLOCKER (remaining ~80%): the comprehensive misuse guard — a heap-env binding that escapes again (`let f = make(); return f` UAF, copy `let g = f`, store, pass-as-arg) and an UNBOUND `make()` (leak) are NOT yet rejected, so the branch is unsound. A sound guard needs an EXHAUSTIVE 'f only ever a call callee' walker (refs_in_expr is unusable — lossy `_ => {}`, 33/~40 ExprKinds, a missed kind = a missed escape = UAF); cleaner end-state is inc-on-copy RC (fold in the shared-ownership slice) so nothing needs rejecting. Then ASAN + LSan gate. SLICE 0 RESIDUAL CLOSED 2026-06-22 (3829b1de): the STAYS-OPEN residual above — a capturing closure stored into a LOCAL aggregate then returned via an identifier (`let h = H{f:|x|x+k}; h`, and the identifier chain `let g=|x|x+k; let h=H{f:g}; h`) — is now GUARDED; both built+ran printing garbage (0 / -1) before and now emit error[E_ESCAPING_CLOSURE_NOT_YET]. reject_escaping_capturing_closure's capturing_vars builder now runs in SOURCE ORDER and reuses tail_escapes_capturing_closure as the `does this RHS produce a capturing closure` predicate (direct closure / aggregate literal / identifier chain), mirroring the ownership pass's collect_closure_let_bindings shapes (closure_escape.rs) — that pass fires only on REF captures (a dangling borrow), so the OWN-capture escape it soundly admits but codegen's stack env cannot yet support is exactly what this guard catches; tail_escapes_capturing_closure also gained the prefix Vec/Map/Repeat-literal + struct ..spread arms for parity with the ownership walker. One-sided as before (codegen 1750/0, par_codegen 123/0, memory_sanitizer 347 passed +1 ign /0). Tests: tests/codegen.rs escaping_capturing_closure_local_struct_then_return_is_rejected, escaping_capturing_closure_local_identifier_chain_is_rejected, non_capturing_closure_local_struct_then_return_still_ok. STILL SILENT (now the only known escaping-closure residual; deferred to the escape-analysis slice — the same field-access class the ownership pass also defers in collect_escape_target): a returned field projection `return h.f` still builds+runs with garbage (-1); guarding it soundly needs struct-shape/projection info to avoid over-rejecting the sound `return h.non_closure_field`. fix field stays empty — the feature is still unimplemented and that residual remains; severity stays high. SEPARATE BUG found while probing (NOT this escape entry): calling a closure stored in a struct field — `(h.f)(arg)` — silently returns 0 even same-frame and non-capturing, because compile_closure_call dispatches only a NAMED-identifier callee via closure_fn_types and a FieldAccess callee falls through to a const-0 stub; notably no existing test ever RUNS a struct-field closure call. Logged as B-2026-06-22-4. SLICE 0 RESIDUAL #2 CLOSED 2026-06-23 (f6a6db65): the returned FIELD-PROJECTION residual flagged above — `let h = H { f: |x| x+k }; return h.f` (also the bare tail `h.f`, and the identifier chain `let g = |x| x+k; let h = H { f: g }; h.f`) — built+ran printing garbage (-1) before and now emits error[E_ESCAPING_CLOSURE_NOT_YET] (interp still prints x+k; guard is codegen-only). reject_escaping_capturing_closure gained a parallel capturing_fields: HashMap<binding, HashSet<field>> built in the SAME source-order pass as capturing_vars — for a direct struct-literal initializer bound to a single name, each field whose value is a capturing closure (a closure literal OR an earlier-bound capturing local) is recorded; tail_escapes_capturing_closure gained a FieldAccess arm that rejects base.field iff capturing_fields[base] contains field. PRECISE by construction (only capturing fields are recorded), so a sound `return h.other_field` (a non-closure or non-capturing field) still compiles — verified by field_projection_guard_does_not_over_reject. Base must be a plain local identifier; deeper projections (a.b.f), projection off a by-value param, and the container index-projection shape stay under-approximated (sound: never false-rejects). One-sided as before (codegen 1759/0, par_codegen 123/0, memory_sanitizer ASAN 347 +1 ign /0). Tests: escaping_capturing_closure_field_projection_is_rejected, escaping_capturing_closure_field_projection_tail_and_chain_is_rejected, field_projection_guard_does_not_over_reject. CORRECTION to the prior 'h.f is the only known escaping-closure residual' claim: probing turned up that the CONTAINER-PUSH shape is ALSO still SILENT — `let v: Vec[Fn(i64)->i64] = Vec.new(); v.push(|x:i64| x+k); return v` BUILDS and RUNS printing garbage (8387098485; interp prints 42). Guarding it soundly needs TYPE info (is the receiver a stdlib collection whose push/insert STORES the element?) — a purely syntactic {push,insert}-on-identifier match would over-reject a user type's push, or a non-storing method that also takes a capturing closure (sort_by / map / filter / retain), breaking the one-sided invariant. That is the escape-analysis slice's container-mutation tracking, a distinct (type-aware) piece. Every heap-env closure place is now supported: RETURN (bare tail / top-level return, branch-leaf), STORE (struct field / tuple / array / Vec element), COPY/MOVE (struct/tuple/array owner COPY, Vec owner MOVE, binding copy), ESCAPE (aggregate / container move-out + caller adopt), by-value ARG-PASS BORROW (binding + struct/tuple/array/Vec owner to a borrows-only callee), and REASSIGNMENT (binding g=f/g=make, struct field r.f=.., Vec element v[i]=..). The original soundness hole — an escaping capturing closure reading a freed stack env — is fully resolved: escaping closures get an RC heap env box, every owner/use site is RC-accounted (inc on copy, dec/free at scope exit, move-out neutralize + caller adopt on escape, drop-old on reassign), and the misuse guard over-rejects (never miscompiles) any not-yet-modeled shape. =====

`--bindings component` (the wasm_wasi default) emitted NON-reproducible component bytes — three builds of identical source produced three DIFFERENT SHA256 at the SAME byte length; `--bindings none` (core module) was always byte-identical. NOT HashMap/iteration-order (the initial hypothesis): a `cmp -l` of two captured cores showed exactly 2 differing bytes, both inside the `name` custom section — `...\x04name\x00! karac_9013_filter_core.core.wasm` vs `...9066...`; `wasm-tools component new` is itself deterministic on fixed input, and the embedded-WIT text is already deterministic (collect_host_fns/collect_wasm_exports iterate `program.items` in source order). Root cause: the embedded-component build links the C-ABI core module to a process-unique SCRATCH file named `karac_<pid>_<stem>.core.wasm` (single-file, src/cli.rs) / `karac_proj_<pid>_<pkg>.core.wasm` (project), and wasm-ld bakes that basename verbatim into the module-name subsection of the wasm `name` section; `wasm-tools component embed`/`component new` then carry it into the final component. Two builds = two processes = two pids → the component differed in exactly the pid digits (same length whenever the pids share a digit width, different bytes always). `--bindings none` was immune because it links straight to the stable `<stem>.wasm` output path (no pid). FIXED by linking the core module under a source-DERIVED basename (`<stem>.core.wasm`) inside a process-unique DIRECTORY — new `componentize::link_core_scratch(stem)` reuses `scratch_dir()` (pid+counter) for the enclosing dir, so parallel builds stay collision-free on disk while the embedded module name — and thus the whole component — is byte-identical run to run; both link sites (single-file + project) and their cleanup (remove the scratch dir, not just the file) updated. Side benefit: the shipped artifact no longer leaks an internal temp path into its name section. Regression: tests/cli.rs `wasm_wasi_component_is_byte_reproducible` builds the same source twice in two distinct processes and asserts byte-identical components (an export drives the WIT embed leg too); a manual 3x build now yields a single SHA. Surfaced via the bench/wasm_size size receipt (the Kāra component brotli figure jittered ~0.2% run-to-run).

Calling a closure stored in a struct field silently returns 0 under `karac build` — `(h.f)(arg)` miscompiles (codegen-only; `karac run` is CORRECT). A closure value (the {fn_ptr, env_ptr} fat pointer) stored in a struct field and invoked through a parenthesized field-access callee — `struct H { f: Fn(i64)->i64 }  let h = H { f: |x| x * 2 };  (h.f)(21)` — typechecks and `karac build` links+runs, but the call evaluates to 0 instead of invoking the closure. Reproduced SAME-FRAME and NON-CAPTURING, so it is independent of the B-2026-06-22-2 escaping-stack-env hole — a distinct closure-call-DISPATCH gap. Confirmed divergence: the same program under `karac run` (tree-walk interpreter) prints the correct 42; only the LLVM backend returns 0. Root-cause hypothesis: compile_closure_call (src/codegen/closures.rs ~L1057) lowers an indirect closure call ONLY when the callee is a NAMED identifier present in closure_fn_types (`match self.closure_fn_types.get(name) { Some(t) => .., None => return Ok(i64 const 0) }`); a FieldAccess callee (and presumably index / tuple-index / call-result place-expressions yielding a closure value) never reaches the fat-pointer extract+indirect-call path, so it falls through to the None const-0 stub. Notably NO existing codegen test RUNS a closure call through a struct field — the only `(x.f)(..)` snippets in tests/codegen.rs are expect_err escape-rejection tests that never execute the program, so the gap was invisible. FIX: generalize closure-call lowering to accept a closure VALUE produced by any expression (field access, index, tuple-index, parenthesized expr, call result) — evaluate it to the {fn_ptr, env_ptr} fat pointer and emit the env-first indirect call — and add E2E tests that actually RUN `(h.f)(arg)` and a Vec[Fn]-index call, asserting parity with `karac run`. Severity high: silent WRONG-OUTPUT, not a diagnostic.

DataFrame.select fresh Vec[String] arg leaked its heap buffer (48 bytes) — `select` is dispatched by try_compile_dataframe_method, which returns early from compile_method_call BEFORE the generic owned-temp argument loop, so a fresh-owned `cols` arg (a Vec[String] literal `df.select(["b", "a"])` — lowered to PrefixCollectionLiteral, which compile_vec_prefix_literal mallocs a real {ptr,len,cap} buffer for — or a fresh call result like other.column_names()) had no consuming binding; select only READS it (names copied in via df_copy_name), so its 48-byte buffer (+ heap element strings) leaked once per call. Pre-existing on main since the phase-11 slice 2c DataFrame codegen (481d6bb6); invisible to local macOS ASAN (no LeakSanitizer), caught only by the Linux LSan gate (asan_dataframe_column_names_select_clean, 48 byte(s) in 1 allocation). FIX: route the arg through the same materialize_owned_temp chokepoint the generic method / free-fn arg loop uses — a FreeVecBuffer (element-type-aware via owned_temp_drops, cap>0-guarded) drains it at scope exit — gated to the fresh forms (expr_yields_fresh_owned_temp || PrefixCollectionLiteral) plus llvm_ty_is_vec_struct, so an identifier/field/index alias (its own binding cleanup) is never double-freed. Mirrors insert's fresh-temp column free. Linux LSan memory_sanitizer 377/0.

Scalar integer-indexing a BORROWED nested collection inferred Type::Error, so a `let row = m[i]` binding (where `m: ref Vec[Vec[i64]]` / `mut ref Slice[T]`) recorded NO surface/element type and codegen later failed with "no handler for method 'len' on variable 'row'" under `karac build` (`karac run`/interpreter was CORRECT; an explicit `let row: Vec[i64] = m[i]` annotation, and the direct double-index forms `m[i][j]` / the nested-index parallel-assignment swap `m[i][j], m[j][i] = m[j][i], m[i][j]`, all worked — only the let-bound-row form broke). Root cause in infer_expr's ExprKind::Index arm (src/typechecker/exprs.rs): the FINAL scalar-index `match &obj_ty` handled Array / Slice / Vector / Vec but had NO Type::Ref/MutRef arm, so a borrowed container fell to `_ => Type::Error` — even though the RANGE-index path (`m[a..b]`) and the Tensor/Column arms just above already peel Ref/MutRef. The Error type propagated silently (no diagnostic), bind_pattern_types recorded nothing for the binding, and method dispatch fell through in codegen. FIX: add a Type::Ref(inner)|Type::MutRef(inner) arm to the scalar-index match that peels one borrow and extracts the inner Array/Slice/Vector/Vec element (mirroring the range path; the Vector arm also records vector_method_receivers), bringing scalar integer indexing of a borrowed collection in line with the rest. Surfaced by kata #48 Rotate Image's matrix display iterating rows of a `ref`-passed Vec[Vec[i64]] (`let row = m[i]; row.len()`). E2E regression: codegen.rs ref_param_nested_vec_row_binding_dispatches + mut_ref_param_nested_vec_row_binding_dispatches. Suites green: typechecker 1040, interpreter 1824, codegen 1909, ASAN 408; clippy/fmt clean.

Printing a `char` that crosses a CALL-RETURN SSA boundary formatted the i32 scalar as its integer codepoint under `karac build` (LLVM codegen) instead of the UTF-8 glyph — `println(f())` for `fn f() -> char { 'A' }` emitted `65`, not `A`; `karac run` (interpreter) was CORRECT, so a run/build divergence. Also hit `println(f"{f()}")` (f-string interpolation of a call) and `println(b.get())` for a method `fn get(self) -> char` (printed `77`, not `M`). NOT triggered by char literals, char-typed identifiers, Vec[char] index reads, `String.push(char)`, or a `let c = pick(); println(c)` binding (the untyped-let type-expr recovery already records `c: char`). Root cause: codegen's print / f-string char arm gates on `expr_is_char` (src/codegen/expr_ops.rs), which had arms for CharLit / Identifier (via var_type_names) / Index (element TypeExpr) / a `.clone()`-only MethodCall / FieldAccess, but NO free-function `Call` arm and no general `MethodCall` arm — so a call result fell through to `_ => false` and the value (char lowers to i32) hit the generic `%lld` integer path. The sibling `expr_is_unsigned_int` already carried exactly these two arms (added 2026-06-06 for the `println(u8_fn())` sign-extension bug, 200 printed as -56). FIX: add to `expr_is_char` a free-fn `Call` arm keyed on `fn_return_type_names[name] == "char"` and generalize the `MethodCall` arm to resolve the receiver's type name and look up the impl method's declared return type under the synth `Type.method` key (keeping the `.clone()` receiver-recursion special case) — a mirror of the unsigned-int arms; this restores the source `char` type across the call-return boundary for both the direct-print and f-string paths. E2E regression: codegen.rs test_e2e_println_char_call_return (direct free-fn return, f-string call interpolation, method return, multibyte 3-byte UTF-8 return, plus the already-working let-bound form as a non-regression guard), asserting glyph parity with `karac run`. Read-only predicate change — no drop/ownership/alloc path touched, so no LSan exposure. Suites green: full codegen E2E 1916, codegen `char` filter 28 incl. the new E2E; clippy --all --all-targets --features llvm and cargo fmt --all --check clean.

`s[i]` (scalar integer index) on a `String` inferred Type::Error SILENTLY in infer_expr's ExprKind::Index final `match &obj_ty` (the `_ => Type::Error` arm had no Type::Str case and recorded NO diagnostic), so the program typechecked, reached the tree-walk interpreter, and hit `unreachable!` on the (Value::String, Value::Int) operand pair (src/interpreter/eval_expr.rs:605) — an internal-error PANIC instead of the newcomer-friendly compile error the spec mandates (design.md § Character type: UTF-8 is variable-width, so `[]` would hide an O(n) scan). Minimal repro `fn main() { let s = "hello"; println(s[0]); }` panicked under `karac run`. ROOT CAUSE: the scalar-index match handled Array/Slice/Vector/Vec (+ a Ref/MutRef peel arm from B-2026-06-29-2) but never String, and String isn't a sequence element type, so it fell to the silent `_ => Type::Error`. FIX: detect `Type::Str` (peeling ref/mut-ref) in the Index arm AFTER the range-index path — so `s[a..b]` String slicing (→ fresh String) is untouched — emit the spec diagnostic ('String does not support indexing with []' + O(n) rationale + `s.char_at(i)` / `s.bytes()[i]` help) and return Type::Error. New `TypeErrorKind::StringNotIndexable` (E0268) is marked is_run_fatal() so `karac run`'s lenient script path ABORTS with error[typecheck] (exit 1) rather than downgrading to warning[typecheck] and tripping the same interpreter `unreachable!` — mirrors the invalid-cast run-fatal family (B-2026-06-13-15); `karac check`/`build` already rejected via the has_type_errors gate. The two alternatives the diagnostic points at keep working (separate paths): `s.char_at(i)` (method call → Option[char]) and `s.bytes()[i]` (Slice[u8] index). Regression: tests/typechecker.rs::{test_string_scalar_index_rejected_with_newcomer_diagnostic asserting the full diagnostic body, test_string_char_at_and_bytes_index_still_work}; codegen string suite (16 incl. bytes-index / char_at / slice) green under --features llvm.

Reassigning a non-`mut` field of a `shared`/`par struct` was unchecked at compile time — `karac build` silently accepted the write (exit 0) while `karac run`'s interpreter caught it only as a runtime error (`write_shared_struct_field` defense-in-depth guard). Root cause: the typechecker had no shared-field write-permission rule and `StructInfo` dropped per-field `is_mut`. FIXED (a0f77cbb): new `SharedFieldNotMut` typecheck kind (run-fatal, E0269) rejects the write on run/check/build, mirroring the interpreter's rule (assignable iff `f.is_mut` OR the field is an interior-mutable `Atomic[T]`/`Mutex[T]`); carried via a new `StructInfo.mut_fields` set, gated on the immediate assignment LHS through the already-captured `is_lhs` in `infer_field_access`. Surfaced verifying book examples. Tests: tests/typechecker.rs shared_struct_non_mut_field_reassign_rejected / _mut_field_reassign_accepted / _atomic_field_reassign_accepted / par_struct_non_mut_field_reassign_rejected / plain_struct_field_reassign_not_gated / shared_field_not_mut_is_run_fatal.

Iterating a shared-borrowed `Vec` of a Copy scalar bound the element `ref i64`, not auto-deref'd in arithmetic — `for x in row` over a `ref Vec[i64]` (the inner loop of a `ref Vec[Vec[i64]]`): `karac run` emitted `warning: expected 'i64', found 'ref i64'` and computed the right answer, while `karac build` HARD-errored `error[typecheck]: expected 'i64', found 'ref i64'` on `total + x` (build stricter than run). Root cause: `element_type_of` re-wrapped a borrowed collection's element in the same borrow form, so a nested `ref Vec[Vec[i64]]` yielded `ref i64` at the innermost element (unlike `Slice[i64]`, which yields `i64`). FIXED (a0f77cbb): the shared-`Type::Ref` arm now binds a Copy-scalar element BY VALUE (`i64` — copying a scalar out of a shared borrow is not a move), matching `Slice[i64]` and the `Str`->`char` peel; the `mut ref` arm is untouched so in-place element mutation keeps `mut ref T`, and non-scalar elements (`ref Vec`, `ref String`) stay borrowed to preserve the move-out-of-borrow rejection. Surfaced verifying book examples. Tests: tests/typechecker.rs for_loop_borrowed_nested_vec_scalar_element_usable_in_arithmetic / _triple_nested / _vec_of_string_element_stays_borrowed; tests/codegen.rs + tests/interpreter.rs for_loop_borrowed_nested_vec_scalar_autoderefs (run/build agreement -> 10).

Atomic[T] op arity diverged between `karac run` and `karac build`: the implicit-ordering form (`c.count.fetch_add(1)` / `c.count.load()`) ran fine under the interpreter (which ignores the absent MemoryOrdering) but failed `karac build` with 'codegen: Atomic.fetch_add takes (value, MemoryOrdering), got 1 args'. The spec defines NO implicit-ordering form — every atomic op is declared with an explicit ordering (deferred.md § Atomic Operations: load(ord), store(val,ord), fetch_*/swap(val,ord), compare_exchange(old,new,succ,fail)) — so the fix requires the ordering on BOTH surfaces (option b, explicit-over-magic). Shipped example examples/mend/examples/concurrent_emails/solution.kara used the 1-arg form, so it ran but did not build. ROOT CAUSE: typechecker infer_method_call special-cased only compare_exchange; load/store/fetch_*/swap fell through to the silent Type::Error arm with NO arity check, so the implicit form passed typecheck; codegen's compile_atomic_method DID require the ordering, hence the split. FIX: (1) new run-fatal TypeErrorKind::AtomicMissingOrdering (E0270) + a typechecker arm that requires the ordering on load (1 arg) / store+RMW (2 args) for Atomic receivers and models the real return type (T for load/RMW, Unit for store) instead of Type::Error; run-fatal so `karac run`'s lenient script path aborts rather than downgrade-and-run, matching build. (2) interpreter backstop in method_call_optres.rs (load/store/fetch_*/swap/compare_exchange) — REQUIRED because a field access through a `ref`/`mut ref` struct param types as Type::Error (fields.rs), so `c.count.fetch_add(1)` on a `ref Counter` slips past typecheck; the guard makes `karac run` reject it too, matching codegen. (3) solution.kara + its canned_responses.json / notes.md / .cast demo mirrors moved to the explicit MemoryOrdering.Relaxed form. Regression: typechecker.rs test_atomic_op_missing_ordering_rejected (asserts kind + run-fatal) / test_atomic_op_with_explicit_ordering_typechecks; interpreter.rs test_atomic_fetch_add_load_explicit_agrees_with_codegen (prints 3) / test_atomic_implicit_ordering_rejected_by_interpreter; par_codegen.rs test_e2e_atomic_fetch_add_load_explicit (prints 3, interpreter parity) / test_atomic_implicit_ordering_rejected_by_codegen. examples/ all build under karac build. Sibling of the same-day B-2026-06-30-3/-4 run/build unifications.

Iterating a `mut ref Vec` of a Copy scalar bound the element `mut ref i64`, not auto-deref'd in arithmetic — the mutable-borrow sibling of B-2026-06-30-4. `for x in xs { x = x * 2; }` over a `mut ref Vec[i64]`: `karac check`/`build` HARD-errored `error[typecheck]: arithmetic operator requires numeric type, found 'mut ref i64'` while `karac run` warned-and-proceeded (and the `x = x * 2` write silently no-op'd) — a run/build divergence. Root cause: `element_type_of`'s `Type::MutRef` arm bound the bare-`for` loop variable as `mut ref i64`, which arithmetic does not auto-deref. But design.md § Iteration (line 2739) makes bare `for x in c` desugar to `c.iter()` — it borrows and yields `ref T` REGARDLESS of the collection's own borrow form (line 2758 reaffirms); mutable iteration is the explicit `.iter_mut()` path, not bare `for`. So the `MutRef` arm was mistyping a read-only shared element as `mut ref`. FIXED (cc002702): mirror the B-2026-06-30-4 shared-`Type::Ref` arm — a Copy scalar element binds BY VALUE (`i64`), aggregates (`mut ref Vec`, `mut ref String`, structs) keep the borrow form so the move-out-of-borrow rejection is preserved. This unifies check/run/build (loop var is `i64`, arithmetic works) and keeps the type HONEST: the element is read-only, so `x = x * 2` is a plain local reassignment that does NOT reach the Vec (bare `for` never mutates — use `xs[i] = ...` over `0..xs.len()`, or the future `.iter_mut()`), consistent across all surfaces. NOT a rejection: `x = x * 2; total = total + x` (reassign-and-use of the by-value loop local) is a legitimate working pattern computing 14; a hard error on loop-var assignment would wrongly reject it. (Renumbered from -5, concurrently claimed on main by the unrelated Atomic-ordering divergence 12c2346f.) Tests: tests/typechecker.rs (mut-ref scalar arithmetic, reassign-and-use, triple-nested, aggregate `mut ref Vec[String]` stays borrowed); tests/interpreter.rs + tests/codegen.rs (run/build agreement — repro prints 3/4 consistently, reassign-and-use computes 14 on both surfaces). fmt + clippy --all-targets clean (native + llvm); codegen E2E + memory_sanitizer green.

A for-loop over a struct FIELD's Vec accessed through `self` inside an impl method (`for s in self.items.iter()`) silently iterated ZERO times under `karac build` while the interpreter (`karac run`) iterated correctly — a SILENT miscompile (wrong output, no error). `self.items.len()` returned the correct count (field Vec intact); only the for-loop over the field was broken. ROOT CAUSE: `try_compile_for_field_iter` (src/codegen/control_flow_for.rs) hand-rolled its own receiver-pointer/field-GEP resolution that (1) matched only `Identifier`/`Index`, never `self` (which parses as `ExprKind::SelfValue`), so the self-field iterable fell through to the for-dispatcher's silent `_ =>` skip; (2) even when matched used `slot.ptr` directly — one indirection short for a `ref self` receiver (the alloca holds a pointer-TO-struct); (3) for shared structs single-loaded `slot.ptr` (short for a `ref self` handle) and hardcoded the header-shifted GEP layout (wrong for Phase-D headerless shared structs). FIX (948a758b): delegate to the canonical `lower_field_access_ptr` (the same field-place resolver the method-call field-receiver path uses), which handles owned vs `ref` bindings vs shared handles and the Phase-D layout uniformly, so the for-loop and method paths can no longer drift; `self` is normalised to a synthetic `Identifier("self")` in the for-loop CALLER (mirrors `compile_method_call`'s `self.field.method()` normalisation), NOT by teaching the resolver about `SelfValue` — the resolver deliberately leaves a bare `SelfValue` at `Ok(None)` to preserve the atomic-on-self method path (`self.count.fetch_add(..)`), and a for-loop source is unambiguously a collection field (atomics are never iterable). Both `.iter()` and bare `for s in self.items` forms route through the same helper, so both are fixed together, on plain and shared structs. Verified byte-identical `karac run` vs `karac build` (default auto-par + AUTO_PAR=0): plain 201, shared 119; atomic-on-self unchanged (3); Linux LSan clean. Discovered while implementing owned-temp slice 3j; independent pre-existing method-body-lowering bug. Tests: tests/codegen.rs test_e2e_for_self_field_vec_iter_in_impl_method / _shared_struct / test_ir_for_self_field_vec_iter_emits_loop_body; tests/memory_sanitizer.rs asan_for_self_field_vec_iter_no_double_free / asan_for_shared_self_field_vec_iter_no_double_free. OUT OF SCOPE (separate pre-existing HARD-error gaps, not silent): owned-`self` method dispatch on a variable receiver, and `self.items[i].method()` indexed-receiver method dispatch.

`TaskGroup.new()` / `tg.spawn(closure)` / `handle.join()` / `tg.cancel()` and the free `spawn(closure)` compiled and ran under `karac build` (codegen lowers them to `karac_runtime_taskgroup_*` / real work-pool tasks) but had NO tree-walk-interpreter evaluation rule, so `karac run` DIVERGED: `TaskGroup.new` hit the generic "accepted by the typechecker but not wired in the tree-walk interpreter — please report this" internal error, and free `spawn(closure)` panicked as "variable 'spawn' not found" (unresolved identifier reaching the eval_expr Identifier arm). A run/build divergence of the same class as the Atomic-ordering split B-2026-06-30-5 (12c2346f). ROOT CAUSE: the interpreter's builtin dispatch had arms for `Atomic.new` / `Mutex.new` / `collect_all` / `par {}` but none for the structured-concurrency surface declared in runtime/stdlib/task_group.kara. FIX (b11459e1): give the interpreter real evaluation rules, following the established convention that concurrency primitives run sequentially/eagerly in the tree-walk host (collect_all / collect_all_vec; real OS threads via thread::scope are reserved for lexically-scoped `par {}`). Two Value variants — `TaskGroup` (stateless marker — the group carries no live task state because children run eagerly) and `TaskHandle(Box<Value>)` (carries the child's already-computed result), each with Display + variant_name arms; a `TaskGroup.new` associated-call arm (sibling of `Atomic.new`) returns the marker; a `spawn` builtin-identifier arm + shared `eval_spawn_closure` run the closure eagerly at the spawn site and box the result into a `TaskHandle` (a panicking child dominates via pending_cf — fail-fast parity with par {} / collect_all); receiver-typed method dispatch routes `TaskGroup.spawn` (run child, return handle), `TaskGroup.cancel` (no-op — children already ran), and `TaskHandle.join` (deliver the boxed result), gated on the concrete receiver value so they never shadow `Command.spawn` / `String.join`. The join-at-spawn model is observably identical to the parallel codegen for the order-independent fan-out/join shape the `ScopeLocal` rules permit (a handle cannot escape its spawning scope, so cross-task communication is confined to shared Atomic/Mutex cells whose interpreter models are already thread-safe); it deliberately does NOT model programs that observe shared mutable state BETWEEN spawn and the group's drop barrier (those read a racy, unsynchronized value under codegen too). Verified byte-identical `karac run` == `karac build`: 60 for the explicit-join fan-out (worker(10)+worker(20)) and 42 for free spawn/join. Tests: tests/interpreter.rs test_taskgroup_spawn_join_agrees_with_codegen / test_free_spawn_join_agrees_with_codegen + codegen E2E twins tests/par_codegen.rs test_e2e_taskgroup_spawn_join / test_e2e_free_spawn_join (all assert the same output). OUT OF SCOPE: genuinely-parallel deferred execution in the interpreter — the tree-walk host holds program/typecheck_result as borrows that cannot cross into a 'static thread::spawn, so `par {}` gets real threads only via its lexical thread::scope.

Arithmetic on a `mut ref`/`ref` numeric SCALAR did not auto-deref, so `x = x + 1i64` where `x: mut ref i64` diverged: `karac check`/`build` HARD-errored `error[typecheck]: arithmetic operator requires numeric type, found 'mut ref i64'` while `karac run` warned-and-applied (the interpreter mutates through the borrow, printing 11) — a check/build ↔ run divergence. The scalar-parameter sibling of B-2026-06-30-6 (which fixed `mut ref Vec` ELEMENT typing under iteration; this fixes a `mut ref` scalar used DIRECTLY in `a = a + b`). NOTE `x += 1i64` already typechecked — `StmtKind::CompoundAssign` is checked as `infer_expr(target); infer_expr(value)` and never builds the `x + 1` binary, so only the explicit `x = x + 1` (desugared Assign with a Binary RHS) hit the arithmetic arm. ROOT CAUSE: the `BinOp::Add|Sub|Mul|Div|Mod` arm of `infer_binary` (src/typechecker/expr_ops.rs) tests `is_numeric(&left_ty)`, which is false for `Type::MutRef(i64)` / `Type::Ref(i64)`; it never read through the borrow, though design.md § "Compound assignment on `mut ref` lvalues" (:5306) mandates read-through (`a = a + b` on a `mut ref T` lvalue desugars to `*a = *a + b`, so the RHS reads `*a`), and comparison ops already strip borrows via `strip_refs_for_compare`. FIX (6f00795b): add `deref_numeric_scalar` — peel a single `ref`/`mut ref` off a NUMERIC-scalar operand — and apply it to both operands in the arithmetic arm BEFORE Q4 literal promotion, so an unsuffixed literal (`x + 1`) still promotes to the pointee type and records its span (a borrow left un-stripped makes `is_numeric` false, skipping promotion and risking a codegen literal-width mismatch). The result type is the bare scalar `T`. Gated on a numeric pointee, so a non-numeric borrow (`mut ref bool`) still reaches the "requires numeric type" diagnostic (reporting the original type). Exposed a co-fixed pre-existing codegen silent miscompile, B-2026-06-30-10. (IDs -9/-10: originally minted -7/-8, which collided with the self-field-for-iter and TaskGroup/spawn fixes that landed concurrently on main.) Tests: tests/typechecker.rs (implicit assign, unsuffixed-literal promotion, `ref` read-through, float, non-numeric-still-errors); tests/interpreter.rs + tests/codegen.rs (run/build agreement, all print 11). fmt + clippy --all-targets clean (native + llvm); typechecker, interpreter, codegen, memory_sanitizer green.

Mutating a `mut ref` numeric-SCALAR parameter via an identifier target (`x = x + 1` / `x += 1`) did NOT propagate to the caller in built binaries — a SILENT miscompile: `karac build` printed the un-mutated value (10) while `karac run` / the interpreter printed 11. Independent of and PRE-EXISTING relative to B-2026-06-30-9: `x += 1i64` already typechecked, so any program mutating a `mut ref` scalar param via `+=`/`=` silently no-op'd on the caller in shipped binaries; the explicit `x = x + 1` form was merely masked by -9's typecheck rejection until it was fixed, which surfaced this. ROOT CAUSE: a `mut ref T` scalar param is POINTER-represented — `inner_type_of_ref` registers it in `ref_params` and its entry alloca holds the borrow POINTER (load_variable derefs on read; get_data_ptr loads the pointer). But the identifier-target store in both `StmtKind::Assign` (the generic `build_store(slot.ptr, val)` fallthrough) and `StmtKind::CompoundAssign` (src/codegen/stmts.rs) wrote into `slot.ptr` — the alloca holding the POINTER — clobbering the borrow pointer with the integer value instead of writing THROUGH it, so the caller's storage was never updated. FIX (6f00795b): in both arms, when the identifier is a numeric-scalar `ref_param`, store the width-coerced value THROUGH `get_data_ptr(name)` (which loads the borrow pointer) and return early. Scalar-only (int/float pointee): Vec/String/struct `mut ref` mutate via methods / field stores that already deref through `get_data_ptr`, and routing their whole-value moves here would bypass the heap move-tracking (eager-free / move-suppression) below. Verified byte-identical `karac run` vs `karac build`: implicit-assign 11, compound-assign 11, narrow `mut ref i32` 42, `ref i64` read-through 11.
