Tests: tests/codegen.rs mut_ref_scalar_param_{implicit_assign,compound_assign,narrow_int_assign}_through_e2e + ref_scalar_param_read_through_arithmetic_e2e; tests/interpreter.rs run/build-parity peers. memory_sanitizer green (scalar stores — no heap alloc/free surface). fmt + clippy --all-targets clean (native + llvm).

A user program that redefined ONE always-injected stdlib type (`struct Response`, exported by `runtime/stdlib/http.kara`) lost the WHOLE module: `Server.serve(addr, handler)` / `Server.serve_tls(...)` HARD-errored `no associated function 'serve' on type 'Server'` (TypeErrorKind::NoMethodFound) at typecheck, taking down the entire tests/http_server.rs serve / serve_tls / http2 suite (18 of 25 tests; the 7 serve_static tests survived only because they don't define `struct Response`). `karac build` (and the codegen test harness's `karac::typecheck`) rejected it hard; `karac run` downgraded the same error to a non-fatal `warning[typecheck]` and then failed later at the interpreter, so the two surfaces disagreed. ROOT CAUSE (git-bisected to f6d9873f, the phase-12 #34 stdlib-collision fix): `register_baked_stdlib` (src/typechecker/env_build.rs) implemented the collision-skip at MODULE granularity — when the user program redefined any struct/enum a baked module exports, the ENTIRE module was dropped from env injection. #34's target (the self-host `struct Parser` vs std.cli's `Parser`, so `Parser.new(tokens)` resolves to the user impl) was met, but the hammer was far too wide: redefining a single common type name (`Response`) also dropped its sibling exports (`Server`, `Request`, `HttpError`, `impl Server`, `impl Request`), so `Server.serve` no longer resolved via the `env.impls` associated-fn lookup in expr_ops.rs. FIX: make the collision-skip per-ITEM instead of per-module — drop only the redefined `struct`/`enum` and any impl block whose target type is that name; keep all sibling types, their impls, and free functions always-injected. This still satisfies #34 (the user's `struct Parser` + `impl Parser` become the sole `Parser` surface — std.cli's `struct Parser` AND `impl Parser` are both dropped, so `Parser.new` binds to the user impl, which is exactly why dropping the type WITHOUT its impls was unsafe and the per-item filter drops both) without amputating the rest of the module. A kept sibling impl whose signature names the redefined type now references the USER's type through the shared env — identical to the pre-f6d9873f shadowing behavior, and only sites that actually call such a sibling are affected (those were already the user's to reconcile). NOT one of the two same-day suspects (489825fa codegen / 12c2346f atomic) that predated the report — both are codegen/atomic-only and structurally cannot emit a typecheck NoMethodFound; the bisect over c119c1f6..d280af49 (1074 commits) pinned the real culprit at f6d9873f (2026-06-18). The `compiling_stdlib` gate (a stdlib module self-compiling must not skip itself) is unchanged. Tests: tests/typechecker.rs test_redefining_one_stdlib_type_keeps_sibling_module_items (redefine `Response`, assert `Server.serve` / `serve_tls` resolve and `req.path()` still works); the full tests/http_server.rs suite (25 passed / 0 failed, was 7/18); #34 guard test_e2e_user_struct_shadows_always_injected_stdlib_type still green. fmt + clippy --all --all-targets clean; cargo test + cargo test --features llvm green.

`String.sorted()` — characters sorted ascending into a fresh String, the canonical anagram key — was interpreter-only: `karac build` HARD-errored `codegen failed: Vec/String method 'sorted' is not yet supported in codegen` while `karac run` computed it via `chars().sort_unstable()`, a run/build divergence for a general-purpose String method. Surfaced authoring kata leetcode/1-100/49-group-anagrams (the sorted-string anagram key `word.sorted()`; the index-map grouping — `Map[String,i64]` key→group-index + `Vec[Vec[String]]` with `groups[i].push(word)` in-place element mutation — and the count-signature sibling key all ALREADY lowered, probed under both surfaces first). FIX (a00a2f58): a `karac_string_sorted(data, len, *mut out_len) -> ptr` runtime helper (runtime/src/clone.rs) mirroring the interpreter char-sort byte-for-byte — sorts by Unicode SCALAR value, not raw byte, so both backends agree on multi-byte input, not just the kata's ASCII. Wired through codegen identically to the sibling `karac_string_{trim,to_lowercase,to_uppercase}` xforms via `build_string_xform_result`; the new method arm is guarded on `string_vars.contains(var_name)` so a String receiver routes to the helper while `Vec[T].sorted()` still falls through to the catch-all UNCHANGED (the same String-vs-Vec disambiguation the `push` arm uses). New runtime symbol → rebuilt lean+full native archives (runtime-archive-rebuild-dance) + reinstalled to ~/.local. Tests: tests/codegen.rs::e2e_string_sorted_codegen (literal + identifier receiver, non-mutating receiver, empty string, anagram equality); tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_string_sorted_no_leak_no_double_free (fresh buffer freed exactly once, 36-byte payload ×1000). fmt + clippy --all --all-targets --features llvm clean; string/sort codegen (230) + interpreter sorted (31) suites green; Vec.sorted() unchanged. Both kata solvers (sorted-key ★ + count-signature) A/B-identical to the Python oracle across karac run, karac build (default auto-par), and KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0.

`String.cmp(other) -> Ordering` — the method form of the `<`/`>` operators — was rejected at typecheck (`error[typecheck]: no method 'cmp' on type 'String'`) under `karac check`/`build`, while `karac run` merely emitted the same as a `warning[typecheck]` and then executed it — a run/check divergence. `impl Ord for String` (cmp/lt/le/gt/ge) + `impl Eq for String` ARE registered in env_build.rs (String is in `eq_ord_targets`), but String's method dispatch (`infer_str_method`, reached because `obj_ty == Type::Str` routes there unconditionally) is a CLOSED hardcoded list whose unknown-method arm calls `require_known_method` — which hard-errors without ever consulting the registered trait impl. So `s.cmp(t)` was unreachable via method syntax even though the operators and the `Vec[String].sort`/`binary_search` byte-order (karac_string_cmp) already existed. Integer/char `.cmp` resolve fine (they fall through to the impl-table lookup, not a closed dispatcher). FIX (1cf09cb6): (a) typecheck — added a `cmp` arm to `infer_str_method` returning `Ordering` and validating exactly one String-like arg; (b) codegen — added a String branch to the `.cmp` lowering in method_call.rs (extract each operand's {ptr,len}, call the existing karac_string_cmp which returns -1/0/+1, map to the Ordering tag via `tag = cmp + 1` since Less=0/Equal=1/Greater=2), guarded on `inferred_receiver_type(object) == Some("String")` so a user struct's derived-Ord `.cmp` still falls through unchanged; (c) NO new runtime symbol (karac_string_cmp pre-existed). The interpreter already computed String.cmp via `value_compare` — but see B-2026-06-30-14, which fixing this exposed. Tests: tests/codegen.rs::e2e_string_cmp_codegen; tests/interpreter.rs::test_match_bare_ordering_variant_from_cmp. Surfaced authoring kata leetcode/1-100/49-group-anagrams (probing `sort_by(|a,b| a.cmp(b))` comparators).

SILENT interpreter miscompile: `match x.cmp(y) { Less => .., Equal => .., Greater => .. }` with BARE (unqualified) Ordering variant patterns ALWAYS took the first arm — `karac run` returned Less for EVERY comparison (both int AND String `.cmp`) while `karac build` (codegen) was correct. A run/build divergence that silently produced wrong results, pre-existing on main and independent of B-2026-06-30-13 (int cmp was already typecheck-valid); fixing -13 (String.cmp method) merely exposed it for String too. ROOT CAUSE: the interpreter's pattern matcher (pattern_match.rs `PatternKind::Binding`) classifies a bare PascalCase identifier as a unit-variant pattern only when `self.env.get(name)` returns a unit `EnumVariant` value — the mechanism user-enum unit variants rely on — otherwise it is a catch-all binding that matches anything. `register_items` (interpreter.rs) registered the built-in `Ordering` (and `MemoryOrdering`) variants under their QUALIFIED names only (`"Ordering.Less"`), so `env.get("Less")` was None and bare `Less` fell through to the always-true binding, making the first arm win. The qualified `Ordering.Less` pattern and `sort_by(|a,b| a.cmp(b))` (which reads the Ordering value directly, no match) both worked, which is why the bug hid. FIX (1cf09cb6): bind BOTH the qualified and the bare names for Ordering + MemoryOrdering, mirroring user-enum registration (PascalCase, so no collision with snake_case value bindings per Kāra's case-class invariant). Tests: tests/interpreter.rs::test_match_bare_ordering_variant_from_cmp (int + String, correct arms). Interpreter (1055) + user-enum bare matching unaffected. Surfaced authoring kata leetcode/1-100/49-group-anagrams while adding String.cmp.

`Vec.sort()` on a non-integer / non-String element type (e.g. `Vec[Vec[String]]`) runs under `karac run` (the interpreter's `value_compare` handles nested Vecs lexicographically) but errors under `karac build`: `codegen failed: Vec.sort() in codegen supports integer and String element types; use sort_by(|a, b| a.cmp(b)) for other element types` (src/codegen/vec_method.rs sort arm). A run/build divergence (honest error, not silent). The suggested workaround itself is incomplete for nested Vecs: after B-2026-06-30-13 `String.cmp` works, but there is no `Vec[T].cmp` method, so `outer.sort_by(|a,b| a.cmp(b))` on `Vec[Vec[String]]` does not typecheck either. FIX DIRECTION: emit a comparator thunk for the general element type in the `sort`/`sort_by` codegen path — recursively compare nested Vec/other Ord element types the way the interpreter's `value_compare` does (lexicographic for Vec, karac_string_cmp for String leaves). Surfaced authoring kata leetcode/1-100/49-group-anagrams (canonicalising the output by sorting the groups; the kata itself does NOT need it — it uses an index-map first-appearance order — so this is tracked, not blocking). NOT fixed inline: heavier codegen slice than the String.sorted/String.cmp gaps the kata drove. FIXED 463fa826 along the sketched direction: recursive `i64 karac_cmp_<T>(ptr,ptr)` comparator family (scalars by declared signedness, floats partial-order NaN=Equal, String via karac_string_cmp, Vec/VecDeque lexicographic-then-length, tuples per-field) wrapped into the karac_vec_sort_by thunk ABI; unordered elements (user structs) keep a loud sort_by-directing error. TWO STALE PREMISES corrected while fixing: (1) the interpreter did NOT handle nested Vecs — value_compare had no Value::Array arm, so nested sorts were a silent NO-OP (Equal fallback + stable sort = insertion order preserved); Array + Slice lexicographic arms added. (2) Floats were also unsupported in codegen sort (now covered by the family).

Tensor element-wise negation `-t` lowered to `0.0 - x` (fsub with a zero LHS) instead of a true IEEE `fneg`, so a `0.0` element negated to `+0.0` under `karac build` while `karac run` (interpreter `eval_unary` computes `-f`) yields `-0.0` — printed as `0` vs `-0`, a silent run-vs-build divergence at exactly the signed-zero edge (all non-zero elements agree; NumPy also fnegs: `-np.array([0.0])` is `[-0.]`). Int tensor neg was unaffected (`0 - x` via compile_binop_typed IS the correct checked form there). Found probing the S3 element-wise-map unification (docs/spikes/reduce-elementwise-trait-unification.md): the shared emitter made the two surfaces' neg postures directly comparable. FIX: MapKernelOp::Neg in src/codegen/kernel.rs emits build_float_neg for float elements (checked `0 - x` kept for ints); compile_tensor_neg routes through it (Unary+Neg) instead of Binop(Sub)+zero-scalar. Regression: tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_tensor_neg_signed_zero (A/B: `-0` on both surfaces).

Column element-wise negation `-c` on an `i64` column lowered to a bare wrapping `ineg` (build_int_neg), so a slot holding `i64::MIN` silently wrapped back to `i64::MIN` under `karac build` while `karac run` traps with `integer overflow` (interpreter `eval_unary` uses `checked_neg`) — a missed-trap miscompile: Kara's checked-arithmetic posture requires the overflow trap, and Tensor's `-t` (0 - x via compile_binop_typed) already trapped, so the two container surfaces disagreed with each other AND with the interpreter. Float column neg (fneg) was already correct. Found probing the S3 element-wise-map unification alongside B-2026-07-01-1. FIX: the shared MapKernelOp::Neg emits checked `0 - x` via compile_binop_typed for int elements (fneg kept for floats); Column `-c` routes through it instead of NegNative. Regression: tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_column_neg_i64_min_traps. Narrow-int columns (i32 etc.) now trap at their own width on neg, which still diverges from the interpreter's width-lax i64 evaluation — that pre-existing class is tracked separately as B-2026-07-01-3.

Interpreter narrow-int width laxity for Column/Tensor element-wise ops: the interpreter stores every integer element as Value::Int(i64) and evaluates element-wise arithmetic at i64 width, so a `Column[i32]` op that overflows i32 does NOT trap under `karac run` and produces an out-of-range value — probe: `Column[i32]` holding `2147483647`, `c + 1` prints `2147483648` under `karac run` while `karac build` traps `integer overflow` (codegen computes at the element's LLVM width via compile_binop_typed, which is the correct checked-at-i32 semantics). A run-vs-build divergence class covering ALL narrow-int (i8/i16/i32/u*) element-wise ops and likely element-typed reductions (sum folds at i64 in the interpreter vs elem-width in codegen's emit_reduce_fold) on Column and Tensor. Found probing the S3 element-wise-map unification (docs/spikes/reduce-elementwise-trait-unification.md). FIX DIRECTION: the interpreter needs element-width-aware arithmetic for container elements — either wrap/trap at the declared element width in the shared map_binop_slots/eval_binary path (threading the element TypeExpr through the container ops), or normalize+bounds-check on element store. Related to the receiver-width class in receiver-width-recovery-for-scalar-methods. FIXED 2026-07-02 (1078e747): narrow_oob now peels Column[T]/Tensor[T, S] recorded types down to their element before the width check — a single peel covers all 4 element-wise container paths (map_binop_slots funnels them through eval_binary with the container binop's span). Column[i32] + 1 on INT_MAX traps 'integer overflow' under karac run, matching build; in-range narrow ops (incl. u8) unchanged; reductions were already interp-correct via the S5 checked folds. Probing this fix unearthed the far bigger codegen literal-packing bug B-2026-07-02-6. Regressions: tests/interpreter.rs::test_column_narrow_element_binop_{overflow_traps,in_range_ok}, tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_narrow_column_elemwise_overflow_traps.

karac test panicked the interpreter ("internal error: entered unreachable code: variable not found; should be caught by resolver", src/interpreter/eval_expr.rs) on any test body calling a name imported from a sibling module — the per-test execution Program was built from ONLY the test module's own items (m.items.clone() in cmd_test), so the imported item resolved and typechecked at the tree level (Resolver/TypeChecker::with_tree) but its body was absent at execution; the JIT path had the same gap (missing symbol at compile). Cross-package dep imports in tests would have hit it identically. Minimal repro: src/util.kara `pub fn triple(...)`, main_test.kara `import util.triple; test { triple(4) }` → panic, exit 101. FIX: cmd_test now executes every test against the karac-run-style merged super-program (build_super_program_for_run — all modules concatenated, imports dropped, gated-stdlib expansions appended), resolved+typechecked once for the whole suite; execution-equivalent to what karac build/run already concatenate, so no new collision constraint. The tree-wide per-module compile pass (imports, visibility, module scoping) still gates first. Regression: tests/cli.rs::test_test_cross_module_import_executes + test_xpkg_test_runner_imports_path_dep_items.

Test-companion merge tripped E0101 'already defined in this scope' when the _test.kara file re-declared an import its production sibling already has — the natural pattern, since each file states its own imports and a companion imports the same helpers the code under test uses (e.g. both main.kara and main_test.kara `import mathx.double;`). build_program_tree's merge_test_companions arm concatenated both files' Item::Import decls verbatim, so the merged module bound the name twice. FIX (src/module.rs merge arm): exact re-imports — same module path, same source name, same alias — are deduped from the companion's items/imports at merge time; a same-binding import from a DIFFERENT path or source name is kept and still errors as the genuine conflict it is. Regression: tests/cli.rs::test_test_companion_reimport_dedup_keeps_genuine_conflicts (conflict kept) + test_xpkg_test_runner_imports_path_dep_items (dedup exercised — fixture imports the same dep items in both files).

Enum-variant Drop-typed temporary passed directly as a call argument never ran its user drop body — the Call arm of track_inline_owned_aggregate_arg registered only the payload-walking EnumDrop (nothing at all for a heap-free enum), and unit-variant (Path) / struct-variant (StructLiteral) temps weren't matched at all. consume(Sig.A(1)) / consume(Sig.B) / consume(Sig.C{x}) where Sig: Drop leaked the drop side effect once per call; let-bound args were fine (the flagged carry-forward inside fixed B-2026-06-10). Fix mirrors the let-path: key on enum_name_of_expr over the three fresh-temp shapes (Identifier excluded — bindings own their drop) and register the karac_drop_<E> wrapper when drop_method_keys has the enum and it isn't shared; UserDrop + EnumDrop are complementary for enums (the wrapper's struct-synthesis half no-ops on enum names) so the heap-payload walk keeps its own registration. LSan-clean (Linux docker gate, fresh compile, ≥36-byte payload).

Fn-call-RETURNED Drop-typed temporary passed directly as a call argument skips the user drop body for BOTH structs and enums — consume_g(make_guard()) / consume_s(make_sig()) where make_* returns a Drop-implementing value print nothing (probe fnret.kara 2026-07-01, one silent skip per call). track_inline_owned_aggregate_arg keys on constructor SHAPES (struct literal / enum-variant Call-Path-StructLiteral); a plain fn call returning the type matches none of them (enum_name_of_expr's Call arm resolves variant ctors only). Closing it needs fn_return_type_names-driven detection PLUS passthrough-chain analysis (g(make()) where g returns its arg — registering both temps would double-drop; see fresh_arg_bare_shared_heap_type's passthrough guard for the pattern). Distinct from fixed B-2026-07-01-6 (constructor temps). FIXED dcd819d8 exactly along the sketched lines: fn_return_type_names-driven detection (a new fnret arm in track_inline_owned_aggregate_arg registering UserDrop + enum payload walk; interp twin via user_fn_return_type_name in run_fresh_temp_arg_drops) PLUS the passthrough analysis as shared syntactic ast::fn_returns_param (any bare-param return site => skip the caller-side arg-temp registration; a mint(g)->Guard returning a FRESH value keeps both drops). Probing exposed the predicted double-drop ALREADY SHIPPING: let x = pass(Guard{id:7}) fired the user body twice on BOTH surfaces (B-2026-07-01-6's ctor-temp registration + the binding drop) — the guard fixes that too. Discarded statement position (make();) covered on both surfaces as well (try_track_discarded_user_drop_temp / the interp stmt-expr discard hook). 8-probe matrix: 7 shapes output-identical across surfaces, mint count-parity, heap-carrying combined probe identical with zero leaks.

Interpreter never runs user impl Drop for value enums in ANY position — let-bound, inline temp arg, or scope exit (probe enumdrop.kara 2026-07-01: karac run prints zero drops where karac build prints one per value; struct let-bindings DO fire under the interpreter, so this is enum-specific). Additionally the interpreter misses the struct INLINE-TEMP call-arg case (interp twin of fixed-in-codegen B-2026-06-10: consume(Guard{id:7}) silent under run, fires under build). A run-vs-build A/B divergence class for RAII-style code: any kata/program observing enum Drop side effects (or struct temp-arg Drop) behaves differently across surfaces. Fix belongs in the interpreter's drop machinery (struct Drop dispatch exists; enums need the same hook, plus temp-arg ownership at call sites). FIXED d6f2e665: drop_target gained the Value::EnumVariant arm (drop_method_keys keeps builtin enums inert); both move suppressors (tail-expr, let-rebind) accept EnumVariant in tandem; run_fresh_temp_arg_drops in eval_call runs the user body on struct-literal/enum-ctor/unit-variant temp args after the call returns (the interp twin of codegen's B-2026-07-01-6 shapes; Identifier args stay with the caller binding's NLL drop, shared types with rc). 14-probe A/B: drop-COUNT parity everywhere; remaining ORDER differences are the sanctioned NLL-vs-scope-exit timing (interp implements design.md § Drop ordering NLL endpoints, codegen drops at scope exit). Joint both-surface gaps noted, no divergence: Vec[DropEnum] element user-drop bodies, and fn-RETURNED temp args (open B-2026-07-01-7).

SILENT miscompile: Stats.* over an integer slice bit-reinterpreted the i64 buffer as f64 under `karac build` — `Stats.sum(vec![3, 1, 2])` printed denormal garbage (~1.5e-323) while `karac run` printed 6. The permissive numeric coercion admitted Vec[i64] (and every other numeric element) against the baked `Slice[f64]` signatures, the interpreter value-coerced each element, and codegen's try_compile_stats_call read the raw buffer at f64 unconditionally — no error anywhere, just wrong numbers. FIX (S5, the non-f64 element axis of the reduce-kernel spike): (a) typechecker `infer_stats_call` intercept (expr_call.rs) types the whole Stats surface from the argument's element — i64 gets element-typed rules (sum/prod -> i64 CHECKED folds that trap on overflow, min/max -> Option[i64], sort -> Vec[i64], argmin/argmax/argsort exact-i64 compares, float statistics promote to f64), f64 keeps the existing table, and any OTHER numeric element (i32/u8/f32/...) is now a hard typecheck error (width-faithful evaluation on both surfaces is blocked on the interpreter width-laxity class B-2026-07-01-3); (b) the element kind is recorded in a new stats_elem_types side-table (TypeChecker -> lowering -> Program -> codegen, the channel_elem_types plumbing) so codegen reads the buffer at the right element type; (c) interpreter int-mode reads the STATIC element type from expr_types (so an EMPTY Vec[i64] still gets the integer identities sum=0/prod=1, not the float -0.0/1.0) and funnels through the new reduce_i64 in src/reduce_kernel.rs; (d) codegen int paths reuse the shared kernel emitters (emit_reduce_fold int seeds, emit_sum_f64_and_count/emit_variance_from over an i64 access, emit_reduce_minmax/argminmax int predicates, emit_sort_scratch's new SortKey::IntValue/IndexIntoInt exact-integer sorts). Exact above 2^53 on both surfaces (9007199254740993 round-trips). Tests: tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_stats_i64_full_surface / _exact_above_2_pow_53_and_empty_identities / _sum_overflow_traps; tests/interpreter.rs::test_stats_i64_* (5); tests/typechecker.rs::test_stats_i64_* + narrow-elem rejection (4); reduce_kernel unit tests (4).

Stats.* arguments MOVE the slice: `let v: Vec[f64] = ...; Stats.sum(v); Stats.mean(v)` is rejected by the ownership checker ('value v moved here, used again here') even though the runtime semantics never consume the argument (the codegen module doc explicitly documents 'the slice is a borrow' and only frees fresh-temp args). Root cause: Kara declares parameter modes on the callee (`ref` = borrow, bare = move; call sites never write `ref`), and runtime/stdlib/stats.kara declares `xs: Slice[f64]` WITHOUT `ref` — so every Stats call consumes its vec under `karac check`/`build`, making two statistics over one dataset impossible without cloning. The whole codegen test suite masks this because the run_program harness never gates on ownership errors (tests/codegen.rs run_program_capturing_inner computes ownershipcheck only to feed codegen's RC-fallback surface — test_e2e_stats_empty_slice passes v FOUR times and stays green while `karac check` rejects the same program). NOT Stats-specific in mechanism (any bare Slice/Vec param moves — that part is the language design), but the stdlib signature contradicts both the documented borrow semantics and obvious usage. FIX DIRECTION: declare the stats.kara params `ref` (audit interpreter/codegen arg handling + the fresh-temp free gate first: a `ref` param changes the ownership pass's view, and materialize_owned_temp on a fresh literal arg must still fire), or give `Slice[T]` params borrow mode by default as the type's design intent; and consider making the codegen harness surface ownership errors so the suite stops masking this class. FIXED 1fd799b7: all 13 stats.kara params declared `ref Slice[f64]`, AND the root enabler — collect_callee_param_modes/collect_method_param_modes walked only the USER program's items, so NO baked-stdlib signature's declared mode ever reached the ownership pass's call-arg classification (the whole baked surface was consume-default); both collectors now walk STDLIB_PROGRAMS first with user entries overriding. Probed: 13-fn reuse over one dataset, borrow-mutate-borrow, fresh temp args (freed via ref-rvalue materialization), i64 elements — check-clean, interp/codegen matched, zero leaks. The harness-masking half (run_program harness never gates on ownership errors, so the codegen suite stayed green on programs karac check rejects) remains a test-infra follow-up — not fixed here.

*const Foo / *mut Foo for an opaque foreign type (unsafe extern "C" { type Foo; }) incorrectly fired E_OPAQUE_TYPE_REQUIRES_INDIRECTION — the canonical C opaque-handle FFI shape (fn use_foo(p: *const Foo)) was rejected outright. The slice-1b indirection walker (lower_type_expr_inner) flipped parent_is_ref=true only through TypeKind::Ref/MutRef; when raw-pointer surface syntax later landed with the strict-provenance slices, TypeKind::Pointer still passed false — exactly the regression the phase-5 line-306 carry-forward predicted (its 'no regression risk today, raw pointers don't parse' note had gone stale; verified-all-surfaces rule caught it). Fix: Pointer arm passes parent_is_ref=true (raw pointers are sized regardless of pointee, like references); diagnostic hint gains the *const/*mut FFI spelling; generic args still reset the flag so *const Vec[Foo] (Foo by-value inside Vec) keeps rejecting. NOTE: commit message says B-2026-07-01-9 — renumbered to -11 post-merge after a parallel-agent id collision (the ledger's -9 is the Stats.* bit-reinterpret bug).

Map.get payload MOVED OUT of the borrow-bound match arm double-frees against the map's stored value — `let s = match m.get(k) { Some(x) => x, None => f"none" }; println(s)` on a plain Map[i64, String] exits 133 TODAY (probe p9_string_moveout.ka 2026-07-01; interpreter prints fine — value semantics copy). The scrutinee is borrow-classified (scrutinee_is_borrow_call), so `x` correctly registers no cleanup, but the arm-tail move hands the ALIASED bit-copy to the let binding, which tracks it as an owned String — both the binding's FreeVecBuffer and the map's drop_val=1 walk free the same buffer. Same class for struct values with the slice-3r per-value drop armed (`match m.get(k) { Some(h) => h }`). The fix is a deep-clone (or reject) when a borrow-mode pattern binding is moved out of the arm — the map retains ownership, so the escapee needs its own copy; mirrors the 3q loop-element consume-from-copy discipline but at the arm-tail move site. Pre-existing before slice 3r (not introduced by it). FIXED slice 3s (be3ddf6e): deep-clone at the borrow-mode bind when the arm moves the name (exhaustive conservative escape walker; read-only arms stay clone-free), plus if-let/while-let/let-else borrow-call flag parity (read-only if-let over Map.get crashed too) and the missing if-let then-tail move suppression (owned v.pop() tails crashed). Residual alias: destructuring payload patterns (Some((a,b)), Some(H{f})) and VecDeque payloads.

Struct-pattern destructure of Option/Result payloads (`Some(Holder { name, id })`) was wholly UNIMPLEMENTED in codegen while the interpreter handled it — every shape failed ("Undefined variable" for direct uses, "no handler for method" for method calls on a destructured field) across match/if-let/while-let, named bindings, fresh temps (v.pop()), and Map.get scrutinees. pattern_payload_word_count/pattern_payload_llvm_type defaulted a PatternKind::Struct sub-pattern to ONE word, so reconstruct_payload_value bound the raw tag-adjacent word and bind_pattern_values' plain-struct arm's StructValue guard silently no-op'd. Fix (slice 3t, 51d562bb): Struct-pattern arms in the three helpers + the 1-word binding_is_struct gate; then two OWNERSHIP legs the probes surfaced — (a) the named-binding BoxedEnumDrop inner walk double-freed CONSUMED fields against the destructured bindings' own cleanup (LLVM DCE'd the malloc/free pair when the payload was unobserved, so len()-only probes were vacuously green — observe payloads in probes), fixed by per-field cap-zero inside the box (suppress_boxed_payload_struct_destructure, all four bind sites, unbound fields stay box-owned; and (b) an ESCAPING destructured field over a Map.get borrow scrutinee aliased the bucket (exit 133), fixed by extending the 3s clone fixup to field granularity. Residual alias: tuple-payload destructure field escape over Map.get; deeper nested sub-patterns.

Option/Result STRUCT payloads as container elements leaked in both widths: Vec[Option[Holder]] (payload wider than Option's 3-word inline area → heap-BOXED per element) leaked box + interior per element (LSan-red, 336B/3 iters), and Vec[Result[Holder, i64]] (payload FITS Result's 5-word area → INLINE) leaked the struct's heap fields — the 3p/3q element-drop gates admitted only inline String/Vec {ptr,len,cap} overlay payloads. Inline struct/enum payloads needed only a gate (the emitters' in-place GEP path is layout-correct: all-i64 words overlay the LLVM aggregate); boxed payloads got a width-keyed debox branch (load w0, null-guard, inner drop, free box) in emit_option/result_drop_fn. Same fix inherits to Map values (3r selection) and deeper nesting (te_recursive_drop_fully_supported). Two interactions closed with it: for-loop element bindings over such Vecs marked as borrows (3q machinery), and moved boxed bindings (v.push(o), m.insert(k,o)) disarmed by nulling the box word — variant-agnostic, branch-safe, rides BoxedEnumDrop's null-guard. Sibling fixed in the same slice (leg A): Some((a,b)) => a over m.get(k) crashed exit 133 — the 3s escaping-borrow clone extended to tuple payload destructures and whole-tuple bindings. Residuals: whole-tuple NON-tail consume may leak its clone (crash→leak downgrade); VecDeque payloads still excluded (no clone/drop arms).

Drop-surface tail bundle (slice 3v, four legs, all probe-red): (1) VecDeque never admitted by the recursive drop/clone gates despite sharing Vec's linear {ptr,len,cap} layout — Vec[VecDeque[String]] and Vec[Option[VecDeque[String]]] leaked their strings; routed through the Vec arms at both dispatchers + the element/payload/clone gates. (2) `let s = v.get_unchecked(i)` bound a shallow element alias with an OWNED track — exit-133 double-free at the two scope exits; now deep-clones like `let s = v[i]` (clone_owned_vec_index_element get_unchecked arm). (3) The Tuple-typed BINDING payload reconstruction assumed single-word elements and insertvalue'd a raw i64 into a {ptr,i64,i64} slot — `Some(x)` binding an (String,i64) payload failed module verification from ANY source (Map.get, Vec.pop, plain bindings); rebuilt via #44's word-correct reconstruct_struct_from_words. (4) 3u's whole-tuple escape clone was untracked (non-tail consume leaked it); tracking it surfaced str-spelling sites 4-6 of the 3p trap: type_expr_has_drop_heap classified inferred-spelled tuples as HEAPLESS (every tuple-drop-synthesis consumer silently no-op'd — a wide latent class), emit_tuple_elem_drops' String arm, and its move-suppression dual zero_tuple_elem_cap_at (fixed in tandem; drop/suppress asymmetry is a double-free). Lesson standing: EVERY "String"-keyed TypeExpr gate in codegen must match both spellings; grep for '"Vec" | "VecDeque" | "String"' patterns when touching drop machinery.

Index reads and for-loops over heap-element vecs (Vec[Vec[String]], Vec[Vec[Vec[i64]]]) under-free — three sort-INDEPENDENT minimal repros (2026-07-02, scratchpad sort/w1,w2,t1c): (a) `let first = v[0]` on Vec[Vec[Vec[i64]]] leaks 16B (the index-read deep-clone's inner level not fully freed — the B-2026-06-19-6 clone + one-level tracking composition); (b) chained read `println(outer[0][0])` on Vec[Vec[String]] leaks 48B (the intermediate `outer[0]` clone's string); (c) `for row in outer { println(row.len()); }` over Vec[Vec[String]] leaks 192B = the ENTIRE content (3 strings + a row buffer) — the for-loop appears to disarm the container binding without the element drops fully covering nested content (contrast: for-over-Vec[Option[String]] is LSan-clean per the 3q suite). All shapes print correct output; macOS leaks-tool detects them (not LSan-only). Probably 2-3 distinct mechanisms sharing the nested-heap-element read path; each needs its own red-confirm + fix. Found while fixing B-2026-06-30-15 (sort itself confirmed leak-free via pop-drain and POD-element probes). FIXED 20258bd4 — and the class was MISATTRIBUTED: every repro was AUTO-PAR-ONLY (KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0 clean on all; the index reads/for-loops merely changed the statement dependency graph enough to trigger grouping, and statement ORDER toggled the leak). Two real holes in the par-group slot transfer: (1) the auto-par dispatch's parent-side re-track used one-level track_vec_var, dropping the karac_drop_Vec_String-class element drops (all nested content leaked); now mirrors the LET site's rich dispatch (heap-env/tensor/map-elem/agg-elem/one-level). (2) explicit `par { }` Step 6 registered NO Vec-slot cleanup at all (both join slots' full contents leaked per invocation); same dispatch registered. Suites never caught it because loop-based builders serialize — leak tests must include independent-lets auto-par-triggering shapes, and leak probes should compare default-build vs KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0 FIRST (instant classification). Diagnosis trail preserved in the auto-par memory note.

Cross-branch reads inside an explicit `par { }` block (a statement branch reading a sibling branch's let-binding, e.g. `par { let x = build(); let y = build(); println(x.len() + y.len()); }` in statement form) PANIC the interpreter with `internal error: entered unreachable code: variable 'x' not found ... should be caught by resolver` instead of producing a proper resolver/typechecker error. Codegen errors more gracefully ('no handler for method len on variable x') but neither surface gives the user the actual rule (per-statement branches can't read sibling bindings; use the join expression). Needs a resolver-level diagnostic for sibling-binding reads inside par statement branches. FIXED 2026-07-02 (fccdd4ab): the resolver now resolves each top-level statement of an explicit `par { }` block as a concurrent BRANCH in its own child scope (resolve_par_block — the old arm shared Seq's sequential resolve_block, which is why sibling reads sailed through). Reads and assign-targets naming a SIBLING branch's binding get a tailored diagnostic via the new par_sibling_bindings map consulted in error_undefined_name: "cannot read 'x' from a sibling `par` branch: each top-level statement in a `par { }` block is a concurrent branch with its own scope (bound at L:C); combine branch results in the block's tail expression instead". The sibling set merges across nested `par`s (an inner par inside branch 2 still cannot read branch 1's binding). The block's TAIL expression is the join point — every branch's bindings re-define into the block scope before it resolves, so tuple joins `(p, q)`, outer-binding reads inside branches, and branch-internal shadowing keep resolving. Both surfaces now emit the identical error[resolve] and refuse to execute (was: interp panic + ungraceful codegen error). Probing surfaced a SEPARATE pre-existing codegen gap at the par join — opened as B-2026-07-02-31. Regressions: tests/resolver.rs::test_par_{sibling_branch_read_rejected,sibling_branch_assign_rejected,nested_par_still_sees_outer_siblings_as_illegal,tail_expression_sees_all_branch_bindings}.

Narrow-element collection LITERALS packed i64/f64 behind a narrow-typed context at EVERY sink: the literal compilers derived the element LLVM type from the first compiled item (int literal = i64), so `let v: Vec[i32] = [10, 20, 30]`, by-value/ref/Slice call args, method args, struct fields, and return position all stored wide-packed buffers that every read reinterpreted at the narrow stride — silent wrong answers (`v[2]` read element 1's low half; iteration summed garbage; Column.from_vec's memcpy propagated it — Column[i32].sum() = 30 not 60, Column[i8] = 1 not 6; `let v: Vec[f64] = [1, 2, 3]` read back denormal garbage from int BITS in double slots). Bare `[v; n]` at a Vec-typed arg sink additionally failed module verification (stack [N x T] array vs the {ptr,i64,i64} Vec ABI). FIX (three layers): (1) typechecker check-mode re-records a collection literal admitted against a scalar-element Vec/VecDeque/ref-Vec/Slice context at its CONTEXTUAL type (record-only — acceptance semantics untouched; Slice contexts record Vec[elem] since the literal materializes as a Vec); (2) codegen literal compilers consume the width via the existing pending_let_elem_type carrier (annotated lets, Column.from_vec inline args) plus a new span-record fallback (literal_span_elem_hint over enum_inst_type_exprs), coercing each element via coerce_literal_elem_to_type (trunc/sext + the int→float sitofp leg); (3) bare repeat literals whose span record says Vec route through build_vec_filled. FOLLOW-ON leak (caught by the new ASAN test on Linux LSan only — macOS missed it via reachable stack slots, the known LSan-reachability caveat, and it PRE-EXISTED for array-literal args): by-value Vec params are caller-retains (callee emits no free — verified in IR), so a fresh collection-literal arg's heap buffer orphaned once per call; the fresh-heap materialization gates in compile_call/compile_method_call now admit collection-literal args, and collection_literal_slice_parts queues the backing-buffer free for Slice-param borrows. 19-probe A/B matrix all-match (interp / default auto-par build / KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0). Regressions: tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_narrow_literal_{vec_packs_at_annotated_width,all_sinks_pack_contextual_width}, tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_narrow_literal_arg_sink_buffers.

Out-of-range integer literals are silently admitted at narrow int contexts: `let x: i8 = 200` typechecks and both surfaces print 200 (the permissive let-coercion class — the value is stored wide); `let v: Vec[i8] = [200]` typechecks and DIVERGES — interp prints 200 (Value::Int kept wide) while build prints -56 (B-2026-07-02-6's literal packing truncates into the honest i8 buffer; pre-fix build read the wide buffer's low byte, also -56, so the divergence is long-standing, not introduced). `let a: Array[i8, 1] = [200]` prints 200 on both (array elements stored wide — its own width dishonesty). FIX DIRECTION: reject integer literals that do not fit the contextual narrow int range at check-mode (scalar and collection-element positions alike — the Rust literal-out-of-range rule); that also makes the 200-vs--56 divergence unrepresentable. Related: the numeric-coercion-permissive class (let-binding coercion widens AND narrows i64<->f64). FIXED 2026-07-02 (d984ddfc): typechecker literal-range validation at three sites sharing check_int_literal_fits (i128 math) — (1) SUFFIXED literals validate against their own suffix at synthesis (`300u8` errors anywhere; negated `-128i8` validates the NEGATED value in the Unary arm and span-suppresses the positive-operand check so bare `128i8` stays an error); (2) UNSUFFIXED bare/negated literals validate against the contextual narrow type in check-mode (annotated lets, fn args, struct fields, returns; `ref T` peels); (3) the B-2026-07-02-6 Vec-context collection-literal arm validates direct integer-literal elements (that arm synthesizes elements so site 2 never sees them; Array-expected literals get it free via per-element check_expr). Negative-into-unsigned gets a dedicated message — `let x: u8 = -1` was the worst divergence (run -1 vs build 18446744073709551615); `f(70000)` vs an i16 param, suffixed `300u8`, struct fields, repeat values, and return position all also silently diverged pre-fix. Probed 18 shapes: all rejected identically on both surfaces now; boundary values (255u8/-128i8/i16-i32 extremes/large u64) locked green by test. RESIDUALS: match PATTERN literals (`200` against an i8 scrutinee — consistent unreachable-arm behavior on both surfaces, no divergence) and generic-inference sinks (`Column.from_vec([300])` with Column[u8] — the literal unifies against a metavar before solving; still diverges 300 vs 44 via honest narrow packing; needs post-solve literal validation). `-9223372036854775808` stays a consistent pre-existing parse error (no unary-minus lexer special-case). Regressions: tests/typechecker.rs::test_int_literal_{out_of_range_rejected_at_narrow_contexts,boundaries_still_admitted}.

SILENT data corruption in DEFAULT auto-par builds: `Vec.sort()`/`sort_by`/`sort_by_key`/`reverse`/`pop`/`remove` were invisible to the auto-parallelizer's write-dependency gate — `method_effects_imply_receiver_mutation` (src/concurrency.rs) found no `.{method}` effect key because the effectchecker's builtin seed table only seeded the push/insert families. A `v.sort()` co-grouped with a later read of `v` raced the runtime's permute-back against the read (NON-DETERMINISTIC duplicated/lost elements: a 2-element Vec[String] section printed `omega,omega` on ~25% of runs; 9 distinct outputs over 25 runs of a 4-section program); `v.pop()`/`v.remove(i)` followed by `v.len()` read a stale pre-mutation {ptr,len,cap} branch-capture copy 100% DETERMINISTICALLY (printed the un-popped len every run). Distinct from B-2026-07-02-4 (par-group slot CLEANUP leaks) — this is missed SERIALIZATION; same class as B-2026-06-20-16 (map entry writes) and the VecDeque.push/pop seeds from the Map+VecDeque coexistence fix. FIX: seed the six method keys `allocates(Heap)` in the builtin table — accurate for the reorder family (runtime sort paths allocate index/scratch buffers, `karac_vec_reverse` a tmp element), over-approximate for pop/remove matching the VecDeque.pop_* precedent. The gate matches on the `.{method}` name suffix, so the `Vec.`-qualified seeds also serialize `Map.remove`/`Set.remove`/VecDeque `.pop()`. RESIDUAL: any future builtin mutating method must be seeded or it silently races — `clear`/`insert`/`truncate`/`dedup`/`retain` are not yet in codegen (honest 'not yet supported' errors) so nothing else is exposed today; when one lands, seed it in the same block. E2E regression test drives all three shapes under the default auto-par build (tests/par_codegen.rs test_e2e_auto_par_sort_and_pop_serialize_against_reads).

`String.cmp(other)` on a NON-identifier receiver — a string LITERAL (`"abd".cmp("abc")`) or an INDEX into a Vec[String] (`v[0].cmp(v[1])`) — typechecked and ran correctly but FAILED `karac build`: `codegen failed: Vec/String method 'cmp' is not yet supported in codegen`. A run/build divergence, and an incomplete-fix follow-on to B-2026-06-30-13 (which shipped String.cmp codegen but guarded the branch on `inferred_receiver_type(object) == Some("String")`, a lookup that resolves only NAMED receivers via var_type_names — a literal / index / field receiver has no var-name, so it fell through to the loud catch-all). Surfaced by a post-reinstall smoke test using `("abd").cmp("abc")`. FIX (3c8cd55b): guard the String branch on the operand LAYOUT (`l.get_type() == vec_struct_type()`, the String {ptr,len,cap} header) instead of the receiver name. Sound because the typechecker admits `.cmp` only on int/char/bool/String; int/char/bool compile to IntValue (handled by the earlier arm), floats are excluded from Ord (NaN), and user-struct `.cmp` is typecheck-rejected — so ANY {ptr,len,cap}-shaped struct pair reaching this arm is a String, for every receiver shape (identifier/local/param already worked; literal + index now join them). Test: tests/codegen.rs::e2e_string_cmp_nonident_receiver_codegen; e2e_string_cmp_codegen still green. fmt + clippy --all --all-targets --features llvm clean.

Explicit closure capture-mode prefix (`own`/`ref`/`mut ref`, design.md Rule 2½) was IGNORED by the closure-escape check: `own |x| x + k` returned from a function still fired E0508 RefCaptureEscapesScope, even though an own-capture escape is the design-blessed CLEAN escape (Rule 2 sub-case (i) — captures move into the closure, no borrow to outlive). Root cause: the Rule 2½ prefix pinning (disjoint-capture slice 5) was applied to the per-PATH map `closure_capture_path_modes` but never to the per-NAME `closure_captures` map, which is the one `check_closure_ref_capture_escapes` reads — so the escape check always saw the body-usage-inferred Ref mode. Meant there was NO check-clean spelling for an escaping capturing closure at all (`karac check` rejected the entire shipped heap-env feature surface in every spelling, while build/run execute it correctly). FIXED e092e041: pin `captures` to the declared mode before the insert (ownership/expr_check.rs), mirroring the existing path-map pinning. Bare (inferred) ref-capture escapes still reject per Rule 2 sub-case (iv). Surfaced by the new E2E ownership gate (this slice) flushing 88 heap_env_* tests; probed via own_probe.kara.

`println(s); println(s)` (or any later use of a printed heap value: `println(c); println(c.len())`) was rejected by `karac check` as a use-after-move — printing CONSUMED its argument. Root cause: the print family (print/println/eprint/eprintln) are typechecker/codegen intrinsics with no declared signature in the user program OR the baked stdlib, so collect_callee_param_modes had no entry and call args fell through to the conservative consume-everything default. Contradicts design.md § Display ('borrows the receiver — the same value can appear in multiple {} slots without being consumed') and both execution surfaces (interp + codegen never consume the arg). Same class as B-2026-07-01-10 (Stats bare-Slice params) — a read-only stdlib surface whose signature/classification says consume. FIXED e092e041: intrinsic Ref-mode entries for the four print fns inserted at the head of collect_callee_param_modes (stdlib/user definitions still override), plus `s: ref String` on the baked Stdout/Stderr print/println methods in runtime/stdlib/io.kara. Cured ~20 E2E reds (string push/concat/display/shadow corpus). Full llvm suites + LSan-lane green — no RC-fallback or codegen behavior shift observed.

`for x in v { .. } println(v.len())` was rejected by `karac check` as a use-after-move of `v` ('moved' at the for-loop header). Root cause: use_classifier.rs walked the For iterable in Mode::Consuming, while design.md § Iterable is explicit — 'The collection is always borrowed — `for` never consumes it. To consume a collection while iterating, call .into_iter() explicitly.' The legacy state-machine walker (ownership/expr_check.rs) already walked it as reading; only the predicate-pipeline classifier consumed, so the UAM predicate fired. FIXED e092e041: For iterable walks Mode::Reading; a consuming iterable sub-expression (v.into_iter()) still classifies itself via the method-receiver walk. Cured the vec_macro_literal/vec_repeat_literal/unsigned_vec_elem/for_loop_string_elem E2E reds.

Comparison operators CONSUME their operands under `karac check`: `if a == b {..} if a == c {..}` on String flags `a` as moved at the first comparison; `a < b` / `a > b` same; and `==` on a user struct consumes the operands EVEN WHEN the resolved PartialEq/Eq impls declare `ref self, other: ref Self`. Both use_classifier.rs and ownership/expr_check.rs walk ExprKind::Binary operands as reads, so the consume comes from the lowered/dispatched eq path (typechecker method lowering or the desugared eq call's receiver/arg classification) — not the Binary arm itself; root not yet isolated. Design is unambiguous (partial_eq.kara: `fn eq(ref self, other: ref Self)`; comparisons borrow). Hits completely natural code — the http_server serve-handler tests iterate headers with `for (k, v) in hdrs { if k == "x-test-echo" {..} if k == "host" {..} }` and the second `k ==` is a use-after-move. Runtime semantics unaffected (interp + codegen never consume; `karac build`/`run` execute correctly) — check-surface only. Grandfathered: test_e2e_string_equality, test_e2e_string_ordering, test_e2e_user_impl_eq_drives_equality, test_server_serve_handler_iterates_headers, test_server_serve_handler_reads_query_param (tests/common/mod.rs OWNERSHIP_GATE_GRANDFATHERED — remove on fix).

Binding a NAMED FUNCTION as a value treats the fn item as affine: `let g = doubler; let h = doubler;` flags `doubler` moved at the first let and use-after-move at the second; likewise `apply(doubler, 10); apply(doubler, 11)` (two call-arg uses). A fn item has no captures and is a plain code pointer — freely duplicable per design.md § First-Class Functions ('User.validate is an unbound function value — no capture'; only closures capturing owned non-Copy values are once-callable). Check-surface only (build/run fine). Grandfathered: fn_value_let_bound_named_fn_passed_and_called, fn_value_named_fn_reused_across_call_sites (remove on fix).

A field- or tuple-path USE consumes the WHOLE root binding: `Add(b) => eval(b.left) + eval(b.right)` flags `b` moved-whole at the first field-path call arg (b.right is then a use-after-move); `match t.0 {..} match t.1 {..}` over a tuple does the same for projections; struct-field iterables (`for a in c.args` then c.callee) likewise. No partial-move tracking for direct place paths — call-arg and match-scrutinee place classification keys the root binding — contradicting the disjoint-place philosophy the borrow checker and closure disjoint-capture (Rule 2¼) already implement. Dominant red class in the ASAN shared-enum drop corpus (match-arm payload struct `b` with two+ Expr fields consumed field-by-field — the natural recursive-eval shape every tree-walking program uses). Check-surface only (build/run + drop behavior correct; the corpus pins that). Grandfathered (remove on fix): test_e2e_struct_wrapped_recursive_shared_enum_tree, test_e2e_vec_of_struct_with_shared_and_option_field, asan_collect_all_heterogeneous_tuple_no_uaf, asan_match_byvalue_shared_enum_* (3), asan_shared_enum_recursive_struct_payload_string_freed_no_leak, asan_struct_nested_enum_leaf/with_direct_enum_field (2), asan_struct_wrapped_* (6), asan_vec_of_shared_enum_elements_freed_no_leak.

`with_provider[Metric](p, || ..); println(p.n)` is rejected — the provider VALUE arg is classified as consumed, so the post-pop use of `p` is a use-after-move. But the documented semantics of the with_provider E2E pin is exactly 'mut ref self mutation VISIBLE AFTER pop' — the value is borrowed by the provider frame, not consumed. with_provider is a typechecker special form whose generic call shape does not route through collect_callee_param_modes (an intrinsic [Ref, Own] entry for the bare name was added in e092e041 but does not match the `with_provider[Effect](..)` callee form), so the args fall to the consume default. Fix direction: classify the special form's value slot as a borrow where its call is lowered/classified. Grandfathered: test_with_provider_e2e_mut_ref_self_mutation_visible_after_pop (remove on fix).

A `ref Column[i64]` parameter SILENTLY produces no output under codegen: `fn fst(c: ref Column[i64], i: i64) -> i64 { match c[i] { Some(v) => v, None => -999 } }` called as fst(a, 0) — `karac check` clean, interpreter prints the right values, but the AOT binary prints NOTHING and exits 0 (probed refcol_probe.kara on clean main d811565c via the primary llvm karac — pre-existing, not introduced by the gate slice). Bare (consuming) Column params work on both surfaces; only the ref form mis-lowers, presumably the builtin-container method/index paths don't deref the ref param (same family as the receiver-width/ref-param deref gotchas in the Column/DataFrame codegen notes). Surfaced attempting the check-clean rewrite of test_e2e_column_3vl_arithmetic (helper reuse over Columns requires ref params after B-2026-07-01-10's precedent). The test keeps bare params and is grandfathered past the ownership gate until this lands (remove on fix).

`ref Slice[i64]` + `unsafe { xs.get_unchecked(i) }` MISCOMPILES: the AOT binary prints the slice header words instead of elements (probed refslice_probe.kara: expected 10/20/30, got 4340063264/3/4 = data-pointer/len/garbage) — get_unchecked indexes the {ptr,len,cap} struct itself rather than dereferencing the ref param to reach the buffer. `karac check` clean, interpreter correct; pre-existing on clean main d811565c (probed via the primary llvm karac). Plain `xs[i]` on `ref Slice` params is fine (Stats corpus, B-2026-07-01-10) — the unsafe get_unchecked lowering is missing the ref-param deref. Surfaced attempting the check-clean rewrite of test_e2e_slice_get_unchecked_in_bounds_returns_element; that test keeps the bare Slice param and is grandfathered past the ownership gate until this lands (remove on fix).

Struct method named like a seq builtin (first/last/get_unchecked) silently returned Unit — try_eval_seq_method receiver-shape arms swallowed non-seq receivers instead of falling through to the impl-block path; b.first() on a trait impl printed (). Fixed: try_eval_impl_method extracted and called early for Struct/SharedStruct receivers + the three swallow arms return None. Residual: user ENUM receivers with builtin-named methods still reach the seq arms.

Generic-mono param surface unimplemented: a Fn(...)-typed param was never in closure_fn_types so f(x) fell to the unknown-callee const-0 placeholder (apply[T](x,f)->T silently returned 0 under build); for-loops over Vec params silently compiled to NOTHING; xs.len() failed loudly. Fixed: mono prologue mirrors compile_function's param registration (closure_abi_fn_type, register_var_from_type_expr under active type_subst, owned_vecstr_params, unconditional ref_params) + the direct-call site passes pointers for ref-mode args.

Un-annotated closure param vs declared-Fn ABI mismatch: params fell back to i64 (pending_closure_param_hints was never set anywhere), so |a| f"{a}!" against Fn(String)->String compiled as (ptr,i64)->i64 while call sites dispatched the declared-Fn ABI — String's pointer word printed as an integer. Fixed: typechecker records the closure literal's resolved Fn type at its span (flows via fn_value_typed_exprs); compile_closure types un-annotated params from it and registers String/Vec params in the semantic side-tables.

let-annotation elem hint leaked into call-argument collection literals: pending_let_elem_type covers the whole RHS (needed by Vec.with_capacity lowering) and used to beat the literal's own span-recorded type, so let s: String = tail_str(vec![100,200,300]) packed the arg elements as i8 and printed 100100100. No generics involved. Fixed: literal_span_elem_hint's per-span type (enum_inst_type_exprs) now wins; span-less literals still fall through to the ambient hint.

Browser playground 100% broken: run_on_interp_thread (the Windows fat-stack fix) lifted EVERY interpreter run onto a fresh 16MB spawn_scoped thread; wasm32-unknown-unknown spawn returns Err(Unsupported) and the .expect trapped every playground run() — even fn main(){println("ctrl")} — as 'failed to spawn interpreter thread'. Found by the A2a-2.2-fu headless-Chrome probe (phase-5-diagnostics line 642); the compile-only check predating the fat-stack fix couldn't see it. Fixed: cfg(target_arch=wasm32) variant runs the closure inline on the caller's stack.

Second universal playground trap + the program-triggered wasm trap class: Interpreter::new seeded xorshift via SystemTime::now(), which panics 'time not implemented' on wasm32 — every run() still trapped after B-2026-07-02-29's fix. Fixed with a fixed golden-ratio seed (deterministic playground RandomSource). Same slice guarded the rest of the sys/unsupported class reachable from playground programs: sleep_ms thread::sleep → no-op pause (arg still evaluated), par{} thread::scope → sequential_mode defaults true on wasm (FIFO-deterministic, matches the seq_scheduler wasm codegen story), Clock.now + RateLimiter.try_acquire → runtime diagnostics instead of module traps. Verified in headless Chrome: control/sleep/par/clock probes green.

Explicit `par { }` blocks whose branches read an OUTER (pre-par) binding, or whose branch RHS contains a nested block expression, fail codegen at the join with 'Undefined variable' while `karac run` executes them correctly — a loud run-vs-build divergence (build refuses; no miscompiled output). Repros (2026-07-02, scratchpad pardiag/p4,p7 — identical on clean main, so pre-existing, surfaced while fixing B-2026-07-02-5): (a) `let base = 100; let total = par { let x = base + 1; let y = base + 2; x + y };` -> build: codegen failed: Undefined variable 'x' (run prints 203); (b) `let t = par { let x = 1; let y = { let x = 10; x + 1 }; x + y };` -> Undefined variable 'y' (run prints 12). The plain shapes work (tuple join `(p, q)` of bare-call branches and a direct `x.len() + y.len()` tail both build), so the explicit-par lowering loses branch bindings only when a branch body is more than a bare call chain — likely the branch classifier / return-slot collection (compute_return_slots in par_blocks.rs) drops branches it cannot slot and the tail then references a binding no slot carries. FIX DIRECTION: make the explicit-par lowering's binding collection cover arbitrary branch statement shapes, or serialize unsupported branches (with the design-mandated note[par_serialized]) instead of failing the join.

Every use of the fully-wired #[profile(P1, ...)] attribute emitted a bogus error[E_UNKNOWN_ATTRIBUTE] alongside the real profile diagnostics: 'profile' was never added to RECOGNIZED_BARE_ATTRIBUTES in src/attribute_validator.rs when the attribute shipped 2026-05-18 (parser scan_profile_attr -> Function.profile_compat, resolver placement/name validation, effectchecker/profile_compat.rs enforcement; phase-5-diagnostics.md line 510) — so karac check failed even on profile-compatible functions and the attribute was unusable under the check gate. Repro: #[profile(embedded)] fn f() -> Vec[i64] { Vec.new() } -> both the correct E_PROFILE_INCOMPATIBLE_EFFECT and the bogus unknown-attribute error. Fixed: allowlist entry (comment points at the checklist item), attr_slice2_profile_attribute_recognised regression pin, and the missing appendix-d book entry (A/B-verified on run + llvm build).

Interpreter had no ExprKind::OffsetOf arm: karac run panicked 'not yet implemented: unhandled expr' on any offset_of[T](field.path) while karac build printed the offset (typechecker+codegen shipped, interp never got the arm). Fixed with an AST-driven layout engine (src/interpreter/layout_query.rs) mirroring llvm_type_for_type_expr/llvm_type_for_name arm-for-arm: natural-alignment struct walks, String/Vec 24-byte {ptr,len,cap}, Slice 16, opaque heap handles ptr, enums {i64 tag, i64 x payload-words}, shared types ptr, Array/Vector/Atomic/Mutex/tuple/union, seeded builtin enum layouts, unknown-name i64 fall-through; nested paths chain like compile_offset_of. A/B byte-identical run-vs-build on 30 probes (mixed-alignment padding, heap/enum fields, 3-level nesting). eval_expr's match is now exhaustive — a future ExprKind fails at compile time, not via runtime todo!.

Sibling gap found probing B-2026-07-02-33: size_of[T]()/align_of[T]() had no interpreter intercept — the Call(Index(Ident,T)) parse shape fell through to variable lookup and panicked 'internal error: variable size_of not found; should be caught by resolver' under karac run (build compiled fine, printed 16/8). Fixed via the same layout engine (eval_layout_query = alloc size / ABI alignment of the lowered type, mirroring compile_layout_query_intrinsic) intercepted in eval_call ahead of normal dispatch, both parse shapes. The typecheck-invalid nested-generic shape size_of[Vec[i64]]() (E_LAYOUT_QUERY_TYPE_ARG_REQUIRED) now degrades to a clean runtime error under run's tolerate-typecheck-errors design instead of panicking. A/B identical on 13 probes.

A read-only owned bare-`T` param reused across call sites is flagged use-after-move by `karac check` (`fn peek(s: Span) -> i64 { s.off }` then `let x = peek(r); let y = peek(r);`). RESOLVED as correct-by-design, NOT a checker change: per design.md a bare-`T` param is OWNED (the caller loses ownership) and ownership modes are DECLARED at the signature, not inferred — so the second `peek(r)` genuinely is a move-after-move and the diagnostic is right. The two shared-enum-drop asan corpus programs were the defect: their read-only `peek` helper now takes `ref` (it only reads a field), making the reuse a legal borrow; both un-grandfathered and pass the strict ownership gate (runtime drop behavior unchanged). Considered but rejected: whole-program inferred-`ref`-mode call-arg classification — that would contradict design.md's declared-modes rule.

Same-scope shadowing re-`let` shared the CFG binding identity of the binding it shadows: after `let x = "..."; let y = x; let x = 99;`, reading the NEW x (`println(x)`) was flagged 'value x moved here, used again here' — karac check false-rejected legal rebind-after-move (UseKind::Define is deliberately not a reassign-kill, so the old binding's consume paired with the new binding's reads), and sibling-scope name reuse could pair as a spurious RC witness. The REPL hard-failed the same shape since its cell pipeline gates on ownershipcheck (surfaced by B-2026-07-02-37's post-shadow replay). Fixed with per-shadow alpha-rename frames in the CFG builder (@shN — same device as @armN/@forN/@cuN, stripped by demangle_binding): a let/let-uninit/let-else that re-binds a visible name (params seeded via build_cfg_with_classification's new param_names arg; every introduction tracked in seen_names) renames subsequent uses until the ENCLOSING block's lowering completes (lower_block truncates the rename stack after fall-through defer emission), so an inner-block shadow pops at block end and a genuine use-after-move of the outer binding after the block still fires.

REPL sessions deterministically bricked by re-binding a persistent let: `let x = 99;` after `let x = "...";` pruned the earlier binder's replay slice (prune_shadowed_lets) while later persistent lets (`let y = x;`) — or the re-binding cell's own RHS (`let x = x + 1;`) — still referenced it, so every cell from the re-bind onward failed 'resolve error: undefined name x' (identical with/without KARAC_REPL_JIT; the failed cell's new binder was never captured, so the session never recovered). Fixed: prune_shadowed_lets -> clear_rebound_let_snapshots — ALL binders stay in persistent_lets (same-scope shadowing is legal Kara; the replay now matches the :save export program exactly) and only the stale value snapshot clears; the interpreter's snapshot override map re-keyed from binding NAME to the binder-pattern SPAN of each name's LAST top-level let (install_let_snapshot_overrides, shared by the cell pipeline and %show) so earlier shadows re-run their recorded RHS in submission order and pattern lets replayed between two binders read historically correct values; the lljit prototype's snapshot classification got the same last-binder gate (an earlier binder must not load the final — possibly differently-typed — global mid-sequence). Depends on B-2026-07-02-36: the post-shadow synthetic is exactly the rebind-after-move shape that predicate false-flagged.

Residual of B-2026-07-02-31: an explicit `par { }` branch whose `let` RHS is a METHOD CALL (`let x = base.abs()`) or an INDEX (`let x = v[0]`) still fails codegen at the join with 'Undefined variable' while `karac run` executes correctly (same run-vs-build divergence; build refuses, no miscompiled output). B-31 (24b1c9f4/ec5c9f20) made `infer_expr_llvm_type` (src/codegen/stmts.rs) cover lowered operator dispatch, block/if/match-expr RHS, and identifier/literal/bare-call RHS, but it conservatively returns None for MethodCall/Index/FieldAccess — sizing those needs impl-return-type / Vec-element-type lookup at that point. Fix direction: extend infer_expr_llvm_type to resolve method-return and index-element types (or thread the typechecker's inferred type through to the par return-slot sizer). Spawned as background task task_6b663d84.

Generic monos leaked every non-tensor name-keyed var side-table across nested compiles AND same-LLVM-shape handle instantiations shared one mono. Only tensor_var_infos was save/restored around compile_mono_function, so after B-2026-07-02-11's full-registration prologue mono #1's 'c -> Column[i64]' entry survived into mono #2 where 'c' was a Tensor param — the Column intercept compiled a column reduce over a tensor handle (SIGSEGV; probed report[C: MyReduce[i64]] instantiated at Column then Tensor). Independently, infer_type_args sees only LLVM value types (a Column and a Tensor arg are both `ptr`) so the two instantiations mangled to the SAME symbol and shared one body. Fixed: SavedVarSideTables swaps all 17 name-keyed tables around both mono wrappers (+ column/tensor/dataframe_var_infos join compile_function's per-fn clear — the same latent leak existed across top-level fns); mono_handle_param_infos threads the arg spans' column/tensor_typed_exprs records into a mangle axis ($c_col_i64 / $c_ten_i64_4) and the mono prologue's registration.

Bound trait-args never substituted in method dispatch through a generic bound: fn grand[C: MyReduce[i64]](c: ref C) -> i64 { c.total() } failed check with "expected 'i64', found 'T'" — dispatch_trait_assoc_fn substituted only Self, so a parameterized bound's method signature kept the trait's raw TypeParam. Fixed: trait_bound_arg_subs binds the bound's generic args to the trait's declared params (find_trait_def: user program then baked stdlib; args lowered in the enclosing generic scope so C: MyReduce[U] works) and dispatch_trait_assoc_fn seeds them into subs + scope. Prerequisite for the S6a stdlib Reduce[T] trait.

Vec[T]-param generic monos never bind T — two element-type instantiations SHARE one mono and the second silently returns garbage under `karac build`: fn head[T](v: ref Vec[T]) -> T { v[0] } called with Vec[i64] then Vec[f64] prints 7 then 4612811918334230528 (2.5's f64 bits as i64); interpreter correct. unify_type_expr only binds BARE Path(T) params against the whole LLVM type (the container-generic-args arm is a TODO) and a Vec's {ptr,len,cap} LLVM shape is element-erased, so the subst has no T entry, the mangle gets no suffix, and the prologue's register_var_from_type_expr falls back to i64 elems. Same family as the S6a handle-instantiation collision (fixed via mono_handle_param_infos, B-2026-07-02-39) but needs general TypeExpr-level mono type args — an S6b prerequisite (trait-method monomorphization needs the same substitution machinery). Probed p8_vec_elem_mono_collision.kara.

FIXED (ee1fd78e): a PARAMETERIZED bound never compared its trait ARGS against the matched impl. `fn spread[C: Reduce[i64]](c: ref C)` called with a Column[f64] (which impls Reduce[f64], NOT Reduce[i64]) PASSED `karac check` (bound satisfaction matched the trait NAME only), ran with silently wrong types (a double result flowing through the i64-typed signature), and only codegen module verification died loud (ret double vs i64). FIX: ImplInfo now records the impl's trait_args (the [T] in `impl[T] Reduce[T] for Column[T]`) plus target_generic_args (the target's args WITH params, since target_args collapses to empty for a generic impl); discharge_type_bounds (src/typechecker/exprs.rs), on a bound carrying generic args, computes the impl's CONCRETE trait args via env.impl_concrete_trait_args (structurally bind the impl params from the receiver's args, substitute through trait_args) and compares them against the bound's requested args (lowered with the fn's params in scope + solved). Rejects only a fully-concrete-vs-fully-concrete mismatch; an unsolved/still-parametric arg on either side is skipped so `C: Reduce[T]` and a matching Reduce[i64] are never false-rejected. Message: 'Column<f64> implements Reduce[f64], not Reduce[i64]'. Tests: typechecker parameterized_trait_bound_args_checked_against_impl + parameterized_trait_bound_matching_accepted. Probed p10_bound_arg_mismatch.kara during S6a.

f64.to_bits()/to_bits32() and the inverse i64.bits_as_f64()/bits_as_f32() had an interpreter + typechecker implementation but NO codegen arm, so a program that round-tripped an f64 through its bit pattern ran under `karac run` yet failed `karac build` with "no handler for method 'to_bits'" — a run/build divergence. Surfaced by the LeetCode #50 Pow(x, n) benchmark, whose diffable cross-language sink sum-folds each result's raw f64 bits into an integer. Fixed: lowered to pure LLVM bitcasts (no runtime helper, no allocation, no new C symbol) — to_bits bitcast f64->i64; to_bits32 fptrunc->f32, bitcast->i32, zext->i64; bits_as_* width-normalize the receiver then bitcast to the float. Mirrors the interpreter arm byte-for-byte incl. the sign-bit-only -0.0 pattern. Regression: e2e_float_to_bits_codegen.

A method declared `-> Self` was broken end-to-end under `karac build` for EVERY form (inherent method, trait impl, static constructor); `karac run` masked it by executing past the typecheck warning, and selfhost/examples never exercise `-> Self` under build (0 occurrences), so the gap was latent. Three legs: (1) TYPECHECK — a `Self` return-type annotation lowers to `Named{"Self"}` (or `TypeParam("Self")` when "Self" is an in-scope generic), and the body's concrete tail type is not assignable to it, so `check_function` rejected valid code with "expected 'Self', found 'W'". (2) CODEGEN PROTOTYPE — `llvm_return_type` hit the unknown-name `i64` fall-through for `Self`, so a struct-returning `-> Self` fn's LLVM type mismatched its aggregate `ret` (module-verify hard failure). (3) CALL-SITE TYPING — the value-receiver method-call return type substituted only the impl's generics, leaving `Self` unresolved, so `a.m()` typed as `Self` and downstream field reads returned 0 (silent miscompile). FIXED (f6e35b1c): items.rs `resolve_self_in_type` resolves `Self` in the lowered return type to the concrete impl target inside `check_function` (identity for a trait default body); helpers.rs `rewrite_self_in_type_expr` rewrites `Self` in the synthesized impl-method's return type before prototype build; expr_method_call.rs resolves `Self` in the method-call return type + params to the concrete receiver. Tests: typechecker `method_self_return_type_resolves_to_impl_target`, codegen e2e `e2e_method_self_return_struct_lowers_and_reads_fields`. Suites green: typechecker 1859 / interpreter 1082 / codegen 1978. Surfaced probing narrow-width x trait machinery. Two follow-ons it EXPOSED are tracked separately: B-2026-07-03-3 (chained aggregate-return field read) and B-2026-07-03-4 (auto-par static `-> Self` result).

PRE-EXISTING codegen miscompile (independent of Self; surfaced while fixing B-2026-07-03-2): a chained `expr.method().field` where the method returns an AGGREGATE (struct) reads the wrong value (0) under `karac build`, while the let-bound form `let b = expr.method(); b.field` is correct, and `karac run` (interp) is correct. Confirmed with a CONCRETE return type (`impl W { fn m(self) -> W { W{v:self.v+1} } }`; `a.m().v` -> 0 vs bound -> 2), so it is not `Self`-specific. A SCALAR (i64) chained return is fine (`a.inc()` where inc -> i64 works), so the bug is specific to a method-call temporary of aggregate shape consumed immediately by field access — the temporary's struct slot is not materialized/addressed before the field GEP. Repro: struct W{v:i64}; impl W{fn m(self)->W{W{v:self.v+1}}}; fn main(){let a=W{v:1}; println(a.m().v);} -> build prints 0, run prints 2. Workaround: bind the call result to a local first. Fix likely in the method-call-result field-access path (materialize the aggregate temp to an alloca before the field offset load). FIXED (839beaea): added MethodCall/Call arms to type_name_of_expr (src/codegen/expr_ops.rs) resolving a call's static return-type name via fn_return_type_names (impl methods keyed by qualified Type.method; free/2-seg-Path calls by callee), so field_index_for locates the field and compile_field_access emits an extractvalue instead of the i64 0 placeholder. Receiver resolved recursively (deeper chains a.m().n, mk(x).field work); non-struct returns degrade to prior behavior. Tests: codegen e2e e2e_chained_call_struct_field_access (scalar/heap-String/multi-field/double-chain/free-fn), memory_sanitizer asan_chained_call_struct_heap_field_no_leak (heap-field temp loop; Linux ASan+LSan clean, macOS ASan clean). codegen 1985+, all suites green; fmt+clippy clean.

FIXED (db020ee6, side effect of B-2026-07-03-11): Auto-par-only miscompile newly reachable after B-2026-07-03-2 enabled `-> Self` builds: a STATIC associated fn returning `-> Self`, bound to a local and then used as a method receiver, produces the wrong value (0) under the DEFAULT auto-par build, while the sequential build (KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0) and interp are correct — and `--concurrency-report` says 'no parallelization opportunities detected', so the divergence is from an auto-par-path codegen decision, NOT from forming a par group. Tightly scoped: the INSTANCE `-> Self` result feeding a method is fine under auto-par, and a CONCRETE static return (`make() -> W`) feeding a method is fine — only the static `make() -> Self` source triggers it. Repro: struct W{v:i64}; trait Dbl{fn twice(self)->Self;} impl W{fn make()->Self{W{v:7}}} impl Dbl for W{fn twice(self)->Self{W{v:self.v+self.v}}} fn main(){let c=W.make(); let d=c.twice(); println(d.v);} -> auto-par build 0, seq build 14, run 14. Likely the auto-par path re-derives the static-assoc `-> Self` call result type from the original (unrewritten, `Self`) method AST rather than the concrete target, mis-materializing the binding's slot. Guarded out of the B-2026-07-03-2 e2e regression test (twice() is called on a literal-bound receiver there). FIX ATTRIBUTION: the binding `let c = W.make()` (static assoc `-> Self`) mis-materialized its slot via the HashMap-order struct reverse-lookup in stmts.rs; B-11 (db020ee6) hardened that path (record_var_type_name central name-subst resolution + a MethodCall ast_hint arm resolving via type_name_of_expr), which also corrected this static-Call binding. Verified: repro yields 0 at db020ee6^ (8a11959d) and 14 at db020ee6 through concurrency_analyze. Regression guard: test_e2e_auto_par_static_self_assoc_fn_receiver (par_codegen.rs). NOTE: trigger is KARAC_AUTO_PAR=1 (or the concurrency_analyze compile path), not the plain default build.

User-defined trait impls on PRIMITIVE integer types are unsupported end-to-end despite being an intended feature (design.md shows `impl Pod for u8`, `impl From[i32] for i64`; env_build.rs references `impl Eq for u8`; a typechecker test asserts `i64` would satisfy a bound if `impl Tr for i64` existed). For `impl Tr for <int> { fn m(self) -> ... { self + ... } }`: (1) `check_impl_block` builds the self type as `Type::Named{name:"u8"}` by hand (items.rs ~1808) instead of lowering the target, so inside the method body `self` is not recognized as numeric — `self + self` errors "arithmetic operator requires numeric type, found 'u8'" for EVERY width incl i64; (2) the method is never registered on the primitive — the call site errors "no method 'm' on type 'u8'" (and a generic bound `fn f[T: Tr](x:T)` reports "`u8` does not implement `Tr`"). Under `karac run` only `impl Tr for i64` accidentally dispatches (runtime int values are i64-typed, matching the i64-keyed impl); every narrow width (u8/u16/u32/i8/i16/i32) AND u64 fail at runtime with "method 'm' not found on type 'i64' (no interpreter dispatch arm)" because the value's runtime type is width-erased to i64 while the impl is keyed on the declared narrow width. Under `karac build` all widths fail at typecheck. Repro: trait Dbl{fn dbl(self)->Self;} impl Dbl for u8{fn dbl(self)->Self{self+self}} fn main(){let a:u8=10; println(a.dbl());}. Fix spans typecheck (lower the impl target so a primitive self is numeric + register methods for primitive impl targets) + interp dispatch (key user primitive-impl dispatch on the declared width, not the erased i64). Depends on B-2026-07-03-2's `Self`-return resolution for the `-> Self` shape. FIX ae7c9525 (DIRECT-call scope): (1) check_impl_block lowers the target and uses the lowered primitive Type as self_type so the body's self is numeric; (2) env_add_impl registers a primitive target under its canonical name (method_callee_type_name) instead of the `_ => return` fall-through; (3) the typecheck method-call arm routes a numeric-primitive receiver with a real impl candidate into normal impl-table dispatch (was poisoning to Type::Error), and the interpreter recovers the DECLARED receiver type from method_callee_types (value type is width-erased to i64/f64). Verified interp/seq/auto-par across all 8 scalar widths with distinguishing per-width impls incl `-> Self`. The generic-BOUND form now typechecks but mis-dispatches at runtime — see B-2026-07-03-24. REGRESSION (introduced by this fix in ae7c9525, missed by a concurrent-load-raced suite run; repaired in d15c8372): registering baked primitive impls made find_methods_with_args('i32',[],'cmp') non-empty, so the routing sent builtin comparison/cast methods (a.cmp(b)) into impl-table dispatch where the baked (self,other) signature mis-counts arity ('expects 2 arguments, found 1') — broke 4 typechecker Ord/PartialOrd tests. Fix: builtin value methods (cmp/eq/ne/lt/le/gt/ge/cast) stay on their dedicated backend path, never routed.

SILENT DATA LOSS / no-op: the interpreter's `value_compare` (src/interpreter/helpers.rs) has NO arm for `Value::Struct` or `Value::EnumVariant`, so two struct (or two enum) values fall to the `_ => value_discriminant(a).cmp(&value_discriminant(b))` fallback where BOTH map to the catch-all discriminant 8 -> always Ordering::Equal. Consequences over ordered containers / sort on struct (and enum) element/key types, ALL silent-wrong under `karac run`: `SortedSet[Struct]` and `SortedMap[Struct,_]` COLLAPSE distinct keys to one (insert 3 distinct structs -> len()==1 — data loss; n11/n13), `Vec[Struct].sort()`/`sorted()` are NO-OPs that return the input order unchanged (n7: prints 3 1 2), and `min`/`max` over a Vec[Struct] are wrong. Hash-based `Set[Struct]`/`Map[Struct,_]` are UNAFFECTED (they route through derived Hash+Eq, not value_compare — n12 dedups correctly).
