Exactly the class FIXED for nested Vec in B-2026-06-30-15 (whose fix worked because Vec elements carry inherent order); Struct/EnumVariant are the remaining missing arms. ARCHITECTURAL wrinkle blocking a one-line arm: `value_compare` is a free fn AND backs `OrdValue::cmp`, which BTreeMap calls deep inside insert with no interpreter/type-registry access, so it cannot see struct FIELD or enum VARIANT declaration order — a naive alphabetical-field / variant-name comparison fixes the data loss but yields a WRONG order (e.g. enum Priority{Low,Med,High} would sort alphabetically High<Low<Med, and struct{last,first} would order by first). Correct fix: embed declaration order into the Struct/EnumVariant Value (or compute an ordering key with type info at SortedSet/SortedMap insert + sort call sites, where the interpreter IS in scope) so both value_compare and OrdValue reflect derived-Ord semantics. Surfaced probing narrow-width x trait (derive(Ord) on struct{i8,u8}); NOT width-specific — reproduces with i64 fields. Repros: n7 (sort no-op), n11 (SortedSet len 1), n13 (SortedMap len 1). FIXED (1b1a843b): added `Value::Struct` and `Value::EnumVariant` arms to value_compare (src/interpreter/helpers.rs) + `compare_field_maps`/`compare_enum_data` helpers. Structs order by type name then fields in sorted-field-name order; enums by enum name then variant name then payload. Both are proper total orders consistent with Value::eq, so distinct values no longer collapse in the OrdValue-keyed BTreeMap (SortedSet/SortedMap counts preserved, Vec[struct].sort() orders, min/max correct). Same class/shape as the nested-Vec fix B-2026-06-30-15. Order is alphabetical (NOT derived-Ord declaration order — value_compare has no type-registry access); that affects only observed sort/iteration SEQUENCE, never membership/dedup/count, and is tracked as B-2026-07-03-12. Test: interpreter test_struct_and_enum_ordering_no_dataloss_and_sorts. interpreter 1085 + all non-llvm green; fmt+clippy(incl llvm) clean.

Codegen side of the derived-Ord-struct ordering surface is unimplemented but fails LOUD (not silent, unlike its interp sibling B-2026-07-03-6): `Vec[Struct].sort()` errors 'Vec.sort() in codegen supports integer, String, float, tuple, and nested-Vec element types; use sort_by(|a,b| ...) for other element types'; `SortedSet.insert`/`SortedMap` on struct element/key types error 'no handler for method insert on variable s (method dispatch fell through)'. SEPARATELY, the comparison operators `<`/`>`/`<=`/`>=` on a `#[derive(Ord)]` struct are a HARD TYPE ERROR on BOTH surfaces ('operator Lt is not defined for operands of type Struct and Struct' under run; 'Unsupported struct binary op: Lt' under build), so `#[derive(Ord)]` on a struct today wires up no usable ordering operator at all — only sort_by(comparator->Ordering) works end-to-end for struct element ordering. The 'use sort_by for other element types' wording implies sort() is INTENDED to honor an element's Ord, so struct/enum element sort() + SortedSet/SortedMap struct keys are the gap; whether direct struct `<` should also lower (vs. being sort_by/.cmp-only by design) is an open design question worth resolving alongside. Related: B-2026-07-03-6 (interp silent version). Repros: n4 (sort build error), n10 (struct `<` hard error), n11 (SortedSet.insert no handler). FIXED (ba67416e sort + 22705148 operators + 31280a03 parity-gate). Extended the recursive karac_cmp_<T> family (emit_cmp_fn_for_type_expr) to named user structs (Body::Tuple template on the struct LLVM layout, DECLARATION field order) and enums (order by variant DISCRIMINANT = tag@field0 = declaration order, then active-variant payload fields @ LLVM index start_word+1 — tuple/struct/unit incl. scalar-in-payload-word loads); cmp_fn_in_progress guards self-recursion (S{next:Vec[S]}→unorderable, not infinite compile recursion). This lights up Vec[Struct/Enum].sort(). Separately the `<`/`<=`/`>`/`>=` operators now lower: typecheck admission (infer_binary requires PartialOrd/Ord on struct/enum operands, closing a looseness where ANY struct `<` was silently admitted) + interp arm (value_compare) + codegen compile_ordered_user_cmp (resolve type name via type_name_of_expr/enum_name_of_expr for bare Enum.Variant literals, call karac_cmp, compare i64 to 0). PARITY GATE (31280a03): emitting the comparator unconditionally broke run==build — test_e2e_vec_sort_unordered_element_still_rejected (non-Ord struct sort must error) AND, worse, Option[i64]<Option[i64] / Ordering<Ordering TYPECHECK (derive Ord) and the interp arm ordered them via value_compare, but codegen can't emit a comparator for a generic enum's TypeParam payload / a baked prelude enum absent from program.items → run silently succeeded while build errored (typecheck gate is useless for parity because karac run executes PAST typecheck errors). Fixed with ONE opt-in predicate applied IDENTICALLY on both surfaces: codegen ord_orderable_types (register_ord_orderable_types from the user program's #[derive(Ord/PartialOrd)] items + user impl Ord/PartialOrd) gating emit_cmp_fn_for_struct/enum, and interp aggregate_is_orderable mirroring it (NON-generic, NON-stdlib user struct/enum deriving Ord/PartialOrd — generic_params.is_empty() + !defining_stdlib_origin are load-bearing: exclude Option/Result AND Ordering/MemoryOrdering). SUB-PART 2 (SortedSet/SortedMap struct KEYS) is NOT a struct-ordering gap — the whole sorted-container codegen backend is interpreter-only in v1 (no runtime B-tree), the pre-existing tracked limitation B-2026-06-20-5, independent of key type. Both engines are STRUCTURAL (ignore a user's hand-written impl Ord body; they agree with EACH OTHER, just not with a custom Ord — separate known limitation). Tests: interpreter test_ordered_operators_on_derived_ord_struct_and_enum + test_struct_enum_ordering..., codegen e2e_struct_sort/e2e_enum_sort/e2e_ordered_operators_on_derived_ord_struct_and_enum, par_codegen test_e2e_auto_par_struct_enum_sort_declaration_order/test_e2e_auto_par_ordered_operators_struct_enum. Green: interpreter 1092, typechecker 1870, codegen 2006, par_codegen 149, non-llvm 7291; fmt+clippy(llvm) clean.

Trait DEFAULT METHODS are not inherited/dispatched onto implementing types: a method with a default body in the trait cannot be called on an implementor unless the impl RE-implements it. design.md intends this feature (line 471 'optional default method implementations'; the cross-trait default-body section at ~3427; default bodies are effect-checked at ~6944) and the typechecker DOES type-check default method BODIES (Self-as-typeparam, self.method() intra-body dispatch — tests at typechecker.rs ~7959/7982/8818), but the default is never registered as a callable method on the impl target. Repro g3 (pure default, empty impl): trait Greet { fn hello(self)->i64 { 42 } } struct C {} impl Greet for C {} ; c.hello() -> typecheck 'no method hello on type C' / interp 'method hello not found on type C (no interpreter dispatch arm)'. Repro g1 (default calling a required method): trait Bump { fn base(self)->i64; fn bumped(self)->i64 { self.base()+1 } } impl Bump for C { fn base(self)->i64 { self.v } } ; c.bumped() fails identically. Copying the default body verbatim into the impl (g2) makes it work, confirming it's the inheritance/resolution that's missing, not the body. Consistent across BOTH surfaces (typecheck hard error under build; interp runtime no-dispatch-arm under run) — a missing feature, not a run/build divergence. NOT narrow-width-specific (repros with i64). Fix spans method resolution: when a method isn't found as an inherent/impl method on T but a trait Tr that T implements declares that method WITH a default body, dispatch to the default with Self=T (typechecker method-lookup + interpreter eval_method_call dispatch + codegen synthesize the default as a `T.method` fn, reusing make_impl_method_function). Surfaced probing narrow-width x trait (trait default returning u8), but general. FIXED (6d488e58): pre-resolve desugar pass `synthesize_trait_default_methods` (src/desugar.rs) copies each non-overridden default-bodied trait method (converted TraitMethod->Function) into every `impl Tr for T` block, so all phases see it as an ordinary impl method (the form that already worked end-to-end). Scoped to user-declared NON-generic traits; generic-trait defaults guarded out (revert cleanly to 'no method', tracked as B-2026-07-03-10). Tests: typechecker trait_default_method_callable_on_implementor, codegen e2e e2e_trait_default_methods_dispatch_on_implementor. Suites green: typechecker 1863 / interpreter 1083 / codegen 1983 / par_codegen 142 / all non-llvm; fmt+clippy clean.

FIXED (dda5d5de): A generic `Slice[T]` by-value param called with a Vec arg fails codegen module verification: `fn gfirst[T](s: Slice[T]) -> T { s[0] }; gfirst(myvec)` emits `call i64 @gfirst({ptr,i64,i64} %a)` against a declared `{ptr,i64}` Slice header — the Vec-to-Slice header coercion is missing at the generic-mono by-value Slice-param call site. The NON-generic form `fn sfirst(s: Slice[i64])` called with a Vec works (the coercion fires in the non-generic direct-call path), and `mut Slice[T]` generic params already synthesize the header (slice 8z); only the by-value `Slice[T]` generic mono is unhandled. `karac check` clean, interpreter correct. Surfaced probing S6b-1's Vec[T] element-collision fix (p7_slice_coerce.kara); distinct from that element-binding bug (B-2026-07-02-41) — this is the arg ABI coercion, not element inference. Route the by-value Slice[T] mono arg through the same `coerce_to_slice` header synthesis the mut-Slice path uses. FIX: compile_generic_call's direct-call arg loop now consults fn_param_slice_elem[mangled] (populated by declare_mono_function via extract_slice_elem_type, element T resolved through the active type_subst) and runs coerce_to_slice for by-value Slice[T] params -- the same header synthesis the non-generic path and the mut-Slice state-machine path already used. Tests: e2e_generic_by_value_slice_param_coercion (codegen, seq) + test_e2e_auto_par_generic_by_value_slice_param (par_codegen). Surfaced two tangential pre-existing gaps ledgered as B-2026-07-03-21 and -22.

Follow-on to B-2026-07-03-8: trait default methods on GENERIC traits are not inherited onto implementors. The `synthesize_trait_default_methods` desugar pass copies a default body verbatim into `impl Tr for T`, but when the trait is generic (`trait Box[U] { fn get(self)->U; fn twice(self)->U { self.get() } }`) the copied `-> U` / body references the trait's own type param `U`, out of scope in the impl (`Box[i64]`) -> resolver 'undefined type U'. The pass therefore GUARDS OUT generic traits (`t.generic_params.is_some()`), leaving them at the pre-fix 'no method' error (no regression, no confusing new error). Correct fix: substitute the impl's trait-args (from `imp.trait_name` PathExpr generic_args, positional against the trait's generic_param_names) through the copied method's param types, return type, and body type-expressions before splicing. Repro e4: trait Box2[T]{fn get(self)->T; fn twice(self)->T{self.get()}} impl Box2[i64] for W{fn get(self)->i64{self.v}} ; w.twice() -> 'no method twice on type W'.

FIXED (db020ee6): PRE-EXISTING (surfaced while verifying B-2026-07-03-8; confirmed still open after the S6b TypeExpr-level mono work of B-2026-07-02-41): codegen does not dispatch a trait method called through a GENERIC TYPE-PARAMETER BOUND. `fn use_it[X: Named](x: X) -> i64 { x.tag() }` fails `karac build` with 'codegen: no handler for method tag on variable x (method dispatch fell through)' while `karac run` executes correctly (7). Confirmed NOT specific to default methods and NOT to narrow width: reproduces with an EXPLICITLY implemented method (e3b: trait Named{fn tag(self)->i64;} impl Named for C{fn tag(self)->i64{7}} fn use_it[X: Named](x: X)->i64{x.tag()}) — codegen's method-call lowering has no arm for a receiver whose type is a bound generic param; it needs monomorphization to bind X to the concrete impl type and dispatch to `<ConcreteType>.tag` (same mono family as B-2026-07-02-41/42). The typechecker resolves it fine (dispatch_typeparam_receiver_method); only codegen falls through. Run/build divergence (build refuses, no miscompile). Repro e3b. FIX: threads a name-level type_subst_names (X->concrete) parallel to the LLVM type_subst; mono param prologue registers a bound-generic receiver under its concrete impl type so inferred_receiver_type resolves X.tag->C.tag, and record_var_type_name resolves any generic-param-named binding centrally. Also closed 3 coupled gaps: (a) mangle collision (all user structs lower to opaque "struct" token so use_it$A/use_it$B shared one symbol -> per-mono concrete-name mangle axis for USER structs/enums, builtins keep $struct); (b) let b=w.bump() (-> Self through bound) fell to the HashMap-order LLVM-shape reverse-lookup in stmts.rs -> added a MethodCall ast_hint arm resolving via type_name_of_expr; (c) narrow-width generic return split out as B-2026-07-03-17. 3 codegen E2E + 1 LSan-clean memory_sanitizer test.

Follow-on to B-2026-07-03-6: interpreter struct/enum ordering in value_compare is by ALPHABETICAL field/variant name, not derived-`Ord` DECLARATION order. This is correct for membership/dedup/count (the data-loss bug is fixed) but the observed SEQUENCE of `Vec[Struct].sort()` / `SortedSet`/`SortedMap` iteration can differ from a Rust-like derived Ord: a struct `{ width, height }` sorts by height-then-width, and — more visibly — an enum `Priority { Low, Med, High }` sorts alphabetically (High < Low < Med) instead of Low < Med < High. value_compare (and OrdValue::cmp, called inside BTreeMap with no interpreter handle) cannot reach the struct field / enum variant declaration order held in typecheck_result.{struct_info,enum_info}. Correct fix needs that order at the comparison point: either store it on the Value at construction (struct fields in declaration order / an ordered map; a variant discriminant index on EnumVariant) or thread a type-order registry to value_compare. Low severity: no data loss, and there is no user-facing struct/enum `<` operator today (a hard type error — see B-2026-07-03-7), so no ordering contract is broken; matters mainly when a user reads the sort/iteration sequence or when codegen later implements struct/enum ordering and must agree. FIXED (9bc8e762): a per-thread declaration-order registry (src/interpreter/type_order.rs: struct name→field→idx, enum name→variant→idx, enum::variant→field→idx) built from TypeCheckResult.{struct_info,enum_info} (both Vec, source order) and installed in Interpreter::new — the universal chokepoint every eval path funnels through, INCLUDING each par{} branch (constructs its own Interpreter::new on its spawned thread), so the thread-local is present on every thread reaching value_compare with no RAII guard and no cross-thread propagation. value_compare/OrdValue::cmp read it via type_order::current() (clone the Rc out, hold no RefCell borrow across the recursive compare); absent registry → alphabetical fallback, so the no-data-loss guarantee stays unconditional. Struct fields, enum variant index, and struct-variant payload fields all order by declaration index now. Test: interpreter test_struct_enum_ordering_is_declaration_order_not_alphabetical (Vec.sort value_compare path + SortedSet OrdValue::cmp-inside-BTreeMap path). interpreter 1092 green.

Interpreter: a `mut ref` SCALAR parameter FORWARDED unmarked into a nested/recursive call did not propagate the callee's mutation back to the caller — a `mut ref i64` accumulator threaded down a recursion read 0. The tree-walk interpreter's copy-in-copy-out (CICO) writeback (eval_call.rs) keyed ONLY on the call-site `mut` marker, but per design.md § Call-site mutation markers an already-in-scope `mut ref` binding FORWARDS WITHOUT a marker, so the chain broke: inner callee mutated its own frame's copy, intermediate frames never copied back. Direct `main` -> `inc(mut t)` (marked) worked; `wrap(x: mut ref i64){ inc(x); }` and any recursion did not. Run/build divergence: correct under `karac build` (codegen has full aliasing), 0 under `karac run`. FIXED (13cbc81e): key CICO writeback on the callee's DECLARED param mode too (TypeKind::MutRef/MutSlice, via new interpreter helper `fn_param_mut_ref_flags`), so a forwarded `mut ref` identifier arg writes back regardless of the marker. Surfaced by #52 marker_arrays.kara (all-zeros counts under run). Repro: fn inc(x: mut ref i64){x=x+1i64;} fn wrap(x: mut ref i64){inc(x);} fn main(){let mut t:i64=0; wrap(mut t); println(t);} -> 0, want 1. Tests: interpreter test_scalar_mut_ref_writeback_through_forwarded_call, test_scalar_mut_ref_accumulator_through_recursion.

Auto-par: the reduction recognizer accepted a `+` reduction whose per-iteration delta RECURSES into the enclosing function (`if legal { total = total + count(...deeper...) }`, a backtracking counter). The reduction is arithmetically valid, but parallelizing it opens a fresh nested parallel region at EVERY recursion level; the fan-out compounds into runaway task nesting that exhausts the stack — SIGBUS (exit 138) at depth, with correct output surviving only for tiny inputs (n<=6 fine, n>=9 crashes). Default-auto-par-only: `karac run` and `KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0 karac build` both correct. FIXED (13cbc81e): concurrency.rs `recognize_reductions` declines a loop whose body directly recurses into its own function, via new comprehensive walker `call_graph::block_calls_function` (reuses the callee collector with a singleton `known` set); the loop falls back to the correct sequential lowering. Direct self-recursion is the demonstrated + common case; transitive/mutual recursion through a helper is a known residual gap. Surfaced by #52 bitmask_count.kara / symmetry.kara (SIGBUS under default build). Repro: the standard bitmask N-Queens counter `fn count(n,row,cols,d1,d2)->i64` swept to n=11 under `karac build`. Tests: concurrency test_reduction_rejects_recursive_self_call_in_body, test_reduction_still_recognized_with_nonrecursive_call_in_body. FOLLOW-ON (shallow-depth-parallel-reduction, docs/spikes/): the conservative compile-time decline was REPLACED by a runtime fork-depth cap (`KARAC_PAR_MAX_FORK_DEPTH`, default 1, thread-local `PAR_REDUCE_DEPTH` in `karac_par_reduce`). Recursive-delta reductions are now recognized and lowered again; only the OUTERMOST level fans out, deeper levels run inline — safe (nesting bounded by a constant, no stack blowup) AND useful (a single N-Queens count parallelizes ~9.5x). `call_graph::block_calls_function` and the recognizer decline were removed. Env reads on the hot path are OnceLock-cached to avoid a per-recursion-node syscall storm.

A GENERIC impl/trait METHOD called on a CONCRETE receiver is not codegen-monomorphized: `fn apply[A](ref self, init: A, f: Fn(A, i64) -> A) -> A { f(init, self.v) }` on a concrete `One`, called `o.apply(2, |a,x| a+x)`, runs correctly under `karac run` (42) but `karac build` dies loud: 'codegen: no handler for method apply on variable o (method dispatch fell through)'. Reproduces with a HAND-WRITTEN generic impl method (no trait, no default body) so it is pre-existing and independent of the S6b-2 default-body inheritance work (B-2026-07-03-10) that surfaced it — the substituted default `fn apply[A]` inherits the same wall. This is S6-pre finding 2b and the S6b-3 slice: codegen's compile_method_call has no arm to bind a method's OWN generic params (`A`) to concrete arg types and mangle a per-call mono. Distinct from B-2026-07-03-11 (dispatch through a generic type-param BOUND — receiver type is the generic param; here the receiver is concrete and the METHOD is generic). Blocks the real Reduce `fold[A]` default. Probed p4_generic_method_default.kara.

SILENT MISCOMPILE: immediate field access on the result of a call that returns an aggregate (struct) — `f().field` — reads 0/default under `karac build`, while `karac run` is correct. General: reproduces with a free fn `fn make() -> Cnt { Cnt { n: 7 } }` and `make().n` -> build prints 0 (run 7); with an instance method `m.go().n`; with a static assoc fn `Cnt.zero()`; owned or ref self; ALL fields (offset-independent: `P{a,b}` -> `make().a`/`make().b` both 0). NOT f-string specific (`make().n + 100` = 100 under build). The workaround is to bind first: `let x = make(); x.field` builds correctly (7), and `let c = make(); c.n` at the call site is fine too — so the aggregate return value materializes correctly; only the direct-field-access-on-call-temporary lowering drops it (likely reads the field off a zeroed/uninitialized spill slot instead of the call's sret aggregate). Pre-existing, unrelated to traits/generics; surfaced probing S6b-2's `g.make().n` / `g.seed().n` default-method calls (the S6b-2 codegen test binds the result first to sidestep it). High severity — extremely common pattern, silent wrong value, run/build divergence. Probed isoH/isoP/isoP2/iso2* .kara. FIXED as a DUPLICATE of B-2026-07-03-3 (identical root cause, found independently while probing S6b-2 generic default methods): type_name_of_expr had no Call/MethodCall arm, so field_index_for returned None and compile_field_access's generic tail emitted the i64 0 placeholder instead of an extractvalue at the field's index. Resolved on main by 839beaea, which adds Call/MethodCall arms to type_name_of_expr (src/codegen/expr_ops.rs) that resolve a call's static return-type name via fn_return_type_names -- free fns key on the bare callee, static assoc fns on the 2-segment Type.method Path, method calls resolve the receiver recursively. Verified every B-16 shape now builds==runs: free-fn make().n=7, multi-field make_p().a/.b=3/9, static assoc Cnt.zero().n/Cnt.forty().n=0/40, ref-self g.goref().n=7, plain non-f-string print(make().n)=7, and make().n + 100=107. Added complementary codegen e2e regression test e2e_call_result_field_access_assoc_and_ref_self (tests/codegen.rs) locking in the assoc-fn / ref-self / multi-field / plain-print shapes that the sibling e2e_chained_call_struct_field_access did not cover; fmt + clippy clean, codegen + memory_sanitizer suites green.

FIXED (db020ee6): a GENERIC function with a NON-generic NARROW return type mis-lowered under karac build (surfaced while probing B-2026-07-03-11; reproduces with NO trait/bound, so pre-existing and independent). `fn narrow[T](x: T) -> u8 { 255 }` (a) failed module verification -- the mono tail-return in compile_mono_function emitted `ret i64 255` into an i8-returning fn because it skipped coerce_to_current_ret_type (the non-generic compile_function path applies it); and (b) once coerced, printed 255u8 as -1 -- expr_is_unsigned_int's Call arm found no fn_return_type_names entry for the un-declared generic fn (generic fns are registered in generic_fns, never declared) and defaulted to signed. karac run was correct both ways. FIX: mono tail-return now calls coerce_to_current_ret_type (truncates to the declared narrow width, no-op for matching/non-scalar returns); generic fns register their CONCRETE return-type name in fn_return_type_names at setup (skipping a generic `-> T` return). Covered by e2e_generic_fn_narrow_width_return.

S6b-4a operator-on-bounded-T: `a OP b` on a type parameter bounded by the stdlib operator trait for that operator (`+`->Add, `-`->Sub, `*`->Mul, `/`->Div, `%`->Rem, unary `-`->Neg) was a HARD typecheck error under `karac build` ('arithmetic operator requires numeric type, found T') and only a warning-then-run under `karac run` -- the run/build divergence that blocked the stdlib Reduce fold-based defaults (whose bodies fold with +/* over a bounded element type). Reproduces with a free fn `fn add_gen[T: Add](a: T, b: T) -> T { a + b }` (build-rejected, run-warns-then-30) AND with a generic-trait default body `fn sum2(ref self) -> T { self.a() + self.b() }` on `trait Foldy[T: Add + Mul]`. FIXED (5c761a6e): PURE TYPECHECK admission mirroring the pre-existing `T: Numeric` arm -- infer_binary (src/typechecker/expr_ops.rs) admits the operator when the operand type param carries the operator trait for THIS operator (arithmetic_operator_trait(op)), yielding result T; the unary-'-' check likewise admits a `T: Neg` bound. SOUND with no check-passes/build-fails gap because user operator-trait impls are FORBIDDEN (resolver: 'operator traits are stdlib-only'), so every instantiation of such a T is a primitive numeric / String (Add) / distinct-numeric that codegen already lowers for the operator post-monomorphization (verified the T: Numeric arithmetic path already builds+runs, so codegen is unchanged). type_param_has_trait_bound accepts BOTH operand spellings: Type::TypeParam (a free-fn-bound param) AND a bare Type::Named{name,args:[]} whose name is an in-scope type param (a `-> T` method result / `let x: T` local inside a generic-trait default body -- the Named-vs-TypeParam trap), by consulting enclosing_bounds (the authoritative in-scope type-param set). Wrong-trait (`-` under T: Add) and unbounded-T arithmetic stay rejected. A/B-verified run == KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0 == default-auto-par build for free-fn (i64/f64/String/Neg) and generic-trait-default (+/*) shapes. NOTE: the feat commit 5c761a6e message references this as B-2026-07-03-17; renumbered to -18 here because a parallel session landed a different B-2026-07-03-17 (generic-fn narrow-return mis-lowering, db020ee6) concurrently. Tests: typechecker operator_on_operator_trait_bounded_type_param_admitted + operator_on_wrong_or_missing_trait_bound_rejected; codegen e2e e2e_operator_on_operator_trait_bounded_type_param.

S6b-4: a user `impl Reduce[T] for MyType` could not inherit a DEFAULT method from the BAKED stdlib `Reduce[T]` trait. synthesize_trait_default_methods (the S6b-2/-3 splice pass) only consulted traits declared in the USER program, but baked stdlib traits live in crate::prelude::STDLIB_PROGRAMS, not program.items -- so there was nothing to splice even once Reduce grew a default body. FIXED (c0a83c33): extracted the per-trait default-method collection into collect_trait_defaults_from_items and call it for the user program AND every STDLIB_PROGRAMS program (user-first, entry().or_insert, so a same-named user trait shadows); clear stdlib_origin on each spliced copy so it is resolved/typechecked/ownership-checked/codegen'd as ordinary user code (a stdlib-origin body is otherwise skipped like the never-checked baked impl bodies). Reduce[T] gains its first DEFAULT method `fn range(ref self) -> T { self.max() - self.min() }`; a CONCRETE `impl Reduce[i64]/[f64] for MyType` inherits .range() with the concrete T substituted, so self.max()-self.min() lowers as native T-T. Only Reduce.range has a stdlib default body today, so the change touches only user Reduce impls. A/B-verified run==KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0==default-auto-par build (Trio range=6, FDuo range=7.5). RESIDUAL (ledgered): generic user impls blocked on B-2026-07-03-20; builtin Column/Tensor do NOT inherit range (their #[compiler_builtin] impls are in STDLIB_PROGRAMS, not rewritten by the splice) -- a separate axis; fold/product/Option-forms still need a fold[A] primitive that would change the required-method set. Test: codegen e2e test_e2e_stdlib_reduce_default_method_inherited_by_user_impl.

FIXED (a0206b1f): a BOUND on a generic impl's own type param (`impl[T: Sub] Pair[T] { ... }`) broke method resolution inside AND outside the impl under `karac build`: self's type resolves to the bare `Pair` (no args), so `self.other_method()` -- and even an external `p.gap()` -- reported 'no method X on type Pair'. UNBOUNDED generic impls (`impl[T] Pair[T]`) work fine; the SOLE trigger is a bound (`T: Sub`, `T: Numeric`, ...) on the impl's generic param. Reproduces with a plain INHERENT impl -- NO traits/defaults/stdlib. Run/build divergence: `karac run` executes past the warning and prints the CORRECT value; `build` hard-errors. ROOT: impl_bounds_discharge (src/typechecker/env.rs) dropped a candidate whenever it could not PROVE the impl's bound -- `self` has the bare target type (no arg to substitute the bound against), and a `Pair[T]` receiver (outer generic param) substitutes to a Type::TypeParam not in the impl table, so bound_satisfied returned false. Both are UNDECIDABLE not FALSE; the concrete instantiation / enclosing bound discharges them. FIX: make discharge permissive for a missing OR Type::TypeParam substitution, in both the inline-bound and where-clause arms; a concrete arg that genuinely fails its bound still drops the candidate (conditional-impl disambiguation unchanged, 6 typechecker conditional-impl tests green). Directly unblocks GENERIC user impls of stdlib Reduce (`impl[T: Sub] Reduce[T] for Pair[T]` inherits+resolves the spliced `range` default, i64->7 run==build). Residual: NON-i64 (f64) element still miscompiles via the separate generic struct-literal arg-inference bug B-2026-07-03-23. Tests: typechecker bounded_generic_impl_methods_resolve, codegen e2e test_e2e_bounded_generic_impl_method_call (i64). Surfaced probing S6b-4.

A narrow-width (u8/u16/u32) local whose RHS is a generic call loses its UNSIGNED-ness when the binding lands in an auto-par PAR GROUP: `fn vfirst[T](v: ref Vec[T]) -> T { v[0] }; let a = geti(); let b = geti(); let u: u8 = vfirst(vu); println(u)` prints -1 (signed i8 view of 255) under the DEFAULT/auto-par build (KARAC_AUTO_PAR=1), while the sequential build (KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0) and interp print 255. The VALUE is correct (0xFF bits); only PRINT signedness is wrong. Root: par_blocks.rs return-slot materialization (the post-join `for slot in &return_slots` loop) registers `variables` and `vec_elem_types` for grouped-let bindings but NEVER `var_type_names` (nor any signedness marker), so expr_is_unsigned_int falls back to signed for the reconstructed binding. The annotation `let u: u8` IS honored sequentially (registered at the let site) but that registration is lost across the par-group slot transfer -- the ReturnSlot carries binding_name + llvm_ty (i8) but not the source signedness. NOT slice-specific (reproduces with a `ref Vec[T]` generic) and NOT caused by B-2026-07-03-9 (that fix only made the Slice variant reachable). Surfaced probing B-2026-07-03-9. Fix: thread the source narrow-int surface name / signedness into ReturnSlot and register var_type_names in the post-join materialization (or consult pattern_binding_types by the binding's span). FIX 9f986336: thread the binding's annotation type name into ReturnSlot (`let_binding_annotation_type_name`) and re-register `var_type_names` in BOTH post-join materialization loops (auto-par dispatch in stmts.rs + explicit `par` blocks in par_blocks.rs). Verified interp/seq/auto-par for u8/u16/u32 (plain + f-string) plus a signed i8 control that stays -1 (no over-correction).

A generic `-> T` return whose T is bound from a Slice/container ELEMENT is not resolved to its concrete type at the f-string format site: `fn gsum[T](s: Slice[T]) -> T { s[0] }; let vs: Vec[String] = [..]; println(f"{gsum(vs)}")` prints the String's heap POINTER as an integer (e.g. 4296181632) instead of the string, on BOTH the sequential and default builds (`karac run` is correct). The DIRECT-param form `fn gid[T](x: T) -> T; f"{gid(s)}"` with a String arg works -- the sole trigger is T being bound via a container element (Slice[T]/Vec[T] param) rather than a bare `x: T` param. The f-string interpolation's type resolution for the call result doesn't recover T=String when T came from the arg's element type, so it formats the value as a scalar. Distinct from B-2026-07-03-9 (arg ABI coercion, fixed) -- this is the RETURN-type resolution at the use/format site. Surfaced probing B-2026-07-03-9 (a `f"{gsum(vs)}"` case, replaced with `glen(vs)` in the landed test). Also a likely sibling of the narrow-signedness use-site gap B-2026-07-03-21 (both are 'generic -> T result type unknown at the use site'). Fix: resolve a container-element-bound T at the format/print site (extend the generic-call return-type-name resolution the way B-2026-07-03-17 did for concrete generic returns). ROOT CAUSE (not the format site as first hypothesized): monomorphization subst inference never bound T=String -- `augment_subst_from_arg_elem_types`'s Slice arm keyed only on `slice_elem_types`, but a Vec/Array arg registers its element in `vec_elem_types` / the `variables` array slot, so T stayed unbound and defaulted to i64; the mono then compiled `s[0]` as an i64 read and returned the String's heap pointer. The i64-element cases masked it (unbound T defaults to i64). FIX b5432340: route the Slice arm through `infer_elem_from_source` (unifies Slice/Vec/Array element resolution), the mono-side twin of the B-2026-07-03-9 by-value arg coercion. Verified interp/seq/auto-par; macOS ASan + Linux LSan clean (s[0] clones the String element).

FIXED (four layers, 7ab01d78 typecheck + 08ee105d/168e2e37 codegen): a generic struct with an inline type-param field (`struct Box[T]{v:T}`, even unbounded / no impl) lost its type arg, so codegen defaulted the element to i64 and mis-read non-i64 fields. `let b = Box{v:2.5}; println(f"{b.v}")` printed 4612811918334230528 (2.5's f64 bits as i64) under build, 2.5 under run; a by-value method on such a struct HARD-crashed the build (Module verification failed). ROOT+FIX: (1) typechecker infers a struct literal's generic args (infer_struct_literal solves them from the field values via fresh metavars -> Box[f64]); (2) field access substitutes the receiver's args into the field type (infer_field_access / field_type_with_receiver_args -> b.v : f64); (3) codegen builds a per-instantiation LLVM struct type (Box[f64] -> {double}) via mono_struct_type wired into llvm_type_for_type_expr's Path arm + compile_struct_init (LLVM interns literal struct types, no cache; struct_generic_params map added); (4) methods on a generic struct monomorphize by the RECEIVER's instantiation -- make_generic_impl_method_function types self as the impl target Box[T] + merges impl generic params, registered into generic_fns and dispatched via compile_generic_call binding the impl T explicitly from the receiver's recorded instantiation (enum_inst_type_from_span / enum_inst_var_types). Covers f64/i64/String elements, field store, two-field arithmetic, function ABI, Vec of a generic struct, and ref-self / by-value-self / bounded-impl methods; A/B-verified run == KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0 == build. This makes the f64 arm of generic Reduce impls (Pair[f64].range()) work (S6b-4c). Renumbered from B-21 (parallel autopar collision; the B-20 fix commit a0206b1f message reads B-21). Gates: codegen 1997+2 new, par 145, typechecker 1870, interpreter 1086, ASAN 495, Linux LSan 495; fmt+clippy clean. Tests: codegen e2e test_e2e_generic_struct_field_monomorphizes_by_element + test_e2e_generic_struct_method_monomorphizes_by_receiver.

Follow-on to B-2026-07-03-5: a generic BOUND over a PRIMITIVE trait impl (`fn f[T: Dbl](x: T) -> T { x.dbl() }` called with a u8/i32/f64) now TYPECHECKS (primitive impls register after B-5) but mis-dispatches at RUNTIME on BOTH surfaces. (1) INTERP: inside the generic fn `x` has runtime value `Value::Int`/`Value::Float` (width-erased to i64/f64) and the receiver type at the `x.dbl()` call site is a TypeParam, so `method_callee_type_name(TypeParam)` is None and NO `method_callee_types` entry is recorded -> dispatch falls back to the erased 'i64.dbl'/'f64.dbl' key and errors "method 'dbl' not found on type 'i64' (no interpreter dispatch arm)" for every width whose impl isn't keyed i64/f64. (2) CODEGEN: monomorphization dispatches the WRONG primitive impl when MULTIPLE impls of the trait exist -- `dbl_it(c)` with c:i32 called the i8 impl (integer-overflow panic); a SINGLE primitive impl monomorphizes correctly. This is the primitive analog of B-2026-07-03-11 (generic-bound dispatch for USER types via type_subst_names). Direct-call `a.dbl()` is fully fixed (B-5). Fix: interp -- thread the concrete arg type into type-param-receiver dispatch (key on the caller's recorded generic instantiation rather than the erased runtime value type); codegen -- make primitive-impl mono dispatch select by the concrete bound type NAME (mirror the type_subst_names name-aware dispatch/mangle B-11 added for user types). FIX d15c8372 BOTH surfaces: (codegen) the mono mangle now appends the concrete primitive NAME from subst_names — narrow ints widen to i64 so the LLVM token collided (f$i64 for every width), the second mono reused the first's body; name-append (primitive analog of B-11's struct/enum fix) makes each width a distinct correctly-dispatching mono. (interp) new `method_typeparam_receiver` typecheck table records the receiver's type-param name (expr_types[receiver.span] is clobbered by the method result type via MethodCall.span==receiver.span); the interpreter resolves it through type_subs_stack to the concrete instantiation, preferred over the width-erased Value key so f32 doesn't fall into an existing f64 impl. Also repaired a B-5 routing regression (see B-2026-07-03-5). Verified all 8 scalar widths interp/seq/auto-par; user-type generic-bound dispatch unregressed.

`.iter().map(f).collect()` into a `Vec` FAILS under `karac build` while working under `karac run` — a run/build divergence on a book-documented idiom (ch10-closures-and-iterators shows `iter.map(|n| n*2).collect()`). MINIMAL: `let d: Vec[i64] = s.iter().map(|x| x*2i64).collect();` prints 4 under run, but build errors `codegen failed: no handler for method 'collect' on non-identifier receiver (method dispatch fell through; this is a codegen bug)`. Codegen only handles `collect` on an IDENTIFIER receiver (already-materialized Vec → clone, method_call.rs:288) and on `chars().collect()` (method_call.rs:3451); a lazy adaptor chain whose collect-receiver is a `map`/`filter` MethodCall (not an identifier, not `chars`) falls through to the dispatch-fail error. Gap is general to any non-identifier/non-`chars` collect receiver (confirmed for `.iter().map(f).collect()`). Surfaced while probing the collection-capacity-presizing spike (narrow-A): `.map().collect()` is the highest-value pre-sizing target but doesn't compile at all under codegen, so the correctness fix (implement the adaptor-chain → Vec lowering) MUST precede any pre-sizing — closes spike S2, and the lowering should reserve the source length while materializing. from_slice already pre-sizes (measured 16.7ms/1M vs Vec.new()+push 74.8ms); this idiom is the open one. FIXED 009fd479: `try_compile_iter_adaptor_collect_to_vec` (method_call.rs) desugars a `map`/`filter` chain to a `for` loop that pushes each surviving/transformed element onto a fresh `Vec`, reusing the supported constructs (for/closure-inline/if/push/block-move-out); output elem type from the collect expr's `owned_temp_drops` entry; the base-most closure param IS the loop var so a heap element keeps its type (`.map(|w| w.len())` over `Vec[String]`). A terminal `map` pushes its value directly — an intermediate `let x = f"…"` routes through the staged-f-string-accumulator path that double-frees the built String once it is also pushed. Verified run==build across POD/filter/filter+map/map+map/type-changing-map(Vec[String])/heap-source; ASAN+LSan clean on a looped heap test. RESIDUAL (see B-2026-07-03-29): non-`map`/`filter` adaptors into collect and a non-terminal f-string `map` still fail loudly under build.

Matching a non-Copy field (or indexed-element field) of a BORROWED `mut ref self` receiver was treated as consuming `self`, so a later whole-`self` use (`match self.tokens[self.pos].token {..}; self.advance()`) read as use-after-move under `karac check`/multi-file `karac build`. `param_types_for_function` left the borrowed `self` ABSENT, so `classify_identifier` fell back to `expr_types` = the bare (non-Copy) Self type and classified `self` as consumable; regressed by 5426bbd1 (projection-aware partial-move tracking, B-2026-07-02-25) which began recording `self.field` consumes. This shape pervades the self-hosted parser, so it reddened all three selfhost parser oracles (selfhost_parser{,_types,_items}) on main while selfhost_lexer stayed green. Fixed by recording a borrowed `self` (`ref self`/`mut ref self`) as a `Type::Ref` (Copy) in `param_types_for_function` so it classifies as a Read; owned `self` left absent so its consume semantics stand. (Renumbered from B-2026-07-03-25 at integration — collided with parallel session ffd50b59.) Regressions: tests/ownership.rs::{test_match_on_borrowed_self_field_does_not_consume_self, test_match_on_owned_self_field_still_consumes}.

An `Option[E]` field where E is a PLAIN (non-`shared`) user enum with a heap payload, dropped undestructured (via a `Some(_)` wildcard match or plain scope-drop), leaks the enum payload's heap buffer — the inline-Option drop path (cf. B-2026-06-10-6, which covered Option[String]/[Vec]/[Map]) does not recurse into a user-enum payload's drop. Minimal repro: `struct A { value: Option[Val] }`, `enum Val { Nothing, Ident(String) }`, Vec[A] consumed -> 360 bytes / 10 allocs leaked under LSan. `Option[shared enum]`, `Option[String]`, `Option[Vec]` are all clean. NON-BLOCKING for self-hosting: every recursive AST enum in the port is `shared`, and AttrArgNode.value is Option[Expr] (shared). FIXED 14d4391e: the destructure leaf of an `Option[<user struct/enum>]` field got no cleanup (`destructure_field_needs_cleanup` excludes Option; whole-struct drop skips Option fields — B-2026-07-03-28). `track_inline_option_agg_payload_var` now registers a tag-guarded `karac_drop_Option_<payload>` (`emit_option_drop_fn` — the same recursive fn the Vec[Option[..]] element path uses, handling the heap-BOXED wide payload) on the leaf when the destructure OWNS the source (fresh temp or moved-in owned binding, never a ref/borrow), paired with a Some-arm tag-zeroing suppressor (`inline_option_agg_payload_vars`) so a Some(v) move-out doesn't double-free. Covers Option[enum] AND Option[struct] payloads, both wildcard-match and plain scope-drop, Vec-sourced and fresh-temp sources. LSan 503/503. RESIDUAL: a Some(v) move-out of a wide/boxed Option-agg payload still leaks the heap box — separate pre-existing bug B-2026-07-03-31 (this fix keeps that path double-free-clean). New tests replace the pinned one: asan_b27_option_enum_undestructured_drop_no_leak / asan_b27_option_struct_undestructured_drop_no_leak.

RESIDUAL of Facet A after B-2026-07-03-33: an `Option` struct FIELD still leaks its Some-payload on plain/consumed drop when the payload is NOT the inline `{ptr,len,cap}` class B-33 handles — specifically `Option[shared]` (the parser's `AttrArgNode.value: Option[Expr]`, Expr shared) and `Option`s nested inside a `Vec[struct]` field (`AttrNode.args: Vec[AttrArgNode]`). Two coupled pieces remain: (a) `Option[shared]` entry-copy = rc-INC of the inline RC pointer (param_own) + drop = rc-DEC, so an `Option[shared]`-bearing struct becomes copy-supported and its struct-drop can free the Option; (b) element-DEEP entry-copy of a `Vec[struct-with-heap]` field so the deeper element drop (which frees the element struct's Options once (a) lands) stays symmetric with the copy (copy-depth == drop-depth; else a caller-retained Vec[struct] double-frees the shared element payloads). Pinned `asan_attr_node_list_drop_consume_and_plain` leaks 240 B / 6 allocs (down from 1434/36 original, 474/12 after B-30, 240/6 after B-33) = exactly the AttrArgNode name+value Options. `Option[boxed-wide]` and `Option[plain-enum]` (B-27) payloads also stay caller-retains. #[ignore]d pinned test. ATTEMPT 2026-07-03 (reverted, patch saved): tried (Change A) freeing the inline Option[String] in the shared-owning-struct DRAIN emit_nested_struct_shared_rc_decs gated on struct_shared_edges_all_option (all shared edges via Option[shared], excludes direct-shared like Arg{value:Expr}); (Piece 2) emit_option_clone_fn = type-aware deep Option clone (String/Vec deep-copy + Option[shared] rc-inc), the clone peer of emit_option_drop_fn, routed from emit_clone_fn_for_type_expr; (Piece 3) element-DEEP entry-copy of a Vec[shared-owning-struct] field via te_shared_owning_struct. RESULT: full macOS ASAN suite GREEN (no double-free; fixed the consume-path AttrN double-free that Change A alone caused), but LSan REGRESSED the pinned test 240/6 -> 714/18 (new direct string + INDIRECT shared-box leaks). ROOT CAUSE = the entry-copy-vs-move rc-balance: making the struct copy-supported forces a deep entry-copy of AttrNode.args, but for a{}in attrs the shared Val boxes get an extra rc-inc (clone) that the move/consume path never rc-decs (source drop suppressed) -> boxes+strings leak. The copy-supported model assumes caller-retains; the pinned test consumes via move. Closing this needs a move-aware entry-copy OR full consume-site rc-balance for copy-supported shared-owning structs -- an ownership-model change, NOT a contained increment. Patch in session scratchpad b28-residual-attempt.patch. FIXED (Phase 2 of the caller-retains model, 7f727aaa): closed WITHOUT the originally-scoped piece (b) — the pinned test's consume path self-balances (for-loop-consume + destructure-leaf rc-dec) and its plain-drop path is fixed by the combined struct drop. Three changes: (1) field_copy_supported admits an Option[shared] field (via option_inner_shared_type_for_type_expr), so the struct becomes copy-supported and its sibling name:Option[String] gets the OptionInline drop; (2) deep_copy_option_inline_payload_in_place rc-INCs the inline box (word 1, ptrtoint) when Some (rc_inc_option_inline_shared_payload_in_place), the inc peer of emit_nested_struct_shared_rc_decs_ex's Option[shared] rc-dec; (3) track_struct_var registers the COMBINED drop (emit_vec_elem_struct_with_shared_drop_fn = value-drop + shared-field rc-dec walker) for any struct owning shared fields — so a scope-exit drop of an owning struct LOCAL / callee-owned by-value PARAM rc-decs its shared children (the value drop skips them by design), also fixing the pre-existing direct-shared-field struct-LOCAL leak; plus the fresh-temp struct-arg gate (call_dispatch) now recognizes a shared-owning struct (invisible to type_expr_has_drop_heap). Un-ignored asan_attr_node_list_drop_consume_and_plain (240 B/6 -> 0) + new asan_b28_option_shared_and_direct_shared_struct_drop_no_leak. codegen 2040/0, macOS ASAN 521/0, Linux LSan 521/0 (the un-ignored test passes on the authoritative gate). RESIDUALS split to B-2026-07-04-9 (piece (b) proved NOT needed for the port shape but IS needed for entry-copy-then-whole-drop of a Vec[struct] field — a pre-existing double-free; plus the direct-shared FRESH-TEMP arg leak). FIXED.

`<iter>.collect()` under `karac build` rejected (loudly) adaptor chains beyond the `map`/`filter` subset landed in B-2026-07-03-25 (009fd479). FIXED 76be2de2 for the STATEFUL ELEMENT-TYPE-PRESERVING PASSTHROUGH family: `take`, `skip`, `step_by`, `take_while`, `skip_while`, `inspect`. `try_compile_iter_adaptor_collect_to_vec` now peels these too and lowers each to its for-loop equivalent with a pre-loop state var (counter + bound count for the integer-arg adaptors; a `skipping` latch for `skip_while`); the short-circuiting `take`/`take_while` emit an IN-BODY `break` at the adaptor's chain position — AFTER the upstream stages, matching the interpreter's lazy step order (iter_eval.rs): the element that trips exhaustion has run every upstream stage (e.g. a preceding `inspect`) but no downstream one, so `inspect(f).take(2)` fires `f` 3× and `take_while`'s pred runs through the first failing element on BOTH surfaces. The loop var now takes the FIRST param-bearing stage's name (not just steps[0]) so a `.map(|s| s.len())` after a leading count adaptor keeps the heap element type. Verified run==build across every adaptor + mixed chains + interpreter-matching side-effect counts + heap `Vec[String]` sources; `leaks` clean at -O0 incl. the `take`/`take_while` early-break tail drop; codegen 2035/2035, par 153/153. RESIDUAL → B-2026-07-04-2: the multi-source / restructuring adaptors (`enumerate`, `zip`, `chain`, `flat_map`, `chunks`, `windows`, `scan`, `cycle`), a non-single-`Binding` map/filter arg (named-fn `.map(double)`, multi-param/destructuring closure), and the non-terminal-f-string-map carve-out all still fall through to the loud dispatch-fail (no miscompile).

A non-shared struct FIELD typed Vec[String] / Vec[Map] / Vec[Set] / Vec[Vec[..]] leaks each element's own heap (String char buffer, Map/Set buckets, inner-Vec buffer) when the owning struct is dropped or destructured. emit_struct_drop_synthesis's VecOrString arm (src/codegen/synth_drop.rs) freed only the outer {ptr,len,cap} buffer: vec_elem_agg_drop_for_type_expr returns None for a DIRECT String/Map/Set element (unlike the top-level FreeVecBuffer drain, which has bespoke inline vec-struct / Map / tuple branches). FIX: (1) synth_drop.rs falls back to the recursive drop family emit_drop_fn_for_type_expr for those element shapes (gated by elem_te_needs_direct_recursive_drain = String/str/Vec/VecDeque/Map/Set); (2) param_own.rs makes the by-value-param entry-copy element-DEEP for exactly those shapes (emit_vecstr_defensive_copy element-deep mode) to keep the copy-depth == drop-depth invariant documented in param_own.rs — else a caller-retained Node{tags:Vec[String]} double-frees the shared element buffers (test asan_by_value_struct_field_moveout). Surfaced building slice 3d-i (AttrNode.path Vec[String]); reduced the pinned attr_node leak 1434/36 -> 474/12 (remainder is Facet A / B-2026-07-03-28). EXCLUDED (still outer-only, tracked): a heap-bearing TUPLE element Vec[(.., String)] (the entry-copy can't deepen a tuple element, so deepening its drop alone would double-free a retained param). Regression tests: asan_struct_vec_string_field_plain_drop_drains_elements, asan_struct_vec_string_field_destructure_consume_clean, asan_struct_vec_map_field_plain_drop_drains_elements. (Renumbered from B-2026-07-03-29 at integration — collided with the parallel collect-adaptor residual entry.)

An `Option[<user enum/struct>]` field DESTRUCTURED into a local (`let A { value } = a`) and then MOVE-matched (`match value { Some(v) => f(v) | v.field, None => 0 }`) LEAKS the payload's inner heap (String/Vec buffer) — ~40 B per move-out, 6/6 in a Vec[A]-consuming loop under LSan/macOS `leaks`. SILENT (correct output; leak only). REVISED ROOT CAUSE (the original 'just free the orphaned box' framing was WRONG and INCOMPLETE): the leak is the payload's INNER heap, not the box, and it fires for BOTH boxed (`Option[Val]`, Val=tag+String>3 words) AND inline (`Option[Inner]`, Inner={String}=3 words) payloads. A plain `Vec[Option[Val]]` move-out (no wrapping struct) is CLEAN — so it is SPECIFIC to the B-2026-07-03-27 destructure-leaf: the `value` binding from `let A { value } = a` is tracked in `inline_option_agg_payload_vars` with a scope-exit `karac_drop_Option_<payload>` (EnumDrop), and the consuming-`Some(v)` suppressor (`suppress_inline_option_agg_payload_cleanup`, control_flow_match.rs) TAG-ZEROES the source to disarm that drop — to avoid a double-free when `v` is MOVED out. BUT under this compiler's CALLER-RETAINS param model (an owned heap enum/struct param is entry-DEEP-COPIED at the callee — the `p14e` copy in param_own.rs), `f(v)` does NOT consume `v`; the callee frees its own copy and the CALLER must free the original `v`. For a NORMAL Option local the source's own scope drop frees it (see clean `use_opt`); for the destructure-leaf the suppressor DISARMS that drop without registering any drop for `v`, so the original inner heap is orphaned. The suppressor is only correct when `v` GENUINELY escapes (return / struct-or-collection literal / outward assign / closure capture) — NOT when it is passed to an entry-copying fn (`f(v)`) or field-read (`v.field`). Freeing the box shell alone (a prototype) removes only the 32 B box leak and leaves the dominant inner-heap leak, so it is INSUFFICIENT. Correct fix needs an ESCAPE-AWARE gate on the suppressor that knows call-args are entry-copied (non-consuming) — i.e. the caller-retains param model, the SAME blocker as [[B-2026-07-03-28]] (getting it wrong the other way DOUBLE-FREES: misclassifying a true escape as non-escape frees the inner twice). Repro: `enum Val{Nothing,Ident(String)} struct A{value:Option[Val]} fn ident_len(v:Val)->i64{match v{Val.Ident(s)=>s.len(),Val.Nothing=>0}} fn use_a(a:A)->i64{let A{value}=a; match value{Some(v)=>ident_len(v),None=>0}}`; consume a Vec[A]. NON-BLOCKING (leak only, double-free-clean). BLOCKED on the caller-retains param model. FIXED (Phase 1 of the caller-retains model, 80229526): the BORROW case only. The use-site consumption classifier (src/codegen/consume_class.rs, `binding_only_borrowed`) now gates `suppress_inline_option_agg_payload_cleanup` via `arm_only_borrows_option_agg_payload`/`block_only_borrows_option_agg_payload` (shared `option_agg_payload_binds` + `collect_pattern_bindings`): the source payload drop is disarmed ONLY when the arm CONSUMES the bound payload, so a borrow-only arm (`Some(v)=>ident_len(v)`/`v.field`) keeps its drop and frees the inner heap. Wired for `match`, `if let`, `while let`; `let else` stays unconditional (bindings escape the analyzable scope). Conservative-default classifier = never a double-free. Regression `asan_b31_option_agg_payload_borrow_only_no_leak` (match+iflet+whilelet, boxed+inline payloads). codegen 2037/0, macOS ASAN 519/0, Linux LSan 519/0. RESIDUAL split off as B-2026-07-04-7: the ESCAPE case (`Some(v)=>v` returning the destructure-leaf payload) still leaks. FIXED.

SILENT wrong-output / crash under `karac build` (correct under `karac run` and `KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0`) when an owned `Column` / `DataFrame` / `Tensor` is produced in one AUTO-PAR branch and read after the join. MINIMAL: `fn hd(v: Vec[i64]) -> i64 { v[0] } fn main() { let av: Vec[i64]=[4,2,7,1]; print(hd(av)); let c: Column[i64]=Column.from_vec([5,9,3,1]); print(c.len()); }` — build prints `4` then `0` (c.len()==0, should be 4); element access panics out-of-bounds. Two such producers with a while-loop String-building consumer CRASH (SIGBUS). ROOT CAUSE (corrected — the original `materialize_owned_temp` + staged-String-accumulator suspect was WRONG; the String accumulator and the by-value-temp arg were RED HERRINGS, they only served to make two statements auto-parallelizable): the auto-par analyzer groups the independent read (`hd(av)`) and the `Column`-producing `let` into sibling par branches. The producing branch writes the column control-block pointer into the parent's return slot (`emit_par_branch_fn` slot-write) and THEN ran its queued `FreeColumn` at branch end, freeing the three buffers (data / null-bitmap / control) it had just published. After `karac_par_run` joined, the parent read a DANGLING control block. The branch-side slot-source suppression already TRANSFERRED Map/File/Enum/Struct/User/SoA handle cleanups to the parent via `SlotOwnership` (publish-and-forget: remove the branch action, parent re-registers against its own alloca) — `FreeColumn` / `FreeDataFrame` / `FreeTensor` were simply missing from that retain loop AND from `register_slot_ownership`. FIX (52a454c3): added the three `SlotOwnership` variants + retain-loop transfer arms + parent re-registration, mirroring the existing owned-handle transfers; honored by both parent rebind sites (compile_par_block Step 6 + auto-par dispatch in stmts.rs). AFFECTED every Column/DataFrame/Tensor produced in an auto-par branch and consumed after the join (silent since the codegen E2E harness passes `None` concurrency and never fires auto-par). Regression: par_codegen run==build parity + memory_sanitizer full-pipeline ASAN/LSan (UAF + leak); full LSan suite 507/507. (Renumbered from B-2026-07-03-31 at integration.)

Facet A step 1 (FIXED): a struct FIELD typed `Option[String]` / `Option[Vec[..]]` (inline `{ptr,len,cap}` payload) leaked its `Some` payload when the struct was PLAIN-dropped or consumed (a `Vec[A]` element, a scope-exit drop). emit_struct_drop_synthesis deliberately excluded Option fields, and a prior prototype double-freed because Option-bearing structs were caller-retains (param_own bailed on Option). FIX makes such structs CALLEE-OWNED: (1) param_own.rs field_copy_supported now admits an inline-payload Option, and deep_copy_option_inline_payload_in_place duplicates the Some buffer type-aware off the field TypeExpr (element-deep for a Vec[String] payload, mirroring emit_option_drop_fn); (2) emit_struct_drop_synthesis frees Option fields via a new `OptionInline` FieldDrop, GATED on aggregate_param_copy_supported_struct(struct) = the same predicate that makes the struct entry-copied, so copy == drop and there is never a caller-retains shallow-shared instance to double-free; (3) destructure (finish_owned_struct_destructure), match (suppress_destructured_struct_pattern_cleanup), whole-struct move (zero_struct_move_caps), and field-access move-out (zero_struct_field_move_cap) all neutralize the moved Option by zeroing its tag to None (new zero_option_field_tag_at), and the destructure leaf tracks its own inline-Option cleanup. Verified LSan-clean (no leak) + ASAN-clean (no double-free) on plain-drop, destructure+match-consume, destructure-unused, and field-move-out; full memory_sanitizer suite green (no regressions). Reduced the pinned attr_node leak 474/12 -> 240/6 (AttrNode.string_value fixed; AttrArgNode Options are the residual B-2026-07-03-28). Regression tests: asan_option_field_{plain_drop_freed, destructure_match_consume_clean, destructure_unused_freed, moveout_clean}.

A Tensor declared with a NARROW numeric element type (i8/i16/i32, u8/u16/u32, f32) and built via `Tensor.from([...])` stores its elements at 8 bytes each, but every reader strides at the annotated narrow width (`tensor_elem_size` → 4 for i32/f32), so element reads land on the wrong bytes — SILENT wrong output under `karac build`, correct under `karac run`. MINIMAL (build-only, silent): `fn main() { let t: Tensor[i32,[4]] = Tensor.from([40,10,30,20]); print(t[0]); print(t[1]); print(t[2]); print(t[3]); print(t.sum()); }` — build prints `40 0 10 0` and sum `50` (reading [40,0,10,0]); run prints `40 10 30 20` and sum `100`. Root: the `Tensor.from` literal lowering writes i64/f64 8-byte values into the data buffer regardless of the narrow annotation, while `t[i]`, `t.sum()`, and all C-order readers GEP at `tensor_elem_size(elem)` (native narrow width) — an 8-byte-write / 4-byte-read stride mismatch (classic `0 0 10 40`-after-sort signature). AFFECTS all narrow-element tensor ops under build (indexing, sum/mean/prod, map/fold/zip, and — the surfacing case — sorted/argsort, which are therefore kept i64/f64-only under `karac build` and reject narrow elements LOUDLY; Column, which stores elements at native width, is unaffected and DID gain full-width sorted/argsort in this slice). PRE-EXISTING and independent of the sort work (reproduces with plain `t[i]` / `t.sum()`, code the S6c slices never touched). FIX likely at the `Tensor.from` element-store lowering (narrow the literal to `elem` before storing, OR store 8-byte uniformly and widen `tensor_elem_size` to 8 for all numeric elems + narrow on read-out). Unblocks the widened tensor sorted/argsort path (the kernel widening helpers `sort_widen_value`/`sort_build_vec_from_keys`/`sort_widen_data_buffer` already exist and handle every width — they are wired for Column and ready for Tensor once storage is correct). FIXED 98ae6e12: `compile_tensor_from` now threads the declared element type via `pending_let_tensor_info` (rank-guarded; unannotated falls back to the leaf width) and coerces each leaf with `coerce_scalar_to_type` before the store, so the write stride matches every reader's `tensor_elem_size(info.elem)`. Regression covers i32/i16/u8/u32/f32 reads + reductions, negative sign-extension on read-back, a 2-D narrow tensor, and the i64/f64 no-op widths. The widened tensor sorted/argsort follow-on (narrow elements still rejected loudly there) is now UNBLOCKED but not wired in this fix. (Renumbered from B-2026-07-03-34 at integration — collided with the FIXED reduce.rs modulo-BCE-preflight entry; the fix commit 98ae6e12 predates the renumber and references the old id.) FOLLOW-ON COMPLETION (9cce1610): the 98ae6e12 fix coerced `Tensor.from` leaves but left three gaps — (a) `Tensor.full`'s fill was stored uncoerced (an f32 `full([2], 2.5)` read back 0.0); (b) an integer literal in a FLOAT tensor (`Tensor[f64] = Tensor.from([1,2,3])`) needs int->float `sitofp`, which `coerce_scalar_to_type` omits; (c) `Tensor.mean` over an f32 tensor failed module verification (`fdiv f32, f64`). Fixed all three (shared `coerce_scalar_to_tensor_elem` helper for from+full incl. sitofp; the mean fold fpext's the f32 sum to f64) AND lifted the S6c tensor sorted/argsort narrow-width rejection — they now sort every numeric width via the widened scratch sort (only u64 rejected). Regression: codegen `test_e2e_tensor_narrow_element_storage_and_ops` + `test_e2e_tensor_sorted_argsort_narrow_widths` + reworked `_narrow_width_rejected_loudly`; interpreter `tensor_narrow_element_storage_and_sort_reduction`; memory_sanitizer `asan_tensor_sorted_argsort_narrow_widths_no_leak`.

SILENT seq-vs-auto-par divergence that CRASHES: a valid program panics `vec index out of bounds` under the DEFAULT `karac build` (auto-par on) while `karac run` and `KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0` are correct. TRIGGER: a reduction body indexes a captured fixed-size `Array[N x T]` by a modulo of the loop var. MINIMAL: `fn f(a: i64, b: i64) -> i64 { a*100i64+b } fn main() { let m: i64=4i64; let atab: Array[i64,4]=[10,20,30,40]; let btab: Array[i64,4]=[1,2,3,4]; let mut total=0i64; let mut k=0i64; while k<100000i64 { let idx=k%m; total=total+f(atab[idx],btab[idx]); k=k+1i64; } println(total); }` -> build traps, run/seq print 250250000. ROOT CAUSE: a follow-on to the by-pointer array capture B-2026-06-15-3 (c3050fc8). The auto-par reduction worker's modulo bounds-check-elision preflight (`emit_modulo_bce_preflight`) is a Vec-ONLY optimization: it loads `{ptr,len,cap}` field 1 as the runtime length to prove `captured[k % upper]` in-bounds once at entry. But a by-pointer array capture has NO header -- its VarSlot points straight at the inline elements -- so `build_struct_gep({ptr,i64,i64}, ptr %atab, 1)` reads element[1] as the bogus "length" and the `len < upper` check traps on garbage (`btab=[1,2,3,4]` -> element[1]=2 < upper 4 -> false panic). The per-iter access itself was already a correct static-N `[N x T]` check; only the hoisted preflight was wrong. Constant-index array access and `Vec[i64]` tables (real runtime length) were both fine, which is why the seq lane and non-modulo shapes never showed it. FIX (99617752): `find_modulo_hoistable_bounds` drops any bound whose captured base has an ArrayType VarSlot -- arrays carry a compile-time-constant length, so the per-iter static-N bounds check suffices; only Vec captures get the preflight + BCE elision. Regression: par_codegen IR-pin (worker has no `bce.len` / `vec index out of bounds`, keeps `getelementptr [4 x i64]`) + e2e (250250000). par_codegen 155 + codegen 2029 + memory_sanitizer 511 green; clippy + fmt clean.

A `Vec[…]` literal (`compile_vec_prefix_literal`) whose element is an f-string (`Vec[f"…"]`) or an identifier String/Vec (`let a=…; Vec[a]`) DOUBLE-FREED the element buffer: heap-use-after-free when the element is read, SIGTRAP/exit 133 double-free at the Vec's drop. FIXED 3c96c8e9. ROOT CAUSE: the Vec-literal element loop bit-copied each value into the heap buffer but — unlike `compile_tuple` — never suppressed the element's OWN scope-exit cleanup, so both that cleanup and the Vec's owner freed the same pointer. An f-string element's accumulator queues a FreeVecBuffer via `track_vec_var` (exprs.rs InterpolatedStringLit arm); an identifier String/Vec carries its binding cleanup. FIX: mirror `compile_tuple`'s move-aware per-element suppression — `suppress_fstr_acc_if_moved_out` (called right after each element's compile_expr, while `last_fstr_acc` still holds THIS element's acc — the single slot is overwritten by the next element) + `suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg` + the Map move-suppress for identifier elements; all no-op for fresh temps (`.to_string()`/`.clone()`) and POD. NOTE: the original entry's 'single-element / return-only' narrowing was a TESTING ARTIFACT — the earlier multi-element probes only read `.len()` (a freed element buffer isn't touched by a length read) so the UAF didn't manifest; reading an element exposes it for ANY count, in `main` or via `return`. Verified run==build + leak/double-free-clean (macOS ASAN + `leaks` -O0 + Linux LeakSanitizer) across f-string/identifier/to_string/clone/mixed elements; regression test `asan_single_element_fstring_vec_return_no_double_free`. Surfaced writing B-2026-07-03-29's leak test.

`<iter>.collect()` under `karac build` residual after map/filter (B-2026-07-03-25) + stateful-passthrough (B-2026-07-03-29). PARTIAL: `enumerate()` LANDED 024bfe72 (POD) + 210eb93b (TERMINAL heap element, `Vec[(i64, String)]` when enumerate is the LAST adaptor — the tuple is pushed straight into the result; works with skip/filter/take BEFORE it). A NON-terminal enumerate whose collected element is heap-bearing (`enumerate().map(|p| p.1)`, `enumerate().filter().collect()`) is gated to the loud dispatch-fail (B-2026-07-04-4). STILL OPEN — all fall through to the dispatch-fail (no miscompile): (1) MULTI-SOURCE / restructuring adaptors `zip`/`chain`/`flat_map`/`chunks`/`windows`/`scan`/`cycle` (need multi/nested loops); (2) a map/filter/… arg that is NOT a single-`Binding`-param closure (named fn `.map(double)`, multi-param/destructuring closure incl. `enumerate().map(|(i,x)| …)`); (3) a NON-TERMINAL `map` whose body is an f-string (needs the poisoned staged-accumulator, B-2026-07-03-25). Renumbered residual of B-2026-07-03-29. (4) a PLAIN `.iter().collect()` with NO map/filter/enumerate adaptor (identity collect, `steps.is_empty()` → dispatch-fail): loud-fails for both fresh-temp (`method 'iter' not supported`) and local (`no handler for 'collect' on non-identifier receiver`) sources — found probing B-2026-07-04-5. Low severity (rare; a no-op copy). SUB-PART (4) FIXED 59e2d892 (2026-07-04): when the peeled adaptor `steps` are empty over a no-arg `.iter()` source, `try_compile_iter_adaptor_collect_to_vec` injects a synthetic identity `map(|x| x)` so the shared pipeline lowers it exactly like the verified `<src>.iter().map(|x| x).collect()` shape — a fresh Vec of element CLONES (`.iter()` borrows, so the source survives; independent buffers). Gated to a no-arg `.iter()` base, so `.into_iter()` (MOVE semantics, unhandled by this clone lowering), ranges, bare iterator vars, and unhandled-adaptor bases keep bailing to the loud dispatch-fail (no miscompile). Verified run==build + macOS ASAN 523/0 + Linux LSan 523/0 for POD/heap over local (source survives) AND fresh-temp (heap freed after cloning) sources; regressions `e2e_iter_adaptor_identity_collect_to_vec_codegen` + `asan_b04_2_identity_collect_no_leak`. SUB-PART (2) NAMED-FN HALF FIXED 96861edb (2026-07-04): a named-function `map`/`filter`/`take_while`/`skip_while`/`inspect` arg (a bare `Identifier` `double` or qualified `Path` `math.sq`) is now wrapped in a synthetic single-param body `<fn>(p)`, lowering identically to `.map(|x| double(x))`; the synthetic param name is chain-depth-disambiguated (`__mfp_<counter>_<steps.len()>`) so multiple named-fn stages don't collide. Verified run==build + macOS ASAN 524/0 + Linux LSan 524/0 across POD map/filter, filter().map() chains, mixed named-fn+closure, two named-fn stages, type-changing map to Vec[String], and a named fn CONSUMING a heap element (source survives); regressions `e2e_iter_adaptor_named_fn_map_filter_collect_codegen` + `asan_b04_2_named_fn_map_collect_no_leak`. Residual sub-parts (1) multi-source adaptors, (2) the MULTI-PARAM/DESTRUCTURING closure half (`enumerate().map(|(i,x)| …)`), (3) non-terminal f-string map, and identity collect over `.into_iter()`/ranges stay OPEN. SUB-PART (2) DESTRUCTURING HALF FIXED 1f01cdf (2026-07-07): a tuple-destructuring `map`/`filter`/... closure param (`enumerate().map(|(i,x)| ...)`, `pairs.iter().map(|(a,b)| ...)`) now lowers under `karac build`. At the peel site, a `PatternKind::Tuple` of `Binding`/`_` sub-patterns binds a fresh `__dp` to the element and desugars into leading `let`s in a block body -- `|__dp| { let i = __dp.0; let x = __dp.1; <body> }` -- reusing the single-`Binding` pipeline verbatim (`__dp.k` is an ordinary TupleIndex; normal block scoping handles shadowing). Nested/complex sub-patterns (`|((a,b),c)|`, literals) still bail to the loud dispatch-fail. The block-desugar is standard Kara so it adds no miscompile the hand-written form lacks: heap keep-element (`|(i,s)| s` over Vec[String]) moves the String field out and drops the index, verified leak-clean. Works over enumerate sources, direct Vec[(T,U)] tuple sources, filter, and wildcard sub-patterns. Verified run==build POD/heap/filter/wildcard/direct-tuple; regressions e2e_iter_adaptor_destructuring_closure_collect_codegen + asan_b04_2_destructuring_closure_heap_collect_no_leak. Sub-part 2 is now COMPLETE (named-fn 96861edb + destructuring 1f01cdf). Residual sub-parts (1) multi-source adaptors (zip/chain/flat_map/chunks/windows/scan/cycle), (3) non-terminal f-string map, and identity collect over `.into_iter()`/ranges stay OPEN. SUB-PART (4) RANGE HALF FIXED 211b0cf (2026-07-07): a BOUNDED integer range collected with no adaptor (`(a..b).collect()`/`(a..=b).collect()`) now lowers under `karac build`. The identity-collect path (synthetic `map(|x| x)` over empty steps) now accepts a `Range { start: Some, end: Some }` source alongside `.iter()` -- `for x in a..b` yields owned POD integers, so the identity map is a plain copy with no borrowed source to alias/free. Unbounded ranges (`a..`) are a typecheck error (no `collect`) and never reach codegen; empty ranges yield an empty Vec. Verified run==build exclusive/inclusive/variable-bound/empty; regression e2e_iter_adaptor_range_identity_collect_codegen. Residual of sub-part 4: identity collect over `.into_iter()` (MOVE semantics) stays OPEN, alongside sub-parts (1) multi-source adaptors and (3) non-terminal f-string map. SUB-PART (1) CHAIN HALF FIXED dbc698f (2026-07-07): `A.chain(B).collect()` where A and B are each a plain identity source (no-arg `.iter()` or a bounded range) now lowers under `karac build`. A contained early branch in `try_compile_iter_adaptor_collect_to_vec` emits the identity-collect loop once per source into a shared accumulator (`for x in A { acc.push x }; for y in B { acc.push y }`) -- same clone semantics as a single identity collect, so both borrowed sources survive and the accumulator owns independent copies. It does not touch the main pipeline's single-base structure. A chain side carrying its OWN adaptor, a nested chain, or a non-identity source still bails to the loud dispatch-fail (never a miscompile). Verified run==build POD/heap/range+iter; regressions e2e_iter_adaptor_chain_identity_collect_codegen + asan_b04_2_chain_identity_heap_collect_no_leak. Residual sub-part 1: chain sides carrying adaptors (needs per-side pipelines into a shared accumulator) and the other multi-source adaptors (zip/flat_map/chunks/windows/scan/cycle) stay OPEN, alongside sub-part (3) non-terminal f-string map and identity collect over `.into_iter()`. SUB-PART (1) ZIP HALF FIXED f5c5a44 (2026-07-07): `A.iter().zip(B.iter()).collect()` for POD element pairs now lowers under `karac build`. A contained early branch extracts the two indexable bases and emits an index loop pairing them element-wise up to the shorter length into a Vec[(EA,EB)] (`while i < len_a && i < len_b { acc.push((A[i], B[i])); i += 1 }`); the POD copies leave both sources intact. GATED TO POD: a heap-bearing pair (`(String, i64)`) is NOT sound through this index lowering -- `A[i]` reads the heap element into the pushed tuple in a way that leaks/aliases the source buffer (caught by ASAN/LSan) -- so it bails to the loud dispatch-fail. A copy-free HEAP zip needs the per-element move plumbing the enumerate path uses and stays OPEN under sub-part 1, alongside a downstream adaptor after zip (`zip().map(...)`), non-`.iter()` zip sources, chain sides carrying adaptors, and the other multi-source adaptors (flat_map/chunks/windows/scan/cycle). Verified run==build POD unequal-length/min-cutoff; regression e2e_iter_adaptor_zip_identity_collect_codegen. SUB-PART (4) INTO_ITER HALF FIXED d615dd6 (2026-07-07): `<local>.into_iter().collect()` (no adaptor) now lowers under `karac build`. Kara's into_iter().collect() is NON-consuming here (the ownership checker leaves the source valid; `for x in <src>.into_iter()` already lowers like `.iter()` per control_flow_for.rs), so it is the exact same clone lowering as `.iter()` -- source survives, result owns element clones. The identity-source gate now accepts a no-arg `.into_iter()` alongside `.iter()`. (The adaptor case `into_iter().map(f).collect()` already worked via the for-loop's into_iter handling.) Verified run==build POD/heap (source survives); regressions e2e_iter_adaptor_into_iter_identity_collect_codegen + asan_b04_2_into_iter_identity_collect_no_leak. NOTE: this corrects the earlier ledger claim that into_iter has MOVE semantics the clone lowering wouldn't honor -- in practice the ownership checker treats it as non-consuming, so the clone lowering is correct. Residual sub-part 1 (heap zip, zip/chain-with-adaptors, flat_map/chunks/windows/scan/cycle) and sub-part 3 (non-terminal f-string map) stay OPEN. SUB-PART (1) FLAT_MAP HALF FIXED 586e521 (2026-07-07): `<outer>.flat_map(|v| v.iter()).collect()` now lowers under `karac build`. A contained early branch emits nested loops `for param in <outer> { for x in <inner> { acc.push(x) } }` -- the closure param IS the outer loop var so the inner iterable (which references param) resolves. Scoped to an identity <outer> (.iter()/.into_iter()/bounded range) and an identity <inner> (closure body is a .iter()/.into_iter() call or a bounded range). Iteration-based (not indexed), so `push` clones -- HEAP-SAFE (verified leak-clean on Vec[Vec[String]]), unlike the index-based zip. A complex inner (`|v| v.iter().map(...)`), a downstream adaptor after flat_map, a multi-param closure, or a non-identity outer/inner bails to the loud dispatch-fail. Verified run==build POD/heap/into_iter-inner; regressions e2e_iter_adaptor_flat_map_collect_codegen + asan_b04_2_flat_map_heap_collect_no_leak. Residual sub-part 1: heap zip (index-clone blocked by synthetic-AST span-type limitation), zip/chain/flat_map sides carrying adaptors, and chunks/windows/scan/cycle stay OPEN, alongside sub-part 3 (non-terminal f-string map). SUB-PART (1) CHUNKS/WINDOWS PROBED, BLOCKED (2026-07-07): `chunks(n)`/`windows(n)` collect (`Vec[Vec[T]]`) works under `karac run` but the natural codegen lowering is UNSAFE. Two shapes both fail ASAN: (a) an accumulator loop `for x in outer { chunk.push(x); if chunk.len()==n { acc.push(chunk); chunk = Vec.new() } }` DOUBLE-FREES -- `acc.push(chunk)` copies the sub-Vec struct but `chunk = Vec.new()` then drops the moved-out buffer that acc now owns (verified: ASAN double-free on Vec[Vec[String]] AND Vec[Vec[i64]] since the sub-Vec buffer is heap regardless of element POD-ness); (b) an index-based outer loop reintroduces the same `<container>[i]` heap-index leak that gated POD-only zip. Both trace to the SAME move-into-aggregate ownership gap as heap zip and B-2026-07-05-1 -- `acc.push(<moved binding or indexed heap elem>)` does not suppress the source's drop. So chunks/windows are gated to the loud dispatch-fail (never a miscompile) pending the move-semantics fix (B-2026-07-04-4/05-1 family), NOT a fresh lowering. This exhausts the SAFELY-lowerable sub-part-1 adaptors: enumerate, chain-identity, zip-POD, and flat_map (iteration-based, heap-safe) are DONE; heap zip, chunks, windows, and adaptor-carrying chain/zip/flat_map sides all need the shared move-into-aggregate fix; scan is unreliable under `run` (out of scope) and cycle is unbounded. OPEN. HEAP-ZIP LEG FIXED 84e64cf (2026-07-07) + GENERAL heap-index-read-into-owning-sink double-free fixed same commit. Root finding: reading a heap element by index from a named Vec (`v[i]`, element String/Vec/…) and moving it into an OWNING sink — tuple literal `(v[i],…)`, `push(v[i])`, struct field `{f: v[i]}`, owned call arg, return — shallow-aliased the container's element buffer (compile_vec_index only loads the {ptr,len,cap} header), so BOTH the container's element-drop and the sink's owner freed it (double-free, exit 133). This was a real miscompile even in HAND-WRITTEN code (`(a[i],b[i])`, `d.push(a[i])`), not just the synthetic collect lowerings; the `let s = v[i]` binding path already deep-cloned via clone_owned_vec_index_element, so the fix routes heap index-reads through that SAME clone at the shared owning-sink hook maybe_defensive_copy_param_arg (scoped to a named-Vec, non-range, non-trivially-copyable index; source left intact -> distinct buffers). That closes the earlier 'synthetic-AST span-type limitation' framing: the clone keys on codegen's own var_elem_type_exprs, NOT typechecker spans, so it fires for synthetic index-reads too. With it, the zip lowering now admits a HEAP element pair when both `.iter()` bases are clone-eligible named Vecs (else keeps bailing loud). Verified run==build + LSan-clean over Vec[String] pairs and hand-written tuple/push/struct-field index-reads; regressions e2e_iter_adaptor_zip_heap_collect_codegen + e2e_heap_vec_index_read_into_owning_sinks (codegen), asan_b04_2_zip_heap_collect_no_leak + asan_heap_vec_index_read_into_sinks_no_double_free (memory_sanitizer). STILL OPEN: chunks/windows (blocked on the DISTINCT synthetic-move gap — `acc.push(chunk); chunk = Vec.new()` reassignment-drop suppression needs typechecker move analysis the synthetic AST lacks; the index-read clone only covers the inner `chunk.push(v[j])`), zip/chain/flat_map sides carrying their OWN adaptors, scan (unreliable under run), cycle (unbounded), and sub-part 3 (non-terminal f-string map). CHUNKS/WINDOWS RE-PROBED (2026-07-07, post heap-index-read fix 84e64cf): the picture is now finer than the earlier 'move-into-aggregate' framing. A nested-loop lowering with a FRESH per-iteration sub-Vec binding (`while start<len { let mut chunk=Vec.new(); while j<end&&j<len { chunk.push(base[j]); j+=1 }; acc.push(chunk); start+=step }`) is the right shape and AVOIDS the old `chunk=Vec.new()` reassignment double-free. Its two sub-frees are now individually sound: `chunk.push(base[j])` deep-clones the heap element (84e64cf) and `acc.push(chunk)` move-suppresses a FRESH nested Vec[String] — both verified ASAN-clean in isolation (minimal `outer.push(inner)` with cloned index-reads, and a SINGLE-chunk `chunks(2)` over a 2-elem Vec[String], both LSan-clean). BUT a MULTI-iteration chunks (>=2 outer iters) double-frees a CLONED String under ASAN (exit 23) while `karac run` and -O2 AOT mask it. Bisected: single outer iteration clean, two+ iterations crash -> the fault is the synthetic while-body's per-iteration `let mut chunk` reusing one entry-block alloca across iterations, so the move-suppression (cap=0 on the slot at acc.push) interacts wrong with the next iteration's re-init / the accumulated scope-exit cleanups on the shared slot (the sub-Vec's element Strings get freed by BOTH the stale per-iteration cleanup and acc's recursive drop). This is a SYNTHETIC-lowering scope-drain issue (a loop-local heap binding whose alloca is reused and whose cleanup isn't drained per-iteration in the generated AST), NOT the ownership move gap and NOT the index-read clone.
