source: selfhost:parser-runtime — isolated while investigating B-2026-07-09-12's render corruption
tracker: selfhost/src/parser.kara::parse_block (Stmt.Exp(ExprStmt) push) + ast_render.kara::render_stmt; codegen packing/reconstruction of a nested shared field in a Vec element of a wide shared-enum payload
title: SILENT WRONG-VALUE miscompile (NOT a crash / UAF — distinct from B-2026-07-09-12): the self-hosted parser's block STATEMENT expressions read back as a garbage `Error` node under AOT while the interpreter is correct. `{ a; }` renders as `(block @0:6 (exprstmt @2:1 (error @1:1)))` COMPILED but `(block @0:6 (exprstmt @2:1 (ident a @2:1)))` under INTERP — a deterministic interp!=compiled codegen divergence, exit 0, no heap corruption. ISOLATED via a block-form bisect (selfhost project, driver calling parse_and_print): `{ }` (empty) OK, `{ a }` (tail-only) OK, `{ a; }` (one Exp stmt) -> stmt expr is `(error @1:1)`, `{ a; b }` -> stmt WRONG + tail `(ident b)` CORRECT, `{ a; b; }` -> both stmts WRONG. So the TAIL (`Option[Expr]`, a direct shared handle) round-trips correctly, but a STATEMENT expr (`Block.stmts: Vec[Stmt]` -> `Stmt::Exp(ExprStmt)` -> `ExprStmt { expr: Expr }`, a bare shared handle NESTED in a plain-enum payload inside a Vec element inside the wide `Expr.Blk(Block)` shared box) reads back as a consistent bogus `Error` variant with span @1:1. The exprstmt's OWN span (@2:1, from `expr_span(e)` computed just before the move at parser.kara:590) is CORRECT — only the moved `expr` field is corrupted, so the defect is in the packing/reconstruction (word-offset) of that nested shared field, NOT its span. The consistent (non-random, non-crashing) wrong value points to a FIELD-OFFSET / word-width packing miscompile in `coerce_to_payload_words`/`reconstruct_payload_value` for a bare shared field nested inside a plain-enum struct payload carried as a Vec element of a wide (19-word / 152-byte) shared-enum payload — the '#44 true-word-width' recursion path — NOT the entry-copy rc-inc / clone-on-extract / nested-drop family B-2026-07-09-12 covers (whose failures CRASH with heap corruption, exit 139).
investigation: 2026-07-10: interp-vs-compiled discriminator (built the selfhost span/token/lexer/ast/parser/ast_render project, ran the same main.kara under `karac build`+exec vs `karac run --interp`) proved interp correct / compiled wrong => codegen, not parser-logic/grammar. Block-form bisect localized it to `Vec[Stmt]` statement exprs (tail `Option[Expr]` is fine). MINIMAL-REPRO ATTEMPTS DID NOT TRIGGER IT (so the standalone repro is still owed): faithful standalone `.kara` programs replicating the exact shapes — `shared enum Expr { Ident(IdentExpr), Blk(Block), Wide(Big), Error(Span) }`, `struct ExprStmt { expr: Expr, span: Span }`, `enum Stmt { Exp(ExprStmt) }`, `struct Block { stmts: Vec[Stmt], tail: Option[Expr], span }`, with the parse-like `let e = ...; let sp = expr_span(e); stmts.push(Stmt.Exp(ExprStmt { expr: e, span: sp })); Expr.Blk(Block { stmts, tail: None, span })` construction AND the real render pattern (render_block destructures + for-loop + render_stmt destructures) — ALL round-trip CORRECTLY (interp==compiled==`block[aaa;]`), including a 19-word-wide `Expr` (18-field `Big` variant) to match the real 152-byte layout. So the trigger needs something more specific in the real parser than the isolated shape+width: candidates are the `mut ref self` parser-state threading, the `parse_expr_bp(0)` recursive-descent construction path (vs a literal), or an interaction with the full 25-variant `Expr` / other node structs' layout. NEXT: instrument or bisect the real parser (progressively simplify parser.kara/ast.kara until `{ a; }` goes green) to get a standalone repro, then fix the packing/reconstruction word-offset for the nested shared field.
resolution: MINIMAL REPRO FOUND 2026-07-10 (15 lines, interp==Ident / compiled crashes) + LOCALIZED to the STRUCT LET-BINDING, not pack/render: `struct IdentExpr { name: String, val: i64 } shared enum Expr { Ident(IdentExpr), Blk(Block) } struct ExprStmt { expr: Expr } enum Stmt { Exp(ExprStmt) } struct Block { stmts: Vec[Stmt], tail: Option[Expr], span: i64 }` with `fn mk_expr() -> Expr { let mut stmts=Vec.new(); stmts.push(Stmt.Exp(ExprStmt { expr: Expr.Ident(IdentExpr{name,val}) })); let block = Block { stmts, tail: None, span: 0 }; Expr.Blk(block) }` then reading `b.stmts[0]` back CRASHES compiled, correct under interp. EXACT TRIGGER (bisected by add-back): ALL THREE needed — (1) the outer struct is LET-BOUND before use (`let block = Block{..}; Expr.Blk(block)` crashes; the INLINE `Expr.Blk(Block{..})` WORKS); (2) the struct has an `Option[SHARED]` field (`tail: Option[Expr]`) — `Option[i64]` in the same slot WORKS, and dropping the tail field entirely WORKS; (3) plus the `Vec[Stmt]` field that reads back corrupt. A probe of `block.stmts[0]` placed RIGHT AFTER `let block = ..` and BEFORE the `Expr.Blk` pack already misbehaves (stack-overflow/garbage), so the corruption is in CONSTRUCTING/STORING the let-bound local `Block` (compile_struct_init writing the struct literal into the block alloca), NOT in the shared-enum pack (`coerce_to_payload_words`) or the reconstruct/render. HYPOTHESIS: a struct-field-layout / offset mismatch when a struct carries a NICHE-optimized `Option[shared]` field (1 pointer word, null=None) — the alloca-store field-init path (GEP by field, compile_struct_init) computes a different width/offset for the `Option[shared]` field than the struct's LLVM type / the SSA insertvalue path uses (which is why the INLINE literal, built via insertvalue, is fine but the let-bound alloca store corrupts the neighboring `Vec` field). Repro saved at examples-scratch B-2026-07-10-1-min.kara. NEXT: inspect compile_struct_init's field offset/width handling for a niche `Option[shared]` field vs the registered struct LLVM layout; the codegen (call_dispatch.rs::coerce_to_payload_words / control_flow_match.rs::reconstruct_payload_value) are likely NOT the culprit — struct construction/layout is. LOCALIZATION SHARPENED 2026-07-10 (corrects the earlier 'compile_struct_init' guess): let-binding the struct ALONE is CLEAN — `let block = Block{stmts,tail:None,span}; <read block.stmts[0]>` with NO shared-enum pack round-trips correctly (interp==compiled). The corruption is specifically the PACK of a LET-BOUND (alloca-LOADED) struct into the shared-enum payload: `Expr.Blk(block)` CRASHES but `Expr.Blk(Block{..})` (INLINE SSA literal, same fields) WORKS. So the culprit is `coerce_to_payload_words` (call_dispatch.rs:4324) / the oversize-BOX decision (`out.len() > num_words`) / `reconstruct_payload_value` coherence — the #44/#49 lineage — behaving DIFFERENTLY for a struct VALUE loaded from an alloca (let-bound) vs a fresh insertvalue aggregate (inline), when the struct carries a NICHE `Option[shared]` field. Hypothesis: the loaded-from-alloca Block's `Option[Expr]` field arrives with a different LLVM width/representation (niche 1-word ptr vs conventional 4-word Option) than the inline-built one, so `llvm_type_word_count` yields a different field word count, flipping the inline-vs-BOX decision between the pack of the two forms while the unpack (`reconstruct_payload_value`) assumes one — the classic pack/unpack box-decision mismatch (#44) but triggered by the alloca-load representation of a niche Option field, not the field count. This is a codegen PACKING bug (not struct-init, not memory-safety/drop). Next: dump + diff the IR of `Expr.Blk(block)` vs `Expr.Blk(Block{..})` for the same fields, focus on the `tail` field's extracted width and the `out.len()` vs `num_words` box branch; make the box decision + reconstruct consult the SAME niche-aware width for a loaded value. CORRECTION 2026-07-10 (refutes the niche-mismatch hypothesis): the non-shared struct's `Option[shared]` field IS a CONVENTIONAL 4-word slot — compile_struct_init WIDENS a niche ptr into it (exprs.rs:2101, `widen_niche_option_ptr_to_field`, B-2026-07-09-11), consistently for BOTH the inline and let-bound forms. So the built Block VALUE is identical either way; the struct layout is NOT the bug. The true minimal condition is that BOTH the let-binding AND the pack are required: V4 (let-bind + `Expr.Blk(block)`) CRASHES, but V5 (let-bind + read, NO pack) WORKS and V4_inline (`Expr.Blk(Block{..})`, no let) WORKS. So the defect is the INTERACTION of the let-bound local's cleanup TRACKING + MOVE-SUPPRESSION with the enum pack: when `block` (a tracked local carrying a `Vec[Stmt]` cleanup AND an `Option[shared]` cleanup) is MOVED into `Expr.Blk(block)`, the move-suppression (`suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg` / the Option[shared] move handling) and the `coerce_to_payload_words` read of the just-suppressed local interact and corrupt the `Vec[Stmt]` field's words — plausibly the suppression zeroing a word at an offset miscomputed because of the 4-word Option field between the Vec (field 0) and span (field 2), or the box/inline num_words decision differing once the source is a tracked+moved alloca. NEXT (clear path): dump the IR for V4 vs V4_inline (via a throwaway `compile_to_ir` unit test, as prior #44/#49 fixes did) and diff the `Expr.Blk` lowering — specifically the move-suppression stores into `block`'s alloca and the coerce word extraction — to find the miscomputed word offset. The bug is in the enum-pack + move-suppression of a let-bound struct with a `Vec` field and a 4-word `Option[shared]` field. FIXED 2026-07-10 (root cause + one-spot fix): the corruption is NOT struct-init and NOT the pack box-decision (both inline and let-bound BOX the 8-word Block identically). The IR diff of let-bound vs inline `Expr.Blk` showed the let-bound form emits an EXTRA `__karac_vec_elem_full_drop_Block(%block)` — the moved-out let-bound local's scope-exit COMBINED drop. That drop calls `__karac_drop_struct_Block` (frees the Vec buffer, `cap>0`-guarded) AND runs a SEPARATE, LEN-driven per-element rc-dec walk over `Vec[Stmt]` (rc-dec each `Stmt::Exp(ExprStmt)`'s shared `Expr` — the B-2026-06-14-28 combined-drop for a Vec whose element transitively owns a shared handle). The whole-struct move-suppression `zero_struct_move_caps` (call_dispatch.rs:3558) zeroed only the Vec `CAP` (field 2), which neutralizes the buffer free but NOT the LEN-driven element rc-dec walk — so moving `block` into `Expr.Blk(block)` (whose boxed payload co-owns the same Stmt elements / Expr handles) still rc-dec'd those handles → premature free → the stmt-expr read back as a garbage `Error` node (and heap corruption). FIX: `zero_struct_move_caps` now ALSO zeroes the Vec `LEN` (field 1) for a `Vec`/`VecDeque`/`String` field, so the len-driven walk skips too and the moved-out source's drop is fully neutralized (harmless when the element owns no shared handle — no such walk exists). Explains every observed condition: needs the let-binding (→ a tracked local with a scope-exit drop), the Vec field (→ the len-driven element walk), AND the `Option[shared]` / shared-bearing element (→ the walk does rc-decs); the INLINE form has no local, hence no drop, hence worked. Gates: memory_sanitizer 580 (+1 regression asan_letbound_struct_vec_shared_elem_moved_into_enum_ctor_no_uaf), codegen 2120, fmt + clippy clean; the self-hosted parser now renders `{ a; }` / `{ a; b }` / `{ a; b; }` statement exprs CORRECTLY (interp==compiled). The parser oracle still CRASHES on the fuller corpus — that residual is the SEPARATE B-2026-07-09-12 render UAF (`malloc unaligned tcache`, the entry-copy fresh-vs-retained issue), not this wrong-value bug.

fix: this commit. A generic (monomorphized) fn whose IMPLICIT TAIL expression is a bare `f"…"` double-freed the returned String under codegen (`karac run`/`build`); the interpreter was correct. `compile_mono_function` (src/codegen/mono.rs) lacked the InterpolatedStringLit-tail cap-suppression block that `compile_function` (src/codegen/functions.rs) has: when a function's final expression is a bare f-string, the loaded {data,len,cap} is the return value, but the f-string accumulator's queued `FreeVecBuffer` frees `data` between the return-value load and `ret` (a use-after-free the caller then double-frees against its own binding). `suppress_cleanup_for_tail_return` only covers Identifier-tail moves, and the f-string acc is staged in `last_fstr_acc` only during `compile_expr`, so the mono needed the same post-compile `zero_vec_alloca_cap(last_fstr_acc.take())` guard. ISOLATION: only the generic + implicit-tail + f-string combination breaks — non-generic tail f-string, generic `let s = f".."; s`, generic `return f".."`, generic tail `.to_string()`/Vec-literal all already worked (they route through suppression or a non-tail path); even a no-interpolation tail f-string in a generic fn double-freed (so it is NOT Display- or param-interpolation-specific — it is the mono tail-f-string ownership transfer). Surfaced while verifying the Display trait surface (which is otherwise complete: user `impl Display`, `#[derive(Display)]`, the non-Display f-string typecheck rejection, and generic `T: Display` dispatch all work in both backends) via `fn describe[T: Display](x: T) -> String { f"item is {x}" }`. FIX: add the guarded `last_fstr_acc.take()` → `zero_vec_alloca_cap` block to `compile_mono_function` right after `suppress_cleanup_for_tail_return`, mirroring `compile_function`. Valgrind-confirmed the pre-fix free came from the caller's read-after-free of the returned buffer; ASAN-clean post-fix. Tests: codegen e2e_generic_tail_fstring_return_codegen; memory_sanitizer asan_generic_tail_fstring_no_double_free (Display struct + primitive + String + no-interp tail, looped). Full suites green (codegen 2122, memory_sanitizer 581, interpreter 1139).
title: Generic (monomorphized) fn with an IMPLICIT TAIL bare `f"…"` double-frees the returned String under codegen (interpreter correct). `compile_mono_function` lacked the InterpolatedStringLit-tail cap-suppression that `compile_function` has. NOT Display-specific — surfaced via generic `T: Display` f-string dispatch.

tracker: src/codegen/calls.rs (try_compile_option_result_method extract path) — no move-suppression of a let-bound Option/Result receiver whose payload is extracted
title: `Option`/`Result` `unwrap` / `expect` / `unwrap_err` / `expect_err` on a LET-BOUND receiver with a HEAP payload double-frees. `let r: Result[String, i64] = ...; r.unwrap()` (or `let r: Result[i64, String]; r.unwrap_err()`) extracts the payload by reconstituting the {ptr,len,cap} words — a SHALLOW alias of the let-bound receiver's inline payload buffer — and returns it; the extracted String/Vec is then freed by its consumer AND by the let-bound receiver `r`'s scope-exit drop → `free(): double free`. A FRESH-TEMP receiver (`f().unwrap()`, `m.get(k).unwrap()`) is CLEAN (the temp has no separate scope-exit drop to double it), and a non-heap payload (i64) is clean (nothing to free), which is why the common cases work and this stayed latent. The extract path (`try_compile_option_result_method`, calls.rs) neither deep-copies the extracted heap payload NOR suppresses the let-bound receiver's drop (cap-zero). FIX: on a heap-payload extract from a LET-BOUND (identifier) Option/Result receiver, either deep-copy the payload buffer (mirror `clone_owned_vec_index_element`) OR cap-zero the receiver's payload words so its drop no-ops (the receiver is consumed by unwrap). Pre-existing (predates unwrap_err); surfaced by the B-2026-07-09-10 unwrap_err work.
resolution: FIXED 2026-07-10 (this commit): the extract-or-panic path (`try_compile_option_result_method`, src/codegen/calls.rs) now calls `suppress_inline_option_result_binding_move(object)` after building the extracted value — zeroing a tracked inline/boxed `Option`/`Result` IDENTIFIER receiver's slot so its scope-exit drop no-ops (the receiver is CONSUMED by unwrap/expect/unwrap_err/expect_err and the returned value now solely owns the aliased buffer). No-op for a fresh-temp receiver (not an identifier — already single-owned) and for a non-heap payload (not in the tracked sets), so the already-working cases are untouched. Covers all four extract methods incl. the new unwrap_err/expect_err (B-2026-07-09-10). Gates: memory_sanitizer 582 (+1 regression asan_letbound_result_option_heap_unwrap_no_double_free — let-bound `Result[String,_]`.unwrap(), `Result[_,String]`.unwrap_err(), `Option[String]`.expect(), looped x20 under LSan), codegen 2123, fmt + clippy clean; `let r = f(); r.unwrap()` on heap payloads is now double-free-free.

title: Returning a heap FIELD through a BORROWED receiver (`fn name(ref self) -> String { self.n }`, or a `ref` param) double-frees under codegen; the interpreter is correct. The borrow does not own the field, so codegen returned an ALIAS of the receiver's {ptr,len,cap} buffer, which the caller's drop of the receiver then freed a second time (and any reuse of the receiver dangled). `karac check` accepts the program (copy-out semantics; the interpreter clones). Fires non-generically; unmasked while making generic associated-type field-returns usable.
fix: this commit. `maybe_defensive_copy_param_arg` (src/codegen/runtime.rs) — the return-value hook run on the tail/return expr in `compile_function` — gained a `FieldAccess` arm: when the receiver is a borrow (`SelfValue`/identifier in `self.ref_params`) and the accessed field is a non-shared heap-owning type (`te_owns_heap_below_buffer` && not `shared_heap_type_for_type_expr`), the loaded field value is deep-CLONED via `emit_clone_fn_for_type_expr(field_te)` (alloca src+dst, call, load) so the returned value owns an independent buffer. The receiver's struct name comes from `inferred_receiver_type(object)` (which resolves `self` too), the field TypeExpr from `struct_field_names` + `struct_field_type_exprs`. Gated on `ref_params` membership so an OWNED receiver's field move-out (handled by zeroing the source cap in `suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg_ex`, which requires the receiver to be dropped by this frame) is untouched — the two cases are mutually exclusive. Shared (RC) fields are left to the refcount machinery. ISOLATION: the receiver is used AFTER the call in the tests (`x.name()` then `x.n`), proving a move would be wrong and a clone is required; covers `ref self` / `mut ref self`, String and Vec[i64] fields. Tests: codegen e2e_ref_self_heap_field_return_codegen; memory_sanitizer asan_ref_self_field_return_no_double_free (looped, leak+double-free clean under LSan/ASan). Suites green: codegen 2127, memory_sanitizer 584, interpreter 1141.

title: The `?` operator does not support MULTI-WORD error types in codegen — a `Result[T, E]` where E is (or contains) a String / Vec / multi-field struct. `karac run --interp` handles it; `karac build`/JIT fails LLVM module verification. The common case is the CROSS-error `?` `From` chain into an error struct carrying a String message: `struct AppError { msg: String }` + `impl From[String] for AppError` + `fn run() -> Result[i64, AppError] { let v = may_fail()?; … }` where `may_fail() -> Result[i64, String]`. Same-error `?` and single-word cross-error `?` (e.g. `i64 -> struct{i64}`) both work; only multi-word E is the gap.
fix: this commit. The `?` error path now round-trips MULTI-WORD error payloads. `rebuild_value_from_payload_words` (src/codegen/calls.rs) generic-struct arm advances a word cursor field-by-field, giving each field its own window of words (1 scalar / 2 Slice / 3 String-Vec, recursively for nested structs) via the new `payload_words_for_type` helper — so `AppError { msg: String }` claims all three words for its String field instead of insertvalue'ing a lone i64 into a 3-word slot. `compile_question` (src/codegen/exprs.rs) now (a) extracts ALL inner-error words gated on the INNER value's LLVM width, (b) reconstructs the `From` source arg at the param's true type via `rebuild_value_from_payload_words` (a String param gets its full {ptr,len,cap}), (c) packs the converted target error back into the OUTER Err slot via `coerce_to_payload_words` across all outer payload words, and (d) the `main() -> Result[(), E]` exit path uses the converted value / reconstructs E from all words. Verified value-correct AND valgrind-clean across: same-error String, cross-error String->struct{String}, i64->struct{i64}, same-error Vec[i64], cross-error String->struct{i64,String} (4-word), and `main() -> Result[(),String]` exit. Full --features llvm suite green (codegen 2127, memory_sanitizer 584, par_codegen 155, cli 516).
detail: The `?` error path represents the Err payload as uniform i64 words (tag + w0/w1/w2), and multiple sites assume <= 1 word per struct field: (1) `rebuild_value_from_payload_words` (src/codegen/calls.rs:1503) — the generic-struct arm (line ~1577) inserts exactly ONE word per field, so `AppError { msg: String }` gets `insertvalue {{ptr,i64,i64}} undef, i64 %w0, 0` (an i64 into a 3-word String field) -> verify failure; it must consume as many words as each field needs (recurse into multi-word fields, tracking a word offset). (2) The `?` cross-error `From`-conversion arg marshaling in `compile_question` (src/codegen/exprs.rs ~1487) reconstructs only single-field structs from `w0` and truncates every other shape to `w0`, so `Target.from(e: String)` is called as `Target.from(i64)`. (A local fix using `rebuild_value_from_payload_words(from_param_ty, w0,w1,w2)` fixes THIS site but exposes the next.) (3) The Err-RETURN packing (src/codegen/exprs.rs ~1533/1571) `coerce_to_i64(propagated_payload)` collapses the multi-word target error to one word and inserts a single `propagated_word` into the Result Err slot; it must decompose via `coerce_to_payload_words(propagated_payload, payload_word_count)` and insert every word. (4) The main-result-exit path (~1544) and the enum-layout payload sizing for a multi-word-E Result must agree on the word count. FIX SHAPE: make the four sites use `coerce_to_payload_words` / a word-offset-aware `rebuild_value_from_payload_words` coherently so an N-word error round-trips pack->unpack. Substantial, memory-safety-critical (error-payload representation) — deferred for review rather than landed piecemeal. In-code comments already flag it ('a multi-word E sees only w0 here — the same documented limitation'). WORKAROUND: use a single-word error type (a bare String is NOT single-word; an i64 code or an enum whose payload fits one word is), or run under `--interp`.

title: Memory leak: `match … { Err(e) => println(e.heapfield) }` where `e` is a struct bound out of an enum/Result payload and the heap field is passed DIRECTLY as a call argument (not via an f-string) leaks the field's heap buffer. Narrow: a plain local struct `println(w.msg)` is clean, and the f-string form `println(f"{e.msg}")` is clean — only the enum-payload-bound-struct + direct-field-arg combination leaks.
detail: Repro (valgrind: `3 bytes definitely lost`): `struct AppError { msg: String } fn run(x: i64) -> Result[i64, AppError] { if x > 0i64 { Result.Ok(x+1i64) } else { Result.Err(AppError { msg: "neg".to_string() }) } } fn main() { match run(-1i64) { Ok(v) => println(f"{v}"), Err(e) => println(e.msg) } }`. The struct `e` is materialized from the Result Err payload in the match arm; `println(e.msg)` moves the `msg` String field out as a direct call argument. The move-out consumes the field but the residual struct/field buffer is never dropped on this path. CONTRAST (all clean): plain local `let w = AppError{..}; println(w.msg)` (lk1); f-string `println(f"{e.msg}")` (q1f); multi-field struct via f-string `println(f"{e.code}:{e.msg}")` (q6). So the gap is the intersection: enum/Result-payload-bound struct binding + heap field passed as a DIRECT call arg. Leak only (no double-free / UAF) — LSan/valgrind class, not ASan-UAF. Discovered while validating the B-2026-07-09-20 `?` multi-word fix; independent of `?` (repros with direct `Result.Err(...)` construction, no `?`). FIX SHAPE: the match-arm struct-payload cleanup must drop heap fields not consumed by the arm body, and/or the direct-field-arg move-out from a match-bound struct must schedule a drop of the containing struct's residual. Compare the (clean) f-string path to see how it wires the field read+drop.
resolution: FIXED 2026-07-10. Root cause (asan + IR dump): a `match` scrutinee of an Option/Result whose INLINE struct payload (word count <= the seed enum inline area, 3 for Option / 5 for Result) is bound WHOLE as `e` never freed the struct payload's own heap fields. The dedicated inline Option/Result cleanup (FreeInline{Option,Result}Payload) frees only a bare {ptr,len,cap} payload (a direct String/Vec), NOT a struct payload's inner fields; and suppress_inline_{option,result}_payload_cleanup zeros the source cap because a `Some(e)`/`Err(e)` binding counts as consuming (pattern_consumes_field) -- so on BOTH the fresh-temp scrutinee (no source cleanup at all) and the named-binding scrutinee (source suppressed) the struct's String was owned by NOBODY and leaked. The ledger's original framing (only the direct-field-arg println(e.msg) shape leaks, f-string clean) was WRONG: the f-string form println(f"{e.msg}") leaks the SAME 3 bytes (it frees its own COPY, not e's original buffer) -- the leak is the struct payload binding itself, independent of how the body reads the field. FIX (src/codegen/pattern_binding.rs): the pattern-binding user-struct arm now also track_struct_var's a copy-supported INLINE Option/Result struct payload (new is_inline_optres_struct_payload gate), so its scope-exit StructDrop frees the fields; the same whole-value / field move-out suppression set (zero_struct_move_caps / zero_struct_field_move_cap) that makes the non-Option/Result arm sound covers `Err(e) => take(e)` / `Err(e) => e` without double-free. Gated to inline payloads via a new pattern_binding_scrutinee_optres_area (3/5) threaded from compile_match, so a heap-BOXED wide payload (owned by the box drop) is left untouched. Regression: asan_result_struct_payload_direct_field_arg (memory_sanitizer; 585 green incl. new test, + codegen 2127 / par_codegen 155 / clippy / fmt). if-let/while-let use a separate binding path (they do not set the flag) -- unaffected, not re-checked here.

title: `Vector[T, N]` (a fixed-size SIMD value) was not classified Copy, so a bare rebind `let e1 = ux;` where `ux: Vector[f64, 2]` was treated as a MOVE and every later use of `ux` fired a spurious use-after-move. Using a vector in an arithmetic expression (`ux * v`, `ux - uy`) was fine (a read, not a consume) — only a bare identifier-RHS rebind moved it, so aliasing a SIMD lane-bundle for readability was wrongly rejected.
detail: Repro (ownership check fails; `ownership_ok` expects clean): `fn mix() -> Vector[f64, 2] { let ux = Vector[f64, 2].splat(2.0); let uy = Vector[f64, 2].splat(3.0); let e1 = ux; let e4 = uy; ux + uy + e1 + e4 }` — errors `value 'ux' moved here, used again`. Root cause: `is_copy_type` (src/ownership.rs) enumerated Int/UInt/Float/Bool/Char/Unit + Tuple/Array(of Copy)/Slice(imm)/Pointer/Shared|Rc|Arc/Ref/Function/Named(derive Copy) but had NO `Type::Vector` arm, so a SIMD vector fell through to `_ => false` (non-Copy). A `Vector[T, N]` is a fixed-size, register-resident POD bundle of N primitive lanes — semantically Copy exactly like the fixed `Array` arm directly above it (and its lane type is always a Copy scalar). Fathom's Mandelbrot SIMD kernel never hit this because it only ever reuses vectors via binops / reassignment (`zr * zr`, `zr = ...`), never a bare `let x = zr;`; Slipstream's collide kernel aliases `ux`/`uy` (the lattice e1..e8 directions), the first bare Copy-rebind of a SIMD value.
resolution: FIXED 2026-07-11. Added `Type::Vector { element, .. } => is_copy_type(element, tc)` to `is_copy_type` in src/ownership.rs, mirroring the `Array` arm (element-wise Copy). SIMD lane types are always primitive scalars so this is always Copy, but keying it on the element keeps the rule principled. Unblocks the Slipstream `Vector[f64, 2]` collide kernel (native oracle checksums byte-identical to the scalar kernel: 1582897806 / 793640938 / 680974524). Regression: tests/ownership.rs::test_vector_is_copy (348 ownership tests green; fmt + clippy --all-targets clean).

title: Self-hosted ITEM/TYPE parser crashes at runtime (heap corruption) — DISTINCT from the B-2026-07-09-12 auto-par bug. After B-2026-07-09-12 fixed the auto-parallelization of `mut ref self` calls (which unblocked the EXPRESSION parser oracle, now un-ignored + passing), the sibling `selfhost_parser_items` / `selfhost_parser_types` oracles STILL crash on ~28/102 item inputs (any `enum` with fields, `fn` with params, `trait`, `impl`, generic item, doc-comment/attr item) — e.g. `pub fn id(x: i64) -> i64 { x }`, `enum Shape { Circle(i64), Rect(i64, i64) }`, `impl Calc { fn add(ref self, a: i64, b: i64) -> i64 { a + b } }`. Crucially it is NOT auto-par: it fails IDENTICALLY with `KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0`. gdb shows the crash in `__GI___libc_free` (heap corruption / double-free) reached from `render_item` via inlined drop helpers (`render_item -> ?? -> free`), i.e. a DROP-TIME double-free while rendering/dropping an item AST node. This is a residual of the original nested-drop / shared-enum-payload move-out double-free family (B-2026-07-09-12's ORIGINAL description) on the WIDER item AST — `FnDefNode` (Vec[FnParamNode], Option[TypeExpr] return, Block body), `EnumDefNode` (Vec[VariantNode] each with Vec of field types), `StructDefNode` (Vec[StructFieldNode]), `TraitDefNode`, `ImplBlockNode` — whose Vec-of-nested-struct-with-shared-child drops are not fully covered by the 6 intra-function clone-on-extract increments that fixed the expression-node cases. REPRO (argv-driven, build once): copy selfhost/src, main reads `Env.args()` and calls `render_item(parse_item_str(argv[1]))`, then sweep the item corpus; ~28 crash. NEXT: asan-trace `render_item` on `pub fn id(x: i64) -> i64 { x }` (drop of a FnDefNode) to pin which Vec[nested-struct]/shared field double-frees; extend the clone-on-extract / drop-synthesis coverage to it. Also recall the sibling drop-synthesis inconsistency observed while investigating B-2026-07-09-12: `__karac_drop_struct_<S>` for a struct with an `Option[shared]` field drops only the FIRST heap field (misses the Option[shared] tail) while `__karac_vec_elem_full_drop_<S>` double-drops the Vec elements — reconcile those two Block/item drop fns as part of this. PINNED 2026-07-10 (asan + gdb, argv-driven item oracle): the crash is `AddressSanitizer: SEGV` / `__GI___libc_free(mem=<garbage, e.g. 0x555555665>)` inside `__karac_vec_elem_full_drop_FnDefNode`, reached via the RENDER path `render_item -> render_type -> <inlined drop>`. The faulting instruction is a `cap>0`-guarded String/Vec buffer free (`cmpq $0, 0x10(%rdi); free(*(%rdi))`) whose data pointer is a garbage sub-binary-base value (a SCALAR field misread as a `{ptr,len,cap}` header, or a stale slot after a render-consume). So it is a render-consume-then-drop double-free / field-offset error on the WIDER item nodes — the item-node extension of the expression render double-free family (B-2026-07-10-1 + the 6 clone-on-extract commits), now hitting FnDefNode `{ is_pub:bool, name:String, generics:Option[GenericParamsNode], self_mode:SelfMode, params:Vec[FnParamNode], ret:Option[TypeExpr], body:Block, attributes:Vec[AttrNode], doc:Option[String], span }` and FnParamNode `{ name:String, ty:TypeExpr(shared), span }` where `TypeExpr` is a `shared enum` (so `ret:Option[TypeExpr]` = Option[shared], `params[].ty` = bare shared). CONFIRMED sub-bug found while tracing: `__karac_drop_struct_Block` (FnDefNode.body) drops ONLY field 0 (`stmts`) and misses field 1 (`tail:Option[shared Expr]`) — its combined sibling `__karac_vec_elem_full_drop_Block` patches the tail but DOUBLE-loops the stmts elements; reconcile those. NEXT: extend the intra-function clone-on-extract / drop-symmetry coverage (the B-2026-07-09-12-ORIGINAL family strategy) to the item structs, asan-gated on the full memory_sanitizer suite; a dedicated multi-commit slice. Debug technique: build the item parser with `main` reading `Env.args()` (argv[1]=item source), asan via a temporary `KARAC_EXTRA_CC_ARGS` link hook in driver.rs, `KARAC_OPT_LEVEL=0` for IR-faithful gdb. ROOT CAUSE + FIX DIRECTION 2026-07-10 (precise): the double-free is the DESTRUCTURE of a CALLER-RETAINS struct PARAM that moves a shared/heap child out. `render_fn_param(p: FnParamNode)` does `let FnParamNode { name, ty, span } = p; ... render_type(ty)`. FnParamNode = `{ name:String, ty:TypeExpr, span }` with `TypeExpr` a `shared enum`, so it is NOT copy-supported -> `make_aggregate_param_callee_owned` bails and `p` is a caller-retains SHALLOW ALIAS (registered in `owned_struct_params`, NO callee StructDrop). The destructure aliases the caller's heap; `render_type(ty)` CONSUMES the shared `ty` (rc-dec) AND the caller's own drop of the param dec's it again -> double-free. This is the SAME clone-on-extract issue as B-2026-07-09-12's shared-enum-payload VIEW case, but for a third source class: a caller-retains struct param. ATTEMPTED FIX (reverted — necessary but INSUFFICIENT): added a `retained_param_src` classification in `finish_owned_struct_destructure` (stmts.rs) that routes such a param's destructure through the existing `clone_on_extract_view_field` (same as `view_src`), plus gated the `Option[struct/enum]` leaf branch off it. This is SOUND for the leaf shapes clone_on_extract supports (bare-`shared` rc-inc, String deep-copy, Vec[shared]) but INERT for the item parser: every crashing item node has a field clone_on_extract BAILS on — `params:Vec[FnParamNode]` (Vec of struct-with-shared, not Vec[shared]), `ret:Option[TypeExpr]` (Option[shared]), `body:Block` (nested struct). So the FIRST unsupported field still aliases -> still crashes (28/102). REAL FIX (the deep slice): EXTEND `clone_on_extract_view_field` to (a) `Vec[<struct/enum with shared children>]` (deep-copy buffer + per-element `deep_copy_vec_aggregate_elements_in_place`, which rc-incs shared + deep-copies buffer children), (b) `Option[shared]` (rc-inc Some), (c) nested non-copy-supported struct (recurse), THEN apply the `retained_param_src` routing. Each shape asan-gated on the full memory_sanitizer suite. This is the residual tail the B-2026-07-09-12 view work also deferred; item nodes force all of it. Also reconcile the `__karac_drop_struct_Block` (misses Option[shared] tail) / `__karac_vec_elem_full_drop_Block` (double-loops stmts) sibling inconsistency. RENDER-CONSUME HALF FIXED 2026-07-11 (a49a29f): the dominant crash was NOT the caller-retains destructure this entry earlier emphasized — it was the ENTRY-COPY path. `FnDefNode` / `EnumDefNode` / `StructDefNode` ARE copy-supported (`aggregate_param_copy_supported_struct` admits `Vec[..]` / `Option[shared]` / nested-struct fields), so `make_aggregate_param_callee_owned` deep-copies them on entry when a `render_*(n: FnDefNode)` takes them by value. But the deep-copy duplicated the buffers WITHOUT rc-INCing the `shared` handles buried inside a `Vec[struct]` element (`params[].ty: TypeExpr`, a shared enum) or a nested struct (`body: Block`), while the combined struct-drop rc-DECs them per element (`vec_elem_agg_drop_for_type_expr`) — caller's retained original and callee's copy both dec → double-free. Enum variant lists hit the same via `Vec[TypeExpr]` (a `Vec[shared]` element list). FIX: a scoped `deep_copy_rc_inc_bare_shared` flag, set only around `make_aggregate_param_callee_owned`'s deep-copy, making `deep_copy_one_aggregate_field` rc-INC a directly-nested bare `shared` field and `deep_copy_vec_aggregate_elements_in_place` rc-INC each `Vec[shared]` element — symmetric with the drop. (An earlier global attempt leaked because the drop side had not yet been reconciled; the #35 / double-loop-drain work since fixed it, so the entry-copy inc is now balanced. Confirmed: the retained_param_src routing / clone-on-extract-nested-struct I also prototyped were NOT load-bearing here — entry-copy rc-inc alone gives the same result — so they were dropped for a minimal 2-file patch.) Result: 34 -> 12 crashing item inputs; the whole fn/impl/trait/enum/struct/generic render-consume class is fixed. Gates: memory_sanitizer 587, codegen 2127, clippy + fmt clean. RESIDUAL 12 (all ATTRIBUTE items with a Some Option field — `#[key = "val"]`, `#[b(1)]`, `#[derive(Clone)]`; `#[a]`/`#[a] struct P` with no arg are CLEAN): a DISTINCT PARSE-SIDE premature free, NOT the entry-copy class. valgrind (real item oracle): the attr's String is `karac_string_clone`'d during `parse_item_str`, FREED during `parse_item_str` (a drop, ~0x13CFC7), then READ (memmove) during `render_item` — freed too early during parse, before the AST it belongs to is rendered. Minimal repro that reproduces (`KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0`, 37-byte payload, x20 loop): `enum Token{StringLiteral(String),Ident(String),Other} struct SpannedToken{token:Token,off:i64} struct AttrNode{path:Vec[String],string_value:Option[String],off:i64} struct FnItem{attrs:Vec[AttrNode],name:String,off:i64} struct P{tokens:Vec[SpannedToken],pos:i64}` with `impl P { fn parse_attr(mut ref self)->AttrNode{ let mut sv:Option[String]=None; match self.tokens[self.pos].token{ StringLiteral(s)=>{sv=Some(s);} ..}; AttrNode{path:..,string_value:sv,off:0} } fn parse_item(mut ref self)->FnItem{ let mut attrs=Vec.new(); attrs.push(self.parse_attr()); FnItem{attrs,name:"f".to_string(),off:0} } }` then `render_item(item:FnItem)` destructures `FnItem`, `for a in attrs { let AttrNode{..,string_value,..}=a; match string_value { Some(s)=>out.push_str(s), None=>{} } }`. NARROWING (partial, inconsistent under aggressive minimization): the trigger needs the match-EXTRACT of `s` from the BORROWED self-field-indexed token (`self.tokens[i].token`, which clones), the assign to an OUTER `Option` var inside the arm (`string_value = Some(s)`), the move of that var into a struct literal, AND the full `AttrNode{path:Vec + string_value:Option}` -> `Vec[AttrNode]` -> `FnItem` nesting + a render-style destructure-consume; dropping AttrNode.path, or the Vec/FnItem nesting, or the render consume each made it stop reproducing in isolation. HYPOTHESIS: the match-arm binding `s` (a clone from a borrowed match) moved into an outer var via ASSIGNMENT has its arm-scope cleanup un-suppressed, so it frees during parse while the built AST still owns it. `#[b(1)]` (value:Option[Expr] via `value = Some(parse_expr())`) hits the same via the parse-result-assign variant. NEXT: fix the match-arm-assign-to-outer-var move-out suppression; verify against the 12 attr inputs + full memory_sanitizer. The 3 parser oracles stay #[ignore]'d against this id; the expression oracle stays green; main is known-good. RESIDUAL SHARPENED 2026-07-11 (session 2, minimal repro found — NO self/match/Token, the earlier match-arm-assign hypothesis was WRONG): the residual attr double-free is the RENDER-CONSUME of a `Vec[<struct owning Option[String]>]` field destructured out of a CALLEE-OWNED (entry-copied) struct. Minimal repro (`KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0`, 37-byte payload, x20): `struct AttrNode { string_value: Option[String] } struct FnItem { attrs: Vec[AttrNode] } fn parse_item()->FnItem{ let mut attrs=Vec.new(); attrs.push(AttrNode{string_value:Some("...37 bytes...".to_string())}); FnItem{attrs} } fn render_attrs(attrs:Vec[AttrNode])->String{ let mut out="".to_string(); for a in attrs{ let AttrNode{string_value}=a; match string_value{Some(s)=>out.push_str(s),None=>{}} } out } fn render_item(item:FnItem)->String{ let FnItem{attrs}=item; render_attrs(attrs) }` called as `render_item(parse_item())` — double-frees the 37-byte String. BISECTION (each rebuild clean): removing the `FnItem` wrapper and passing `Vec[AttrNode]` DIRECTLY to render_attrs is CLEAN (T3); building+dropping the FnItem with no consume is CLEAN (T6); destructuring `let FnItem{attrs}=item` + reading `attrs.len()` (no element consume) is CLEAN (T7). It crashes ONLY when the `attrs` destructured out of the entry-copied `FnItem` is then CONSUMED (for-loop that moves each element out + destructures its `Option[String]`), whether inline (T2) or via a separate fn (T8). RULED OUT: the entry-copy deep-copy is CORRECT — a `[deepcopy-option]` debug confirms `deep_copy_option_inline_payload_in_place` FIRES for the `Vec[AttrNode]` element's `Option[String]` during the `FnItem` entry-copy, so `item`'s copy owns an INDEPENDENT String buffer from the temp's. The struct-drop's Vec element drain is `cap>0`-guarded, so `zero_struct_field_move_cap` cap-zeroing the transferred `attrs` in `item` correctly disarms item's drop. So the double-free is in the DESTRUCTURE-TRANSFER + downstream CONSUME interaction: `attrs` bound out of a callee-owned struct via `finish_owned_struct_destructure` (Vec transferable arm: `track_owned_destructure_field_cleanup` + `zero_struct_field_move_cap`) and then MOVED into a consumer (render_attrs) or for-loop-consumed — the transferred-Vec binding's cleanup is likely NOT retracted by that downstream move/consume (a fresh moved Vec param IS, hence T3 clean), so both the destructure-leaf cleanup and the consumer free the same element `Option[String]`. NEXT: compare the move-out suppression of a `track_owned_destructure_field_cleanup`-tracked Vec binding vs a fresh owned Vec local when passed to a by-value fn / consumed by a `for` loop; the transferred binding needs the same cap-zero / cleanup-retract on downstream move. (The binary is stripped even at KARAC_OPT_LEVEL=0, so gdb/addr2line can't name the two free sites — reason from the repro.) This is `Vec[struct-with-Option[String]]`-specific; the entry-copy rc-inc fix (a49a29f) that closed 22 inputs was the shared-handle sibling of this same destructure-transfer-completeness family. DEFINITIVE MINIMAL REPRO 2026-07-11 (12 lines, even the FnItem/destructure was a red herring): a NAMED `Vec[<struct owning Option[String]>]` local MOVED into a by-value consumer double-frees an element's `Option[String]`. `struct AttrNode{string_value:Option[String]} fn parse_attrs()->Vec[AttrNode]{ let mut a=Vec.new(); a.push(AttrNode{string_value:Some("...37b...".to_string())}); a } fn render_attrs(attrs:Vec[AttrNode])->String{ let mut o="".to_string(); for a in attrs{ let AttrNode{string_value}=a; match string_value{Some(s)=>o.push_str(s),None=>{}} } o } fn main() with panics{ let mut i=0; while i<20{ let v=parse_attrs(); println(render_attrs(v)); i=i+1 } }` — CRASHES (T11). CRITICAL discriminators: passing the SAME value as a TEMPORARY (`render_attrs(parse_attrs())`, no named binding) is CLEAN (T3); a `Vec[String]` (no per-element Option) is CLEAN (T12). So the bug is the caller-side MOVE-OUT SUPPRESSION for a named `Vec[struct-with-Option[String]-element]` binding passed by value: `render_attrs(v)` fails to retract / cap-zero `v`'s scope-exit `FreeVecBuffer` cleanup, so BOTH v's cleanup (in the caller, its cap-guarded per-element agg-drop draining each element's `Option[String]`) AND render_attrs's own consume free the same buffers. The FreeVecBuffer drain IS cap-guarded (runtime.rs ~4911) so a correct cap-zero WOULD suppress it — the gap is that `suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg` (call_dispatch.rs) does not fire / zero the source for this element class. LIKELY ROOT: an all-`Option` element struct like `AttrNode` has `type_expr_has_drop_heap == FALSE` (the known ArgN caveat), so `v` is either not registered with an element-draining `FreeVecBuffer` in the way the suppressor recognizes, or the suppressor's element-heap gate skips it — while the drain path (via `vec_elem_agg_drop_for_type_expr` / the struct's own drop) DOES reach the `Option[String]`. FIX AREA: make `suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg` (+ its `_ex`) cap-zero a moved `Vec[struct]` whose element only owns `Option`/inline heap — align the move-out suppressor's element-heap classification with the drain's (`vec_elem_agg_drop_for_type_expr` presence), i.e. treat `Option[String]`/`Option[inline]`-only element structs as heap-bearing for suppression. Verify against T11 + the 12 attr item inputs + full memory_sanitizer. This is the caller-side move-out sibling of the entry-copy completeness family a49a29f fixed. ROOT CAUSE PINPOINTED 2026-07-11: `type_expr_has_drop_heap` (src/codegen/synth_drop.rs:1721) hardcodes `"Option" | "Result" => false` (line 1737), so a struct owning ONLY an `Option[String]` / `Option[inline-heap]` / `Option[shared]` field classifies as HEAP-FREE — even though the struct's drop DOES free that Option payload (the `OptionInline` / rc-dec arms). Discriminating repros (all named-binding `let v = mk(); consume(v)`, x20, valgrind): `Vec[String]` CLEAN (TA), `Vec[struct{name:String}]` CLEAN (TB) — both heap-classified and correctly neutralized — but `Vec[struct{string_value:Option[String]}]` DOUBLE-FREES (T11). So the caller-side move-out of the named `v` into the by-value consumer relies on the element's `type_expr_has_drop_heap` to decide whether to neutralize v's scope-exit cleanup; the false-negative for the Option-only element skips it, so BOTH v's cleanup (draining each element's Option[String] via the struct drop) and the consumer free the same buffer. NOTE while narrowing: `suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg` (call_dispatch.rs:3327) is NOT invoked for a plain user-fn-call owned-Vec arg (debug: no `var=v`) — TA/TB are neutralized by a DIFFERENT mechanism in the user-call arg path; find that path and align its element-heap gate (or fix `type_expr_has_drop_heap` for Option, carefully — it is load-bearing across drop synthesis, so prefer a local fix at the move-out neutralization site or a dedicated `element_needs_move_neutralize` predicate that counts Option[heap]). FIX + asan-gate against T11 + the 12 attr item inputs + full memory_sanitizer. This is the LAST identified blocker for the item/type oracles (with a49a29f, the only remaining crashers are the 12 attr items, all this shape). FIX AREA LOCALIZED 2026-07-11 (final narrowing, no fn boundary needed): the double-free is the FOR-LOOP consuming a NAMED `Vec[<struct owning Option[String]>]` binding — `let v = parse_attrs(); for a in v { let AttrNode{string_value}=a; match string_value{Some(s)=>out.push_str(s),None=>{}} }` CRASHES (TC), but the same over a `Vec[String]` or `Vec[struct{String}]` is CLEAN (TA/TB), and passing v to a fn WITHOUT consuming is CLEAN (TD). Mechanism: `for a in v` (compile_for_vec_var, control_flow_for.rs:1057) MOVES each element out and the arm consumes its `Option[String]`, but the source `v`'s scope-exit `FreeVecBuffer` still DRAINS every element (its cap-guarded per-element agg-drop frees each `Option[String]`) → the consumed payloads are freed twice. The loop neutralizes the source drain for a heap-CLASSIFIED element (`Vec[String]`, `Vec[struct{String}]`) but NOT for a struct whose only heap is `Option[String]`, because that struct mis-classifies as heap-free (`type_expr_has_drop_heap` returns false for `Option`, synth_drop.rs:1737) — though a direct patch of that predicate alone did NOT fix it (built + tested), so the loop's neutralization uses a DIFFERENT/narrower element-heap gate that must ALSO be widened to count `Option[heap]` elements. FIX: in `compile_for_vec_var` (owned-binding path) neutralize the source Vec's per-element drain for a `Vec[struct-with-Option[heap]]` element (cap-zero after the loop consumes all, or mark elements consumed), aligning the gate with what the FreeVecBuffer drain / struct drop actually frees. asan-gate against TC/T11 + the 12 attr item inputs + full memory_sanitizer. With a49a29f (entry-copy rc-inc, 34->12), this is the SOLE remaining item/type-oracle blocker — all 12 residual crashers are attr items whose `AttrNode` carries `Option[String]` (string_value) / `Option[Expr]` (arg value). GENERAL MECHANISM CONFIRMED 2026-07-11 (final): the residual is the FOR-LOOP-ELEMENT VIEW MOVE-OUT, a general member of the B-2026-07-09-12 view-move-out family (not Option- or attr-specific). `compile_for_vec_var` (control_flow_for.rs:1057) binds the loop var `a` to a SHALLOW COPY of the element (load elem_ptr -> bind_pattern), so `a` VIEWS the element's heap in v's buffer while v's scope-exit FreeVecBuffer drains every element; moving ANY heap child OUT of `a` frees it and v's drain frees it again -> double-free. Discriminators (`let v=mk(); for a in v{...}`): E1 `match a.string_value{Some(s)=>push_str(s)}` CRASH; E4 `let AttrNode{name}=a; collected.push(name)` (String child MOVED out) CRASH; E2 `push_str(name)` (child BORROWED) CLEAN; E5 child bound but NOT consumed CLEAN. Trigger = CONSUMING (moving out) a heap child of the element view. The existing for-loop-element suppression only covers moving the WHOLE element into a consumer (defensive COPY at push/insert), NOT a child extracted from it. FIX (each substantial, asan-gate the large for-loop suite): (A) make `for a in v` over an OWNED Vec CONSUME v — per-iteration owned cleanup for `a` + suppress v's per-element drain (buffer-only); conflicts with the defensive-copy-at-consume model, which must be disabled for owned-consume loops. (B) register the element `a` as a VIEW source and wire clone-on-extract for every child-consume shape (struct-field destructure, Option/Result-field match, field-into-push). Recommend (A). Same multi-session view-move-out family as B-2026-07-09-12 — scope as its own slice.
investigation: INVESTIGATION 2026-07-10 (deep-traced with argv-driven item oracle + valgrind + single-file minimal repros; NO fix landed — changes reverted, main stays clean). The crash is a THREE-LAYER drop/ownership problem; the isolated first two layers are fixable and were fixed in a reverted branch, but the third (a pre-existing struct-drop synthesis bug) is the blocker and needs its own slice. TRIGGER (precise): a non-empty Vec[struct-with-shared] (fn/trait/impl params = Vec[FnParamNode]) OR Vec[shared] (enum tuple-variant tuple_types = Vec[TypeExpr]) field, consumed during render. Struct-def fields (Vec[StructFieldNode]) render CLEAN in the real parser NOT because StructFieldNode differs from FnParamNode (they are near-identical) but because StructDefNode has that ONE heap Vec field whereas FnDefNode has MULTIPLE heap fields (params: Vec[FnParamNode] + ret: Option[TypeExpr] + body: Block) — and it is the MULTI-heap-field struct DROP that miscompiles (layer 3). LAYER 1 (fixed in isolation): render_fn_param(p: FnParamNode) does `let FnParamNode { name, ty, span } = p; render_type(ty)`. When `p` is a CALLER-RETAINS struct param (owned_struct_params) the moved-out bare-shared `ty` (rc-dec'd by render_type) double-frees against the caller's own drop. FIX: a `retained_param_src` classification in finish_owned_struct_destructure (stmts.rs) routing such a param's destructure through clone_on_extract_view_field (same as the shared-enum-payload VIEW case) — its bare-shared rc-inc / String deep-copy / Option[shared] arms suffice. Verified clean (minimal repros mp, mf, min). LAYER 2 (fixed in isolation): FnDefNode/FnParamNode are actually COPY-SUPPORTED (field_copy_supported, param_own.rs ~293, returns true for `Vec`/`String` UNCONDITIONALLY without checking the element carries shared), so they are ENTRY-COPIED (make_aggregate_param_callee_owned), NOT caller-retains. The entry-copy of a Vec[struct-with-shared] field (deep_copy_one_aggregate_field Vec arm -> deep_copy_vec_aggregate_elements_in_place -> per-element deep_copy_struct_heap_fields) copies each element's String but does NOT rc-inc its bare-shared GRANDCHILD (deep_copy_one_aggregate_field has no bare-shared arm — deliberately, since a DIRECT bare-shared param field must not be rc-inc'd there: the reverted moved-arg leak, B-2026-07-09-12). So the callee's independent copy aliases the caller's ty box and both drops free it. FIX: a `clone_on_extract_deep_copy_shared` flag enabling a bare-shared rc-inc arm in deep_copy_one_aggregate_field, set ONLY while DESCENDING INTO Vec[agg] elements (a shared grandchild buried in a Vec has no caller inc/dec path — only the element's __karac_vec_elem_full_drop_<S> rc-decs it — so the copy MUST rc-inc it; the top-level direct-field arm stays off). Verified clean in isolation (mfd_p = FnDefNode{name,params}; a = params unused; b = params consumed via count()). LAYER 3 (THE BLOCKER, NOT fixed): a struct with BOTH a Vec[agg] field AND an Option[shared] field mis-drops. Isolator `comb.kara`: struct FnDefNode { name: String, params: Vec[FnParamNode], ret: Option[TypeExpr], span } consumed with ret UNUSED still throws 11 valgrind invalid-frees, while {name,params} alone (mfd_p) and {name,ret} alone (mfd_r) are BOTH clean. So __karac_drop_struct_FnDefNode (or its interaction with the entry-copy of two heap fields) double-frees when a Vec[agg] and an Option[shared] field coexist — the same sibling drop-synthesis inconsistency already noted in this entry (drop_struct drops only the first heap field / vec_elem_full_drop double-loops). NEXT SESSION: fix emit_struct_drop_synthesis for a multi-heap-field struct FIRST (make {Vec[agg], Option[shared]} coexist drop each field exactly once), THEN re-land layers 1+2. Minimal isolator to gate on: comb.kara above (build single-file, valgrind, expect 0 errors). DEBUG LESSON: an E0401 (a fn-local enum variant `BareShared(StructType<'ctx>)` cannot carry the outer impl lifetime) silently FAILED two `cargo build` runs, so a stale binary was tested for several iterations — always confirm `Finished` (not just exit 0 of a backgrounded build) and, when in doubt, `strings target/release/karac | grep <new-symbol>` before trusting a behavioral result.

source: kata:76-minimum-window-substring (byte-indexed count table `need[c]`/`have[c]` where `c: u8` from `String.bytes()`)
title: Indexing a Vec[T]/Slice[T]/Array[T,N] (read OR write) with a NARROWER-than-i64 integer index — e.g. a `u8` from `String.bytes()` used directly as a count-table index, the natural sliding-window / counting-sort idiom — emitted an INVALID bounds-check ICmp: codegen built `icmp uge i8 %idx, i64 %len` WITHOUT widening the index to the length's i64 type, so LLVM module verification failed ('Both operands to ICmp instruction are not of the same type!') and `karac build`/JIT aborted. The TREE-WALK INTERPRETER handled the same code (implicit widen), so it was a run==build DIVERGENCE (interp passed, codegen failed). Repro: `fn main(){ let b="A".bytes(); let c=b[0i64]; let mut v:Vec[i64]=Vec.new(); let mut i=0i64; while i<128i64 {v.push(i); i=i+1i64;} println(f"{v[c]}"); }` — interp printed 65, `karac build` failed verification. ROOT CAUSE: the ~12 Vec/Slice/Array/SoA index-lowering sites in src/codegen/calls.rs and src/codegen/collections.rs (plus tensor.rs) took the index at its own int width (`compile_expr(index)?.into_int_value()`) and compared it against the i64 length directly. The Column index path already went through `coerce_to_i64` (so column indexing with a u8 worked) — the Vec/Slice/Array paths did not. FIX: route every index through the existing `coerce_to_i64` helper (zext when narrower, trunc when wider) before the bounds `icmp` and the GEP, at all sites (calls.rs 3, collections.rs 9, tensor.rs 1). Regression test locks a u8-indexed Vec[i64] read AND write. Found by dogfooding kata-katas #76 Minimum Window Substring.

kāra is the SLOWEST of five compiled mirrors on kata #76 minimum-window-substring (M5 AND x86 container) — a two-pointer sliding-window bounds-check-elision gap. M5 seq lane (--warmup 5 --runs 30, all 99.7% CPU, single-threaded): go 265.3 < c 282.7 < rust -O 285.7 < rust_ovf (rustc -O -C overflow-checks=on) 312.3 < KARA 351.0 ms. So kāra 1.24x behind the C floor, 1.23x behind rust -O, and 1.12x behind EQUAL-SAFETY rust_ovf — both overflow-checked, so that 1.12x is a pure NON-overflow codegen gap. Container was worse (kara 789.6 vs c 510.6 = 1.55x C); the M5 narrowed it but did NOT reorder — unlike #73/#74 (which went kāra-last->1st/2nd on M5), kāra stays LAST here. EVIDENCE (warm, /usr/bin/time -l): instructions retired kāra 12.457B vs C 7.576B (1.64x) vs rust_ovf 11.141B (1.12x); cycles kāra 1.557B vs C 1.293B (1.20x, matches the wall ratio); IPC kāra 8.00 — the HIGHEST of the three (C 5.86, rust_ovf 7.79). So the gap is instruction COUNT, not pipelining: many CHEAP instructions retired fast, i.e. surviving bounds-checks, not slow ones. Binary 33.4 KiB (C parity), runtime RSS 1.4 MiB (C parity), compile 89 ms (~rustc) — the whole gap is the runtime hot loop. HOT LOOP (bench/minimum_window_substring.kara fn min_window): `while r < n { let cr = s[r]; have[cr]+=1; ...; while formed==required { let cl = s[l]; have[cl]-=1; ...; l = l + 1 } r = r + 1 }` with n = s.len() and s an unmutated `ref Vec[i64]`. DIAGNOSIS (leading hypothesis, and it is exactly a class this investigation already documents): the surviving check is `s[l]` — the window-left pointer `l` is incremented ONLY inside the conditional `while formed==required` shrink loop, so it is a CONDITIONALLY-UPDATED MONOTONE variable, precisely the class bce_monotonic_assume.md § Diagnostic pins as unfoldable (kata #26 `nums[k]`, kata #88 `nums1[i]`): its phi is not an AddRec and is self-referential, so SCEV gives up and LVI widens to overdefined, and LLVM cannot derive the RELATIONAL invariant l <= r < n on any pass ordering. This is the sliding-window sibling of the midpoint idiom (B-2026-06-16-1) — same relational-assume shape, different derivation. `s[r]` (r unconditionally ++'d) MAY also survive if the `let n = s.len()` capture blocks the AddRec==len proof. The count tables `have[cr]`/`need[cr]`/`have[cl]`/`need[cl]` are VALUE-indexed (cr=s[r] in the 4-symbol alphabet {0,1,2,3} but typed unconstrained i64), so they are bounds-checked in BOTH kāra and Rust and are NOT the differentiator — though a value-range narrowing (if karac tracked the alphabet bound) is a secondary opportunity. FIX DIRECTION: extend src/codegen/control_flow_bce.rs's relational-assume machinery (the binsearch_guard_pair / try_emit_binsearch_midpoint_assumes path that landed the midpoint pair) to the two-pointer shrink idiom — for a var `l` conditionally incremented under a guard inside a loop dominated by `l <= r` and `r < n`, emit `assume(l < n)` (and/or `assume(l <= r)`) at the `s[l]` use, gated on emission + soundness-checked identically (AOT overflow trapping makes the no-wrap precondition hold on all defined executions). CONFIRMATION NEEDED (first step of the fix slice, NOT yet done): otool/opt-dump min_window's post-inline IR (methodology in bce_monotonic_assume.md § Diagnostic), enumerate which of s[r]/s[l]/have[]/need[] icmp+cold-panic checks survive default<O2>, and verify injected assumes fold them (Experiment A/B style). ORACLE / repro: the kata itself — sink 487341820, kara-katas/leetcode/1-100/76-minimum-window-substring/bench/results.json (M5), dual-run reproducible (kāra 351.0 +/- 7.7 ms, tight). Not a correctness bug; perf codegen-gap. Relatives: B-2026-06-16-1 (midpoint idiom, landed), and the #26/#88 conditionally-monotone diagnostic rows in docs/investigations/bce_monotonic_assume.md.
